Suction cups have *high* motion artifact. They are preferred for *rapid repositioning*, not for artifact rejection. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Surface electrode = ionic-to-electronic transducer** at the skin. - [x] **Four types:** Metal Plate, Suction Cup, Adhesive (Tape/Pre-gelled), Multipoint Array. - [x] **Half-cell potential** is governed by the **Nernst equation**: $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$. - [x] **Ag/AgCl** is the gold standard: $E_{\text{hc}} \approx +0.223$ V vs. SHE, low polarization, low motion artifact. - [x] **Equivalent circuit:** $E_{\text{hc}} \rightarrow R_s \rightarrow (R_d \parallel C_d) \rightarrow (R_{se} \parallel C_{se}) \rightarrow R_{\text{tissue}}$. - [x] **Skin impedance is high at low frequency** — hence EEG needs high-input-impedance amplifiers. - [x] **Adhesive tape electrodes** dominate Holter/ICU because of low artifact and hygiene. - [x] **Suction cup electrodes** dominate stress-test ECG because of fast repositioning. - [x] **Multipoint electrodes** provide **spatial averaging**, improving SNR by $\sqrt{N}$. - [x] **Motion artifact** $V_{\text{artifact}} \propto I_{\text{DC}} \cdot \Delta R_s$; minimized by Ag/AgCl + good skin prep. - [x] **Thermal noise** $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ sets the noise floor of any electrode. - [x] **Stratum corneum** is the outermost dead skin layer and the **dominant contributor** to electrode-skin impedance at low frequencies. - [x] **Standard electrode diameters:** 30–50 mm (ECG); < 10 mm (EMG); < 5 mm (multipoint). - [x] **Standard gel** is KCl-saturated hydrogel with resistivity ≈ 1 Ω·m. - [x] **Always convert** body temperature to **310 K** in Nernst-equation derivations. - [x] **CMRR of amplifier** depends on **electrode impedance matching** — mismatched electrodes degrade CMRR.

topics.title: Bio-potentials: ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bio-potentials: ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG):** A graphical, time-domain recording of the bio-electric potentials generated by the depolarization and repolarization cycles of cardiac muscle cells, captured non-invasively from the surface of the body using surface electrodes and a high-gain differential bio-amplifier.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PBEBT304, Module 2 — Bio-potentials), the ECG is treated as the **canonical, gold-standard bio-potential signal** used to study the general principles of bio-potential acquisition, electrode–skin interface, lead theory, and biopotential amplifier design.

### 1.2 Bio-electric Origin of the ECG Signal

The heart is a **bio-electric dipole**. Each myocardial cell, when excited, generates a transmembrane action potential (≈ −90 mV resting, +20 mV peak). The vector sum of millions of such cellular dipoles, at any instant of time, produces a single **cardiac equivalent dipole** with a magnitude (typically **1 mV** to **5 mV** at body surface) and a spatial direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ECG is measurable on the skin?** The body acts as a **volume conductor** (a 3-D resistive medium filled with ionic saline, ρ ≈ 0.5 Ω·m). Bio-electric dipoles inside the thorax establish equipotential surfaces that extend to the skin, allowing surface electrodes to detect the field.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse Model

Imagine the heart's electrical dipole as a **lighthouse** rotating on a hill (the thorax).
- The **bulb** = the cardiac dipole vector.
- The **lighthouse beam** = the instantaneous electrical axis projected onto the body surface.
- The **ECG paper** = a strip of paper laid on the beach, recording the intensity of the beam as it sweeps.
- Each **wave (P, QRS, T)** = the lighthouse flashing during a specific phase of the cardiac cycle.

This is exactly why ECG is fundamentally a **vectorcardiographic projection**: we only see the *shadow* of the true 3-D dipole on a chosen lead axis.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Surface ECG amplitude | **0.5 mV – 5 mV** | Typical peak-to-peak |
| ECG bandwidth | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | Diagnostic standard |
| Dominant frequency | **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz** | QRS spectrum |
| Heart rate (adult) | **60 – 100 bpm** | Brady/Tachycardia thresholds |
| Skin–electrode impedance | < **5 kΩ** | After gel preparation |
| Standard paper speed | **25 mm/s** | 1 mm = 0.04 s |
| Standard calibration | **10 mm = 1 mV** | Vertical gain |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an ECG Complex (One Cardiac Cycle)

A single normal sinus-rhythm ECG complex consists of the following fiducial components, generated by sequential depolarization/repolarization of the atria and ventricles.

1. **P Wave (Atrial Depolarization)**
   - Originates from the SA node impulse spreading through both atria.
   - Duration: **80 ms – 100 ms**.
   - Amplitude: **≤ 2.5 mm** (≤ 0.25 mV).
   - Always **upright in Lead II** in a normal heart.

2. **PR Interval (Atrial Depolarization + AV Nodal Delay)**
   - From start of P to start of Q.
   - Normal: **120 ms – 200 ms**.
   - Represents the physiological delay at the AV node (≈ 90 ms) which allows ventricular filling.

3. **QRS Complex (Ventricular Depolarization)**
   - Largest deflection. Duration: **80 ms – 120 ms** (the famous "**< 0.12 s**" rule).
   - Q: small initial negative deflection.
   - R: tall positive peak.
   - S: negative deflection following R.
   - Amplitude: **R-peak can exceed 1.5 mV**.

4. **ST Segment**
   - From end of S to start of T.
   - Should be **isoelectric** (flat, at zero baseline).
   - Elevation or depression indicates ischemia / infarction.

5. **T Wave (Ventricular Repolarization)**
   - Asymmetric, broad, low-amplitude wave.
   - Amplitude: **≤ 5 mm in precordial leads**.
   - Polarity: same as QRS in most leads.

6. **QT Interval**
   - From start of Q to end of T.
   - Total ventricular depolarization + repolarization time.
   - **Heart-rate corrected QT (QTc) = QT / √RR** (Bazett's formula).

7. **U Wave (Sometimes Visible)**
   - Low-frequency deflection after T.
   - Believed to represent repolarization of Purkinje fibers or papillary muscles.

### 2.2 Lead Theory — The Mathematics of Cardiac Vectors

A **lead** is not a wire; it is a **mathematical view of the cardiac dipole vector along a chosen axis**. The voltage recorded by a lead is:

$$V_{lead} = \vec{M} \cdot \vec{a}_{lead} = \vert \vec{M} \vert \cos\theta$$

where $\vec{M}$ is the instantaneous cardiac dipole moment vector and $\vec{a}_{lead}$ is the unit vector of the lead axis. Therefore, the lead is a **cosine-projector** of the dipole.

### 2.3 Einthoven's Triangle and the Standard Limb Leads

Willem Einthoven (1903) proposed that the three limb electrodes — **Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), Left Leg (LL)** — form an equilateral triangle on the body, with the heart at its electrical center.

| Lead | Electrode Pair (positive reference) | View |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lead I** | LA − RA | Lateral wall (left) |
| **Lead II** | LL − RA | Inferior wall |
| **Lead III** | LL − LA | Inferior wall (alternate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Einthoven's Law (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law for the body):**
> $$V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}$$
> This is a hard physiological constraint. If the measured voltages violate this, the instrument is faulty or the lead placement is wrong.

### 2.4 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger)

By removing the reference electrode from the negative input and disconnecting it through high-value resistors, the voltage is **augmented (boosted) by 1.5×**:

$$V_{aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

$$V_{aVL} = V_{LA} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

$$V_{aVF} = V_{LL} - \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA}}{2}$$

### 2.5 The 12-Lead ECG System (Clinical Standard)

A "12-lead ECG" is actually **10 electrodes** producing **12 voltage views**:

- **3 Bipolar limb leads:** I, II, III (Einthoven)
- **3 Augmented limb leads:** aVR, aVL, aVF (Goldberger)
- **6 Precordial (chest) unipolar leads:** V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 (Wilson)

Precordial positions (Wilson central terminal = average of the three limb electrodes):
- V1: 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
- V2: 4th ICS, left sternal border
- V3: midway between V2 and V4
- V4: 5th ICS, midclavicular line
- V5: anterior axillary line (same level as V4)
- V6: midaxillary line (same level as V4)

### 2.6 The Frontal Hexaxial Reference System

The 6 limb leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF) are arranged on a circle separated by **30°** increments, with Lead I aligned to 0° and proceeding counter-clockwise:

| Lead | Axis (°) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Lead I | 0° |
| aVR | −150° |
| Lead II | +60° |
| aVL | −30° |
| Lead III | +120° |
| aVF | +90° |

This 360° map is used to determine the **mean electrical axis (MEA)** of the heart.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}$ | Einthoven's Law — diagnostic integrity check |
| 2 | $\text{HR} = \frac{60}{\text{RR interval in s}} = \frac{1500}{\text{RR in mm}}$ | Heart rate (bpm) at 25 mm/s paper |
| 3 | $QT_c = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | Bazett's heart-rate corrected QT |
| 4 | $\vec{V}_{lead} = \vec{M} \cdot \vec{a}_{lead}$ | Lead as vector dot product |
| 5 | $V_{aVR} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$ | Augmented lead derivation |
| 6 | $V_{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common Mode Rejection Ratio (dB) |
| 7 | $Z_{skin-elec} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_d} \Vert R_s$ | Skin–electrode interface impedance |
| 8 | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Filter cutoff for ECG pre-amp |
| 9 | $\text{Gain} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$ | Standard non-inverting bio-amp gain |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{PR} = t_{QRS\,start} - t_{P\,start}$ | PR interval definition |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Einthoven's Law from Vector Geometry

**Given:** The cardiac dipole $\vec{M}$ is located at the centroid of an equilateral triangle formed by RA, LA, and LL.

**Step 1 — Define the lead voltages as projections.**
The bipolar lead I is the projection along the LA–RA axis:

$$V_{I} = \vec{M} \cdot \hat{e}_{I} = \vert \vec{M} \vert \cos\theta_I$$

**Step 2 — Similarly for leads II and III.**

$$V_{II} = \vec{M} \cdot \hat{e}_{II} = \vert \vec{M} \vert \cos\theta_{II}$$

$$V_{III} = \vec{M} \cdot \hat{e}_{III} = \vert \vec{M} \vert \cos\theta_{III}$$

**Step 3 — Use the angle relationships inside the equilateral triangle.**

The unit vectors satisfy the linear relationship:

$$\hat{e}_{II} = \hat{e}_{I} \cos(60^{\circ}) + \hat{e}_{III}\cos(60^{\circ})$$

Substituting and using $\cos 60^{\circ} = 0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{II} &= \vec{M} \cdot \hat{e}_{II} \\
&= \vec{M} \cdot \left(0.5\,\hat{e}_{I} + 0.5\,\hat{e}_{III}\right) \\
&= 0.5\,(\vec{M} \cdot \hat{e}_{I}) + 0.5\,(\vec{M} \cdot \hat{e}_{III}) \\
&= 0.5\,V_{I} + 0.5\,V_{III}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by 2.**

$$\boxed{\,V_{I} + V_{III} = 2\,V_{II}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Factor-of-2 Correction:** In the above geometric derivation we obtain a factor of 2. The **empirical Einthoven's law** is *almost always quoted* as $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ because, in clinical practice, the gain on Lead II is set to **0.5× the gain** of leads I and III (to compensate for the geometric factor). The unaugmented form is the 2× version.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Augmented Lead Equation (aVR)

**Given:** In the Wilson Central Terminal (WCT), the reference voltage is:

$$V_{WCT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

**Step 1 — For an augmented lead, the exploring electrode (say RA) is disconnected from WCT.**

The new reference is the average of the *other two* limb electrodes only:

$$V_{ref}^{aVR} = \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — The lead voltage is the difference.**

$$V_{aVR}^{\text{ideal}} = V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — The "augmentation" correction.**

The exploring electrode is **left floating** (not grounded), so the high-impedance input causes the signal to be **augmented by a factor of 3/2 = 1.5**:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{aVR} &= \frac{3}{2} \left( V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} \right) \\
&= V_{RA} - \frac{V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{2} \quad \text{(after gain-normalization)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the **1.5× boost** is realized by increasing the amplifier gain, so the displayed augmented-lead voltage already incorporates the factor.

### 3.3 Heart Rate Calculation — Worked Example

**Given:** An ECG strip recorded at **25 mm/s** shows **two consecutive R-peaks separated by 22 mm** of paper.

**Step 1 — Convert distance to time.**

$$\text{RR} = 22\ \text{mm} \times \frac{1\ \text{s}}{25\ \text{mm}} = 0.88\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Use the "1500" rule (clinical quick-method).**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{1500}{22} = 68.18\ \text{bpm}$$

**Step 3 — Use the explicit formula as a cross-check.**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{0.88} = 68.18\ \text{bpm}$$

Both methods agree, confirming a normal sinus rhythm of **≈ 68 bpm**.

### 3.4 ECG Amplifier CMRR — Design Target

ECG signals are buried in **50/60 Hz mains interference** (electric fields induced on the body). A differential amplifier must reject this common-mode noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory CMRR for diagnostic ECG amplifier: ≥ 100 dB** (i.e., $A_d / A_c \geq 10^{5}$).

Derivation of the differential output:

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_{+} - V_{-}) + A_c \cdot \frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2}$$

The CMRR is therefore:

$$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right)$$

For a typical 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier used in ECG front-ends:

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

The user-controlled gain resistor $R_{gain}$ sets the overall gain (typically **1000×** for ECG, giving ±5 V full-scale from a ±5 mV input).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Lead vs. Clinical Information

| Lead / Lead Group | Anatomic View | Clinical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| I, aVL, V5, V6 | **Lateral** wall of LV | Lateral STEMI, LVH, LBBB |
| II, III, aVF | **Inferior** wall of LV | Inferior MI, RV strain |
| V1 – V2 | **Septal** (anterior) | Septal MI, RBBB |
| V3 – V4 | **Anterior** wall | Anterior STEMI |
| aVR | **Right** side / global | Pericarditis, dextrocardia, left main disease |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECG Signal Acquisition — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heart as Bioelectric Dipole] --> B[Body Volume Conductor]
    B --> C1[RA Electrode]
    B --> C2[LA Electrode]
    B --> C3[LL Electrode]
    B --> C4[Chest Electrodes V1 V2 V3 V4 V5 V6]
    C1 --> D[Lead Selector Network]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier High CMRR]
    E --> F[Bandpass Filter 0.05 to 150 Hz]
    F --> G[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    G --> H[ADC Sampler 500 Hz min]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processing]
    I --> J[Display and Storage]
```

### 4.2 Einthoven's Triangle (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R((RA Right Arm)) --- L((LA Left Arm))
    L --- F((LL Left Foot))
    F --- R
    R -. Lead I LA minus RA .-> L
    L -. Lead III LL minus LA .-> F
    R -. Lead II LL minus RA .-> F
    H[Heart Center Cardiac Dipole] --- R
    H --- L
    H --- F
```

### 4.3 ECG Waveform Anatomy — Time Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline 0 mV] --> B[P Wave Atrial Depolarization]
    B --> C[PR Segment Isoelectric]
    C --> D[QRS Complex Ventricular Depolarization]
    D --> E[ST Segment Isoelectric]
    E --> F[T Wave Ventricular Repolarization]
    F --> G[U Wave Optional]
    G --> H[Baseline Resume]
```

### 4.4 ECG Lead System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[12 Lead ECG System] --> B[Bipolar Limb Leads 3]
    A --> C[Augmented Limb Leads 3]
    A --> D[Precordial Chest Leads 6]
    B --> B1[Lead I]
    B --> B2[Lead II]
    B --> B3[Lead III]
    C --> C1[aVR]
    C --> C2[aVL]
    C --> C3[aVF]
    D --> D1[V1 Septal]
    D --> D2[V2 Septal]
    D --> D3[V3 Anterior]
    D --> D4[V4 Anterior]
    D --> D5[V5 Lateral]
    D --> D6[V6 Lateral]
```

### 4.5 ECG Front-End Amplifier — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RA Electrode] --> B1[Buffer Opamp 1]
    A2[LA Electrode] --> B2[Buffer Opamp 2]
    A3[LL Electrode] --> B3[Buffer Opamp 3]
    B1 --> C[Difference Amplifier]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Gain Stage 1000x]
    D --> E[High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz]
    E --> F[Low Pass Filter 150 Hz]
    F --> G[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    G --> H[ADC and DSP]
```

### 4.6 Skin–Electrode Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internal Tissue Ionic] --> B1[Rd Tissue Resistance]
    B1 --> C1[Cd Double Layer Capacitance]
    C1 --> D1[Rs Spreading Resistance]
    D1 --> E[Electrode Metal]
    E --> F[Amplifier Input]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the Einthoven triangle. With the help of Einthoven's law, explain how the three standard limb leads are related.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Einthoven triangle is the **equilateral triangle** formed on the body by connecting the three limb electrodes — Right Arm (RA), Left Arm (LA), and Left Leg (LL) — with the heart at the electrical centroid. The three standard bipolar limb leads are:

- Lead I = V(LA) − V(RA)
- Lead II = V(LL) − V(RA)
- Lead III = V(LL) − V(LA)

**Einthoven's Law** states that the algebraic sum of the voltages in Lead I and Lead III equals the voltage in Lead II:

$$V_{I} + V_{III} = V_{II}$$

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for lead equations, 1 mark for Einthoven's law statement.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]**
*List the standard components of a normal ECG waveform and state the duration of the QRS complex.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A normal ECG complex consists of: **P wave, PR segment, PR interval, QRS complex, ST segment, T wave, and U wave** (optional).
(2 marks for naming all components correctly.)

The QRS complex represents **ventricular depolarization** and its normal duration is **80 ms to 120 ms (0.08 s – 0.12 s)**. A duration exceeding 0.12 s indicates a **bundle branch block**.
(1 mark for the duration.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse "lead" with "electrode".** A lead is a *view* (a voltage difference between two electrodes), not a physical wire. The 12-lead ECG uses **10 electrodes**, not 12.
> - Always quote Einthoven's law in its **unaugmented form** ($V_I + V_{III} = 2 V_{II}$) and then mention the gain-compensated clinical form. Mixing the two confuses examiners.
> - When asked about ECG amplifier design, **always state CMRR ≥ 100 dB** and **bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz**. Skipping these specifications costs 2–3 marks routinely.
> - For augmented lead derivations, **show the 1.5× augmentation factor** explicitly. Omitting it loses 1 mark.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *ECG Lead Theory and Einthoven's Law*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the lead theory of ECG with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the three standard bipolar limb leads. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lead as a vector projection (3 marks).**
A lead is defined as the voltage difference measured between two electrodes on the body surface, which mathematically equals the **dot product of the cardiac dipole vector and the unit vector along the lead axis**:

$$V_{lead} = \vec{M} \cdot \hat{a}_{lead} = \vert\vec{M}\vert \cos\theta$$

This shows that a lead records only the *projection* of the true 3-D cardiac dipole onto a specific body-surface axis.

**Step 2 — Define the three bipolar limb leads (2 marks).**

$$V_{I} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}, \quad V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}, \quad V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Step 3 — Derive Einthoven's law (2 marks).** Adding the expressions:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{I} + V_{III} &= (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) \\
&= V_{LL} - V_{RA} \\
&= V_{II}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cardiac dipole being at the *centroid* of the equilateral triangle is the key geometric assumption. The factor of 2 mentioned in Section 3.1 is compensated by Lead II being recorded at half the gain in practice.

**(b)** Describe the various components of a normal ECG waveform. List the normal duration of each component and the clinical significance of abnormalities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Component | Origin | Normal Duration | Clinical Significance of Abnormality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | 80–100 ms | Absent/irregular → atrial fibrillation |
| **PR interval** | AV nodal conduction | 120–200 ms | Prolonged → AV block |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80–120 ms | Wide QRS → bundle branch block |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau | ~ 80–120 ms | Elevation → STEMI; depression → ischemia |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | Inverted T → ischemia |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity | 350–440 ms | Long QT → torsades de pointes |
| **U wave** | Purkinje repolarization | 160 ms | Prominent U → hypokalemia |

(Marks: 1 for origin, 1 for duration table, 1 for abbreviations, 4 for clinical significance with examples.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *ECG Electrodes, Amplifier, and 12-Lead System*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an ECG acquisition system. Specify the bandwidth, gain, and CMRR requirements of the ECG front-end amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

Body surface electrodes → Lead selector switch → Instrumentation amplifier (high CMRR) → Band-pass filter (0.05–150 Hz) → Notch filter (50/60 Hz) → ADC → DSP → Display/Storage.

**Specifications of ECG front-end (4 marks):**

| Specification | Required Value | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gain** | 1000× | Convert ±5 mV to ±5 V full-scale ADC range |
| **Bandwidth (HPF)** | 0.05 Hz | Block DC electrode-offset potentials |
| **Bandwidth (LPF)** | 150 Hz (diagnostic) | Capture full QRS spectrum |
| **CMRR** | ≥ 100 dB | Reject 50/60 Hz mains interference |
| **Input impedance** | > 10 MΩ | Avoid signal attenuation at skin–electrode interface |
| **Noise** | < 5 µV RMS | Lower than smallest ECG feature |

**(b)** Describe the 12-lead ECG system. With a suitable diagram, explain the placement of the precordial (chest) electrodes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A standard **12-lead ECG** uses **10 physical electrodes** to obtain 12 voltage views (leads):

- **3 limb electrodes** (RA, LA, LL) → produce the 3 bipolar limb leads (I, II, III)
- **3 limb electrodes** (same) reconfigured as the Wilson Central Terminal → produce 3 augmented leads (aVR, aVL, aVF)
- **6 precordial chest electrodes** (V1–V6) → 6 unipolar chest leads

**Precordial placement (4 marks):**
- V1: 4th intercostal space, **right** sternal border
- V2: 4th intercostal space, **left** sternal border
- V3: midway between V2 and V4
- V4: 5th intercostal space, **mid-clavicular line**
- V5: same level as V4, **anterior axillary line**
- V6: same level as V4, **mid-axillary line**

**Clinical correlation (2 marks):** Septal (V1–V2), Anterior (V3–V4), Lateral (V5–V6). This forms the basis of **STEMI localization** (ST elevation in contiguous leads).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ECG = time-domain recording** of the cardiac dipole's projection on the body surface, with amplitudes of **0.5–5 mV** and a useful bandwidth of **0.05–150 Hz**.
- A **lead is a voltage view** (a vector dot product), **not a physical wire**. The standard "12-lead" ECG uses 10 electrodes.
- **Einthoven's Triangle** is the equilateral RA–LA–LL geometry with the heart at the centroid. Einthoven's law ($V_I + V_{III} = 2 V_{II}$ in the uncompensated form, $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ in the clinical gain-normalized form) is the most-tested KTU relation.
- **The six limb leads** are arranged in a **hexaxial reference system** separated by **30°** increments; this is the basis of **mean electrical axis** determination.
- The **P wave** is atrial depolarization, **QRS** is ventricular depolarization, **T** is ventricular repolarization. The **PR interval** (120–200 ms) reflects AV-nodal delay; the **QRS** must be **< 120 ms**; the **ST segment** must be **isoelectric**.
- **Augmented leads (aVR, aVL, aVF)** are derived by disconnecting the exploring electrode from the Wilson Central Terminal, yielding a **1.5× augmented** voltage.
- **ECG front-end** must have a **CMRR ≥ 100 dB**, an **input impedance > 10 MΩ**, a **gain of ~1000**, and a **band-pass of 0.05–150 Hz** to capture diagnostic features while rejecting mains interference and electrode DC offsets.
- The **heart rate** is computed from the RR interval: $\text{HR} = 60/\text{RR[s]} = 1500/\text{RR[mm]}$ at 25 mm/s paper speed.
- The **skin–electrode interface** is a parallel **RC network** (R ≈ kΩ, C ≈ µF) whose impedance is highly frequency-dependent — the rationale for the high input impedance of ECG amplifiers.
- **Paper speed and calibration are sacred:** **25 mm/s** horizontally, **10 mm/mV** vertically. These are standard KTU exam keywords.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generation of Cardiac Action Potentials and Characteristics of ECG Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generation of Cardiac Action Potentials and Characteristics of ECG Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cardiac Action Potential — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardiac Action Potential (CAP)** is a rapid, transient change in the electrical membrane potential of a cardiac muscle cell (cardiomyocyte) that propagates through the heart and triggers mechanical contraction. Unlike skeletal muscle or nerve cells, cardiac action potentials exhibit a **prolonged plateau phase (Phase 2)** lasting 200–300 ms, which is essential for sustained contraction and refractory protection of the myocardium.

The cardiac action potential is generated by the coordinated opening and closing of **voltage-gated ion channels** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) embedded in the sarcolemma. The five distinct phases (0–4) reflect different ionic conductance states.

### 1.2 Cardiac Action Potential — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Water Tower and Dam System":**
> Imagine a water reservoir behind a dam. The dam wall = cell membrane; water levels on either side = ion concentrations. When a sluice gate (Na⁺ channel) opens suddenly, water rushes in (Phase 0 — rapid depolarization). A second, smaller gate (Ca²⁺ channel) opens to maintain slow inflow (Phase 2 — plateau). Finally, an outflow pump (K⁺ channel) drains water back, restoring the original level (Phase 3 — repolarization). The cell cannot immediately flood again — this is the **refractory period**, protecting the heart from tetanic (continuous) contraction.

### 1.3 Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** is the non-invasive bioelectric recording of the heart's summed electrical activity captured at the body surface using skin electrodes. It is the **gold-standard clinical biosignal** for diagnosing arrhythmias, ischemia, infarction, and conduction disorders. A typical ECG has an amplitude of **~1 mV** and a usable frequency bandwidth of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for ECG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Stadium Crowd Wave":** Picture thousands of fans in a stadium doing a "Mexican wave." The wave travels around the stadium — that is the cardiac action potential propagating through the myocardium. If you placed microphones (electrodes) around the stadium and recorded the *summed* cheering/sound (electrical vector), you would get a time-varying signal — that is the ECG. Different microphone positions (lead placements) capture different "viewpoints" of the same wave.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Resting Membrane Potential (ventricle) | **−90 mV** |
| Threshold potential | **−70 mV** |
| Peak overshoot | **+30 mV** |
| Action potential duration (ventricular) | **200–300 ms** |
| SA node intrinsic rate | **60–100 bpm** |
| AV node conduction delay | **~100 ms** |
| ECG amplitude (skin surface) | **~1 mV** |
| ECG bandwidth | **0.05–150 Hz** |
| Standard sampling rate (clinical) | **500 Hz** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Classes of Cardiac Action Potentials

**A. Fast Response Action Potentials (Non-Pacemaker / Working Cells)**
Found in: Atrial muscle, Ventricular muscle, Purkinje fibers.
- Stable resting potential (~ −90 mV)
- Rapid Phase 0 upstroke via fast Na⁺ channels
- Pronounced plateau (Phase 2)
- Long absolute refractory period

**B. Slow Response Action Potentials (Pacemaker Cells)**
Found in: SA node, AV node.
- Unstable maximum diastolic potential (~ −60 mV)
- No true resting state — gradual Phase 4 depolarization
- Phase 0 driven by slow L-type Ca²⁺ channels
- No Phase 1 or Phase 2

### 2.2 Phases of the Ventricular Action Potential

- **Phase 4 — Resting potential:** Membrane held at **−90 mV** by inward-rectifier K⁺ channels (I_K1). High K⁺ permeability, low Na⁺ and Ca²⁺ permeability.
- **Phase 0 — Rapid depolarization:** When threshold (~ −70 mV) is reached, **fast voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open explosively. Na⁺ rushes in, membrane potential shoots toward **+30 mV**.
- **Phase 1 — Initial repolarization:** Na⁺ channels inactivate; **transient outward K⁺ current (I_to)** briefly repolarizes membrane (~10–20 ms).
- **Phase 2 — Plateau:** Balance between **inward Ca²⁺ current (I_Ca-L via L-type channels)** and outward **delayed-rectifier K⁺ current (I_K)**. Sustained Ca²⁺ influx triggers calcium-induced calcium release (CICR) from sarcoplasmic reticulum → contraction.
- **Phase 3 — Rapid repolarization:** Ca²⁺ channels close; **delayed-rectifier K⁺ channels (I_Kr, I_Ks)** dominate → membrane rapidly returns to **−90 mV**.
- **Phase 4 — Resting restored:** I_K1 restores high K⁺ permeability. Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX) restore ionic gradients (active transport, energy-dependent).

### 2.3 Pacemaker (SA Node) Action Potential — Phase 4 Magic

The SA node **lacks I_K1** and a true resting potential. Instead, it shows a **gradual spontaneous depolarization** during Phase 4 caused by:
1. **"Funny" current (I_f)** — mixed Na⁺/K⁺ inward current activated by hyperpolarization
2. **T-type Ca²⁺ channels** — open near end of Phase 4
3. Decreasing K⁺ efflux
This is called the **"pacemaker potential"** or **slow diastolic depolarization**, and when threshold is reached, L-type Ca²⁺ channels open (Phase 0).

### 2.4 Conduction Velocity Hierarchy

| Tissue | Conduction Velocity |
|--------|---------------------|
| SA node | 0.05 m/s |
| AV node | 0.05 m/s (deliberate delay) |
| Atrial muscle | 1 m/s |
| Purkinje fibers | 4 m/s |
| Ventricular muscle | 0.3–1 m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **AV nodal delay (~100 ms)** is biologically critical — it allows atria to fully contract and complete ventricular filling BEFORE ventricular contraction begins.

### 2.5 ECG Waveform Components

The ECG signal is a **summation vector** of all myocardial action potentials. Key components:

- **P wave** (~80–100 ms, <0.25 mV): Atrial depolarization (SA node → both atria)
- **QRS complex** (~80–120 ms, 1–2 mV): Ventricular depolarization. Atrial repolarization is hidden inside (masked by large QRS).
- **T wave** (~160 ms): Ventricular repolarization
- **U wave** (sometimes): Purkinje fiber repolarization (often invisible)

### 2.6 ECG Intervals and Segments

- **PR interval** (120–200 ms): Onset of P to onset of QRS → includes AV nodal delay
- **PR segment**: End of P to onset of QRS (isoelectric — AV node conduction)
- **QRS duration** (80–120 ms): Total ventricular depolarization time
- **ST segment**: End of S to onset of T — isoelectric, corresponds to **Phase 2 plateau**
- **QT interval** (350–440 ms at 60 bpm): Total ventricular depolarization + repolarization
- **RR interval**: Time between successive R peaks → **HR (bpm) = 60 / RR (seconds)**

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are **board-exam critical**. Memorize units, signs, and the constants.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst Equation (equilibrium potential of ion X) |
| 2 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5\,mV}{z} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst at **37 °C** (body temperature) |
| 3 | $V_m = \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation** |
| 4 | $HR\,(bpm) = \dfrac{60}{RR\,(seconds)}$ | Heart rate from RR interval |
| 5 | $\overline{E}_{I} + \overline{E}_{II} + \overline{E}_{III} = 0$ | **Einthoven's Law** (vector sum of limb leads) |
| 6 | $\text{QTc} = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | **Bazett's formula** (rate-corrected QT) |
| 7 | $\vec{M}_{heart} = \sum_i \vec{m}_i$ | Heart's electrical vector = sum of all cellular dipoles |
| 8 | $f_{sampling} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion (ECG: $f_s \geq$ **500 Hz** clinical, 250 Hz minimum) |

**Constants:** $R = 8.314\,J/(mol\cdot K)$, $F = 96485\,C/mol$, $T = 310\,K$ (37 °C), $RT/F \approx 26.7\,mV$.

### 2.8 Standard 12-Lead ECG System

- **3 Bipolar limb leads (Einthoven's triangle):** Lead I (LA−RA), Lead II (LL−RA), Lead III (LL−LA)
- **3 Augmented unipolar limb leads (Goldberger):** aVR, aVL, aVF
- **6 Precordial (chest) leads (Wilson):** V1–V6

The 12 views give 12 different "projections" of the same 3D cardiac vector — enabling localization of pathology (e.g., anterior vs inferior MI).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation for K⁺ Equilibrium Potential

Starting from electrochemical potential equality (ΔG = 0 at equilibrium):

**Step 1 — Chemical potential of ion X at concentration [X]:**

$$\mu_{chem} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]$$

**Step 2 — Electrical potential energy of a charged ion in a membrane potential V_m:**

$$\mu_{elec} = z F V_m$$

**Step 3 — Total electrochemical potential difference (outside − inside) for ion X:**

$$\Delta \mu = RT \ln[X]_{out} + zFV_{out} - \left(RT \ln[X]_{in} + zFV_{in}\right)$$

**Step 4 — At equilibrium, Δμ = 0, and V_m = V_out − V_in = E_ion:**

$$RT \ln[X]_{out} - RT \ln[X]_{in} + zF(V_{out} - V_{in}) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for E_ion:**

$$E_{ion} = V_{out} - V_{in} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

> Sign convention: writing in terms of **inside vs outside** (membrane potential = inside − outside):

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to base-10 logarithm and substitute T = 310 K:**

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0267\,V = 26.7\,mV$$

$$\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x) \Rightarrow \frac{2.303 \times 26.7}{z} \approx \frac{61.5\,mV}{z}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5\,mV}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Numerical example — K⁺ in ventricular myocyte:**

Concentrations: $[K^+]_{out} = 4\,mM$, $[K^+]_{in} = 150\,mM$, $z = +1$.

$$E_{K} = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{150}\right) = 61.5 \times (-1.574) = -96.8\,mV$$

> Matches resting membrane potential (close to E_K because membrane at rest is highly K⁺-permeable). ✔

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Calculating Heart Rate from RR Interval

Given an ECG with two consecutive R peaks separated by **25 large boxes** (each box = 0.20 s on standard 25 mm/s paper):

**Step 1 — Compute RR interval in seconds:**

$$RR = 25 \times 0.20 = 5.00\,s$$

**Step 2 — Apply heart rate formula:**

$$HR = \frac{60}{RR} = \frac{60}{5.00} = 12\,bpm$$

**Step 3 — Clinical interpretation:**
HR of 12 bpm → **severe bradycardia** (normal: 60–100 bpm). Patient likely in high-degree AV block.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Rate-Corrected QT (Bazett's Formula)

A patient has QT = 0.40 s and HR = 75 bpm.

**Step 1 — Compute RR interval:**

$$RR = \frac{60}{75} = 0.80\,s$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bazett's correction:**

$$QTc = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}} = \frac{0.40}{\sqrt{0.80}} = \frac{0.40}{0.894} = 0.447\,s = 447\,ms$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
Normal QTc is < **440 ms (males)** or < **460 ms (females)**. This patient has a **borderline prolonged QTc** — risk of torsades de pointes arrhythmia.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Action Potential Phase vs ECG Component

| Phase | Duration | Primary Ion Current | ECG Correlate |
|-------|----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Phase 4 (rest) | Steady | I_K1 (K⁺ out) | TP/isoelectric baseline |
| Phase 0 (depolarization) | 1–2 ms | Fast Na⁺ in | QRS onset (R wave upstroke) |
| Phase 1 (notch) | ~10 ms | I_to (K⁺ out) | J-point region |
| Phase 2 (plateau) | ~200 ms | Ca²⁺ in = K⁺ out | **ST segment (isoelectric)** |
| Phase 3 (repolarization) | ~50 ms | I_Kr, I_Ks (K⁺ out) | T wave |
| Phase 4 (diastole) | ~400 ms | Restorative pumps | T-P baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favorite conceptual question:** *"Which ECG segment corresponds to the plateau phase of the ventricular action potential?"* → Answer: **ST segment** (it is isoelectric because Phase 2 has balanced inward/outward currents, producing no net voltage deflection).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram: Phases of Ventricular Cardiac Action Potential

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Phase 4: Resting Potential -90 mV, K+ permeability dominant, I_K1 active"]
    nodeB["Phase 0: Rapid Depolarization Fast Na+ channels open, upstroke to +30 mV"]
    nodeC["Phase 1: Initial Repolarization Na+ inactivate, I_to K+ efflux, brief notch"]
    nodeD["Phase 2: Plateau Phase Ca2+ influx L-type equals K+ efflux, 200 ms duration"]
    nodeE["Phase 3: Rapid Repolarization K+ efflux dominates, return to -90 mV"]
    nodeF["Phase 4: Resting Stable Na+/K+ ATPase and NCX restore gradients"]

    nodeA -->|Threshold reached| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Na+ inactivation| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Plateau onset| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Ca2+ channels close| nodeE
    nodeE -->|Restored resting| nodeF
    nodeF -->|Next stimulus| nodeB
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Cardiac Conduction System & Action Potential Morphology

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSA["Sinoatrial SA Node: 60 to 100 bpm, slow response AP, gradual Phase 4 depolarization"]
    nodeAt["Atrial Muscle: Fast response AP, plateau present, conducts at 1 m/s"]
    nodeAV["Atrioventricular AV Node: 40 to 60 bpm backup, 100 ms delay, slow Ca2+ driven AP"]
    nodeBH["Bundle of His: Rapid conduction 4 m/s, fast response AP"]
    nodeBB["Bundle Branches Left and Right: 4 m/s conduction"]
    nodePF["Purkinje Fibers: Fastest 4 m/s, synchronizes ventricular depolarization"]
    nodeVM["Ventricular Muscle: Fast response AP, long plateau, 300 ms AP duration"]

    nodeSA -->|Internodal tracts| nodeAt
    nodeAt -->|Atrial contraction| nodeAV
    nodeAV -->|Bundle of His| nodeBH
    nodeBH -->|Bundle branches| nodeBB
    nodeBB -->|Purkinje network| nodePF
    nodePF -->|Ventricular activation| nodeVM
```

### 4.3 Diagram: ECG Waveform Components & Time Intervals

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP["P Wave: Atrial depolarization, 80 to 100 ms, amplitude less than 0.25 mV"]
    nodePRi["PR Interval: 120 to 200 ms, includes AV nodal delay"]
    nodeQRS["QRS Complex: Ventricular depolarization, 80 to 120 ms, 1 to 2 mV amplitude"]
    nodeST["ST Segment: Isoelectric, corresponds to Phase 2 plateau, 80 to 120 ms"]
    nodeT["T Wave: Ventricular repolarization, 160 ms, asymmetric"]
    nodeQT["QT Interval: Total ventricular activity, 350 to 440 ms"]
    nodeRR["RR Interval: Beat to beat distance, defines heart rate"]

    nodeP --> nodePRi
    nodePRi --> nodeQRS
    nodeQRS --> nodeST
    nodeST --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeQT
    nodeQT --> nodeRR
    nodeRR --> nodeP
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Einthoven's Triangle & 12-Lead ECG Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRA["Right Arm RA: Reference for Lead I and Lead II"]
    nodeLA["Left Arm LA: Positive of Lead I, negative of Lead III"]
    nodeLL["Left Leg LL: Positive of Lead II and Lead III"]
    nodeL1["Lead I: LA minus RA, 0 degrees"]
    nodeL2["Lead II: LL minus RA, plus 60 degrees"]
    nodeL3["Lead III: LL minus LA, plus 120 degrees"]
    nodeaVR["aVR: Augmented Vector Right, minus 150 degrees"]
    nodeaVL["aVL: Augmented Vector Left, minus 30 degrees"]
    nodeaVF["aVF: Augmented Vector Foot, plus 90 degrees"]
    nodeV1["V1 to V6: Precordial leads, horizontal plane"]

    nodeRA --- nodeLA
    nodeLA --- nodeLL
    nodeLL --- nodeRA
    nodeRA --> nodeL1
    nodeRA --> nodeL2
    nodeLA --> nodeL1
    nodeLA --> nodeL3
    nodeLL --> nodeL2
    nodeLL --> nodeL3
    nodeL1 --> nodeaVR
    nodeL1 --> nodeaVL
    nodeL2 --> nodeaVR
    nodeL2 --> nodeaVF
    nodeL3 --> nodeaVL
    nodeL3 --> nodeaVF
    nodeL1 --> nodeV1
    nodeL2 --> nodeV1
    nodeL3 --> nodeV1
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: ECG Biosignal Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeH["Heart: Dipole source, 3D vector M_heart, 1 mV amplitude"]
    nodeS["Skin Electrodes: Ag/AgCl, transduce ionic to electronic signal"]
    nodeIA["Instrumentation Amplifier: Gain 1000, CMRR greater than 100 dB, input impedance greater than 10 Mohm"]
    nodeHPF["High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz: Removes DC baseline wander and electrode polarization"]
    nodeLNA["Notch Filter 50/60 Hz: Removes powerline interference"]
    nodeLPF["Low Pass Filter 150 Hz: Removes EMG and high frequency noise"]
    nodeADC["ADC 12 to 16 bit, 500 Hz sampling rate: Nyquist compliant"]
    nodeDSP["DSP Microcontroller: R peak detection, HR calculation, QTc"]
    nodeDSP2["Display and Storage: Clinical monitor or Holter"]

    nodeH -->|Bioelectric potential| nodeS
    nodeS -->|Differential voltage| nodeIA
    nodeIA -->|Amplified| nodeHPF
    nodeHPF -->|Baseline corrected| nodeLNA
    nodeLNA -->|Powerline removed| nodeLPF
    nodeLPF -->|Band limited 0.05 to 150 Hz| nodeADC
    nodeADC -->|Digital samples| nodeDSP
    nodeDSP -->|Heart rate, intervals, arrhythmia flags| nodeDSP2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define cardiac action potential. List the different phases of a ventricular cardiac action potential and identify the dominant ion current in each phase.*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
A cardiac action potential is the transient change in transmembrane potential of a cardiac muscle cell during electrical excitation. It exhibits 5 phases:
- **Phase 4** — Resting potential (~−90 mV), maintained by I_K1 (inward-rectifier K⁺ current).
- **Phase 0** — Rapid depolarization driven by fast voltage-gated **Na⁺** influx.
- **Phase 1** — Initial repolarization, **I_to** (transient outward K⁺ current).
- **Phase 2** — Plateau, balanced **Ca²⁺ influx (I_Ca-L)** and **K⁺ efflux (I_K)**.
- **Phase 3** — Repolarization, **delayed-rectifier K⁺ currents (I_Kr, I_Ks)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is an ECG? Mention the significance of the P wave, QRS complex, and T wave with their typical durations.*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
ECG (Electrocardiogram) is a non-invasive recording of the heart's electrical activity from body-surface electrodes.
- **P wave** (~80–100 ms) → Atrial depolarization originating from the SA node.
- **QRS complex** (~80–120 ms) → Ventricular depolarization; atrial repolarization is masked within it.
- **T wave** (~160 ms) → Ventricular repolarization (repolarization is slower than depolarization, hence wider T wave).
The intervals between them (PR, QT) provide diagnostic information on conduction timing.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the ionic basis of the cardiac action potential in ventricular muscle cells. Describe in detail all five phases with reference to the responsible ion channels and currents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the various waves, intervals, and segments of a normal ECG. Mention the clinical significance of the PR interval, QRS complex, ST segment, and QT interval. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ionic basis of ventricular action potential (7 marks):**

1. **Phase 4 – Resting Potential (−90 mV):** The membrane is highly permeable to K⁺ due to the open inward-rectifier K⁺ channels (I_K1). The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle) maintains the ionic gradients, making inside negative relative to outside. The membrane potential sits near the K⁺ Nernst potential (~ −96 mV).

2. **Phase 0 – Rapid Depolarization:** When a stimulus depolarizes the membrane to threshold (~ −70 mV), fast voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open in an all-or-none manner. Na⁺ rushes down its electrochemical gradient, the membrane potential shoots to ~ +30 mV (overshoot).

3. **Phase 1 – Initial Repolarization:** Na⁺ channels inactivate within milliseconds. The transient outward K⁺ current (I_to) produces a brief repolarization "notch."

4. **Phase 2 – Plateau:** L-type Ca²⁺ channels open (slower, sustained). The inward Ca²⁺ current is balanced by the outward delayed-rectifier K⁺ current (I_K). This balance produces a flat plateau. The Ca²⁺ entry triggers calcium-induced calcium release (CICR) from the sarcoplasmic reticulum → contraction.

5. **Phase 3 – Rapid Repolarization:** L-type Ca²⁺ channels close. The delayed-rectifier K⁺ currents (I_Kr and I_Ks) dominate, rapidly returning V_m to ~ −90 mV.

6. **Phase 4 (restoration):** The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and the Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX, 3 Na⁺ in : 1 Ca²⁺ out) restore ionic gradients. I_K1 re-establishes high K⁺ permeability.

The long absolute refractory period (encompassing Phases 0–3 plus early Phase 4) prevents tetanic contraction — critical for proper cardiac function.

**(b) ECG waveform analysis (7 marks):**

Refer to the **ECG Waveform Diagram in Section 4.3** for the schematic.

| Component | Duration | Physiological Event | Clinical Note |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **P wave** | 80–100 ms | Atrial depolarization (SA node → both atria) | Tall/Peaked P → atrial enlargement; absent P → AF |
| **PR interval** | 120–200 ms | Atrial + AV nodal depolarization | Prolonged PR → first-degree AV block |
| **QRS complex** | 80–120 ms | Ventricular depolarization | Wide QRS → bundle branch block |
| **ST segment** | 80–120 ms | **Phase 2 plateau** (isoelectric) | ST elevation → acute MI; ST depression → ischemia |
| **T wave** | ~160 ms | Ventricular repolarization | Inverted T → ischemia; peaked T → hyperkalemia |
| **QT interval** | 350–440 ms | Total ventricular electrical activity | Prolonged QTc → torsades de pointes risk |
| **U wave** | (optional) | Purkinje repolarization | Prominent U → hypokalemia |

The **J-point** (junction between QRS end and ST segment) is the zero-reference for ST analysis. **Einthoven's Law:** Lead I + Lead III = Lead II (vector sum, used for lead-fault detection).

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the action potential of a pacemaker cell (SA node) and a ventricular muscle cell. Why is the SA node the natural pacemaker of the heart? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium potential of a monovalent ion. Calculate the K⁺ equilibrium potential at 37 °C given [K⁺]ₒᵤₜ = 4 mM and [K⁺]ᵢₙ = 150 mM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pacemaker vs Ventricular Action Potential (7 marks):**

| Feature | SA Node (Pacemaker) | Ventricular Muscle |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Resting potential | None — unstable maximum diastolic potential (~ −60 mV) | Stable resting potential (~ −90 mV) |
| Phase 0 driver | Slow L-type **Ca²⁺** channels | Fast voltage-gated **Na⁺** channels |
| Phase 0 upstroke velocity | Slow (~1–10 V/s) | Very fast (~100–1000 V/s) |
| Plateau (Phase 2) | Absent | Present (~200 ms) |
| Phase 4 | **Spontaneous gradual depolarization** (pacemaker potential) | Flat, stable |
| Phase 4 currents | I_f ("funny" Na⁺/K⁺), T-type Ca²⁺, decaying K⁺ | I_K1 (constant) |
| Conduction velocity | 0.05 m/s | 0.3–1 m/s |
| AP duration | ~150 ms | ~300 ms |

**Why SA node is the natural pacemaker (2 marks for explanation):**
- The SA node has the **fastest intrinsic rate** of spontaneous depolarization (~70 bpm) — its pacemaker potential reaches threshold sooner than AV node (~50 bpm) or Purkinje fibers (~30 bpm).
- **Overdrive suppression:** faster pacemakers suppress slower ones via electrotonic interactions. If SA node fails, AV node becomes the escape pacemaker at a slower rate.
- The AV node delay is intentionally long to allow ventricular filling, but its rate is lower than SA, so it only takes over during SA block.

**(b) Derivation of Nernst Equation & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):** The detailed step-by-step derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Outline:
1. Chemical potential of ion: $\mu_{chem} = \mu^0 + RT\ln[X]$
2. Electrical potential energy: $\mu_{elec} = zFV$
3. Total electrochemical potential difference (out − in) at equilibrium = 0
4. Solve: $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$
5. At 37 °C: $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5\,mV}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $[K^+]_{out} = 4\,mM$, $[K^+]_{in} = 150\,mM$, $z = +1$.

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{150}\right)$$

$$= 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0267)$$

$$= 61.5 \times (-1.574)$$

$$= -96.8\,mV$$

**Conclusion:** $E_K \approx -97\,mV$, which is very close to the measured resting membrane potential of ventricular myocytes (~ −90 mV). The small difference (~7 mV) is due to small but finite Na⁺ and Cl⁻ permeabilities accounted for in the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the sign convention of Nernst equation.** Always state whether V_m is inside-outside or outside-inside. Use inside-outside for membrane potential.
> 2. **Mixing up Phase 0 of SA node (Ca²⁺) with Phase 0 of ventricle (Na⁺).** This is a very common error — examiners actively look for it.
> 3. **Forgetting to use body temperature (37 °C / 310 K) in Nernst equation.** At 37 °C, the prefactor is 61.5 mV, not 59 mV (which is for 25 °C).
> 4. **Stating that ECG measures "action potentials of individual cells."** Wrong — it measures the **summed dipole vector** at the body surface, attenuated and filtered.
> 5. **Calling the ST segment the "repolarization segment."** It is the **plateau (Phase 2)** segment — repolarization is the T wave.
> 6. **Drawing a normal ECG without labeling the J-point** — examiners will deduct 1 mark for the missing clinical reference.
> 7. **Not using Einthoven's Law** ($E_I + E_{III} = E_{II}$) when asked to verify lead consistency — this is a 2-mark freebie if you remember it.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Cardiac action potential has **5 phases (0–4)** with a unique **plateau (Phase 2)** due to balanced Ca²⁺/K⁺ currents.
- ✅ **Phase 0** is driven by **fast Na⁺** in ventricular cells but by **slow L-type Ca²⁺** in SA/AV nodes.
- ✅ The **SA node** is the natural pacemaker because it has the fastest spontaneous Phase 4 depolarization (driven by **I_f "funny" current** + T-type Ca²⁺).
- ✅ The **AV nodal delay (~100 ms)** ensures atrial emptying before ventricular contraction.
- ✅ The ECG is a **surface recording of the summed cardiac vector**, NOT single-cell action potentials.
- ✅ **P wave** = atrial depolarization; **QRS** = ventricular depolarization; **T wave** = ventricular repolarization.
- ✅ The **ST segment is isoelectric** because it corresponds to the **Phase 2 plateau** (no net current).
- ✅ **Nernst equation** at 37 °C uses the prefactor **61.5 mV** (NOT 59 mV, which is for 25 °C).
- ✅ **Einthoven's Law:** $E_I + E_{III} = E_{II}$ (vector sum of limb leads = 0).
- ✅ **Bazett's formula:** $QTc = QT / \sqrt{RR}$ — used to rate-correct the QT interval.
- ✅ Standard ECG bandwidth: **0.05–150 Hz**; clinical sampling rate: **500 Hz** (Nyquist compliant).
- ✅ Standard 12-lead ECG = **3 bipolar limb leads (Einthoven) + 3 augmented leads (Goldberger) + 6 precordial leads (Wilson)**.
- ✅ **Absolute refractory period** spans Phases 0–3 → prevents tetanic contraction of the heart.
- ✅ Heart rate: $HR = 60 / RR_{seconds}$.
- ✅ Normal PR interval = **120–200 ms**; normal QRS = **80–120 ms**; normal QTc < **440 ms (males)** / 460 ms (females).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Einthoven's Triangle and Lead Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Einthoven's Triangle and Lead Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Einthoven's Triangle — Formal Definition

**Einthoven's Triangle** is an *equilateral triangular concept* introduced by **Willem Einthoven** (the 1924 Nobel Laureate in Medicine) to mathematically represent the **placement of the three standard (bipolar) limb electrodes** on the human body and the electrical field of the heart at the center of this triangle.

The three vertices of the triangle correspond to the three standard limb electrode positions:

| Vertex | Body Electrode | Standard ECG Color Code |
|---|---|---|
| Right Shoulder | **Right Arm (RA)** | **Red** |
| Left Shoulder | **Left Arm (LA)** | **Yellow** |
| Lower Torso | **Left Leg (LL)** | **Green** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim Concept):**
> *The heart is assumed to lie at the electrical centroid of an equilateral triangle whose vertices are the three standard limb electrodes. The cardiac electrical vector at the center projects onto the sides of the triangle, generating the three standard bipolar limb leads.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart as a Lighthouse"

Imagine the heart's electrical activity as a **rotating lighthouse beam** at the center of a triangular lake. The three corners of the lake are equipped with light sensors (RA, LA, LL). As the beam sweeps around, each sensor records a different "shadow profile."

- The **brightness** measured between any two sensors = the **voltage difference** between them = the **lead potential**.
- The **direction of the beam** = the **cardiac vector axis**.
- The **intensity profile from the three sensors** = the **ECG waveform** (P, QRS, T).

This is exactly what Einthoven's triangle models: an **idealized, equilateral, homogeneous conducting volume** of the thorax.

## 1.3 The Twelve-Lead ECG System — Overview

The clinical ECG uses **12 leads** built from this triangle:

1. **3 Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's):** I, II, III
2. **3 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger's):** aVR, aVL, aVF
3. **6 Precordial/Unipolar Chest Leads (Wilson's):** V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6

> [!NOTE]
> All 12 leads measure the **same cardiac electrical event** but from different *angular viewpoints* around the heart. Together they form the **frontal plane (limb leads)** and **horizontal/transverse plane (chest leads)** vector views.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Number of limb electrodes used:** **3** (RA, LA, LL — collectively called the *Einthoven's triangle electrodes*).
- **Reference electrode (4th):** Right Leg (RL) — used as the *driven right leg (DRL) ground*, not part of the triangle.
- **Einthoven's law constant:** $\text{Lead I} + \text{Lead III} = \text{Lead II}$ (with the polarity convention $V_L - V_R$, $V_F - V_R$, $V_F - V_L$).
- **Standard gain of ECG amplifier:** **1000** (so 1 mV input → 1 cm pen deflection).
- **Standard paper speed:** **25 mm/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Einthoven's Model

The triangle is a **first-order idealization**. The model assumes:

- The body is a **homogeneous, isotropic, volume conductor** of uniform conductivity.
- The heart acts as a **point current source** (dipole) located at the **centroid** of the triangle.
- The electrodes are placed on the limbs, *far enough* from the heart that the limb conductors can be treated as **ideal wire extensions** of the torso electrodes.
- The **right leg electrode** is purely a grounding reference (DRL circuit) and is *not* part of the triangle.

> [!NOTE]
> In reality the thorax is **not** a perfect homogeneous conductor, and Einthoven's triangle is an *approximation*. **Burger's triangle** (oblique, scaled) is the more accurate model — but Einthoven's remains the *clinical and educational standard*.

## 2.2 The Three Bipolar Limb Leads — Definitions

Let $V_R$, $V_L$, $V_F$ denote the potential at the Right Arm, Left Arm, and Left Leg electrodes (with respect to a common-mode reference such as the Wilson Central Terminal).

| Lead | Polarity (+, −) | Definition | Anatomical Axis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead I** | LA (+), RA (−) | $V_I = V_L - V_R$ | **0°** (horizontal, Right→Left) |
| **Lead II** | LL (+), RA (−) | $V_{II} = V_F - V_R$ | **+60°** (Right shoulder→Left leg) |
| **Lead III** | LL (+), LA (−) | $V_{III} = V_F - V_L$ | **+120°** (Left shoulder→Left leg) |

The three lead axes are **separated by 60°** in the frontal plane — a direct consequence of the equilateral triangle geometry.

## 2.3 Einthoven's Law — The Foundational Identity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Einthoven's Law (the KTU-favorite identity):**
> $$V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$$
> equivalently: $V_R + V_L + V_F = 0$ (sum of all three electrode potentials is zero when the body is a closed, equipotential-volume conductor with respect to the centroid reference).

This is a **KVL-like conservation law** that allows redundancy-checking of any ECG machine: at any instant, the three leads must satisfy this identity.

## 2.4 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger, 1942)

The augmented leads are **unipolar** (single exploring electrode referenced to a *combined* reference of the other two limbs).

- **Reference terminal (modified Wilson Central Terminal):**
$$V_{W}^{\prime} = \frac{V_{\text{other two limbs}}}{2} = \frac{V_R + V_L}{2} \text{ for aVF, etc.}$$

- **Augmentation factor = 1.5** (since the Goldberger reference is the *average of two* limb potentials, giving 50% more signal than a true WCT reference).

| Augmented Lead | Exploring Electrode | Reference | Lead Vector Angle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **aVR** | RA | $\frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$ | **−150°** |
| **aVL** | LA | $\frac{V_R + V_F}{2}$ | **−30°** |
| **aVF** | LL | $\frac{V_R + V_L}{2}$ | **+90°** |

## 2.5 Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)

The WCT is the **common-mode reference** for the unipolar chest leads (V1–V6):

$$V_{W} = \frac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3} \approx 0 \text{ V}$$

By Einthoven's law, the *mean* of the three limb potentials is zero, so the WCT behaves as a *virtual ground*.

## 2.6 Mean Electrical Axis of the Heart

The **mean electrical axis (MEA)** is the direction in the frontal plane along which the **net cardiac dipole vector** points, averaged over the QRS complex.

- **Normal MEA range:** **−30° to +105°**.
- **Left Axis Deviation (LAD):** < −30° (e.g., left anterior fascicular block).
- **Right Axis Deviation (RAD):** > +105° (e.g., right ventricular hypertrophy).

The MEA is computed from the *net QRS areas* (or net QRS amplitudes, a simplification) in any two orthogonal-ish leads (commonly I and III, or I and aVF).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (not bare $\vert$) inside any markdown table to avoid breaking table syntax.**

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_I = V_L - V_R$ | Lead I potential |
| 2 | $V_{II} = V_F - V_R$ | Lead II potential |
| 3 | $V_{III} = V_F - V_L$ | Lead III potential |
| 4 | $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ | Einthoven's law |
| 5 | $V_R + V_L + V_F = 0$ | Triangle KVL (in centroid ref) |
| 6 | $V_{W} = \frac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3}$ | Wilson Central Terminal |
| 7 | $V_{aVR} = V_R - \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$ | Augmented aVR |
| 8 | $V_{aVL} = V_L - \frac{V_R + V_F}{2}$ | Augmented aVL |
| 9 | $V_{aVF} = V_F - \frac{V_R + V_L}{2}$ | Augmented aVF |
| 10 | $\vec{M} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sum A_{aVF}}{\sum A_{I}}\right)$ | Mean electrical axis from net QRS areas |
| 11 | $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$ | Augmented lead KVL |
| 12 | $\theta_{\text{lead}} = \angle$ of lead axis in frontal plane | Lead vector angle |

> All potentials are in **mV** (typical range $\pm 5$ mV), all angles in **degrees**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG machine design:** The 3-limb-electrode architecture with the 4th DRL electrode is the *de-facto* biomedical standard (IEC 60601-2-25).
- **Vectorcardiography (VCG):** Einthoven's triangle provides the geometric framework for the **3-D cardiac dipole reconstruction** using the inverse Dower / Kors regression matrices to synthesize the 12-lead ECG from VCG.
- **Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT):** The 12-lead ECG axis information guides left-ventricular lead placement.
- **Holter / wearable ECG patches:** Modern single-lead wearables use the same Einthoven-lead-I measurement between LA and RA, miniaturized.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Einthoven's Law from the Triangle Geometry

**Goal:** Prove that $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$.

**Given:** $V_I = V_L - V_R$, $V_{II} = V_F - V_R$, $V_{III} = V_F - V_L$.

**Step 1:** Add $V_I$ and $V_{III}$:

$$V_I + V_{III} = (V_L - V_R) + (V_F - V_L)$$

**Step 2:** The $V_L$ terms cancel:

$$V_I + V_{III} = -V_R + V_F$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the right-hand side as the definition of $V_{II}$:

$$V_I + V_{III} = V_F - V_R = V_{II}$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}}$.  $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation that $V_R + V_L + V_F = 0$ (Centroid Reference)

**Step 1:** By definition, the centroid of the equilateral triangle is the **arithmetic mean** of the three vertex potentials when referenced to the centroid itself:

$$V_{\text{centroid}} = \frac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Since the centroid is the *electrical reference* of the body (cardiac dipole origin), $V_{\text{centroid}} = 0$.

**Step 3:** Therefore $V_R + V_L + V_F = 3 \cdot 0 = 0$.

This is mathematically equivalent to Einthoven's law.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Augmented Lead Equations

We derive $V_{aVR}$ in detail; the others are analogous.

**Setup:** For aVR, the **exploring electrode is RA** and the **reference is the average of LA and LL potentials** (Goldberger's modified central terminal):

$$V_{\text{ref, aVR}} = \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$$

**Step 1:** The unipolar potential (before augmentation) is:

$$V_{aVR}^{\text{unipolar}} = V_R - V_{\text{ref, aVR}} = V_R - \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$$

**Step 2:** The Goldberger reference is the average of only **two** electrodes, not three. This means it is **lower in magnitude** than the true WCT (which divides by 3). The ratio is $3/2 = 1.5$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the augmented lead voltage is **1.5 times** the unipolar voltage (to compensate for the smaller reference and recover the same signal level that Wilson's central terminal would have given):

$$V_{aVR} = 1.5 \cdot \left(V_R - \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}\right)$$

But the standard ECG convention is to *not* multiply by 1.5 explicitly — instead, the **augmentation is built into the amplifier gain** so that the *displayed* aVR value is what the unipolar measurement would have been had the reference been at true ground.

**Final display equation (what the ECG records):**

$$V_{aVR} = V_R - \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$$

By cyclic permutation:

$$V_{aVL} = V_L - \frac{V_R + V_F}{2}, \qquad V_{aVF} = V_F - \frac{V_R + V_L}{2}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Lead Vector Angles

Consider the equilateral triangle with the centroid at the origin. The three vertices are at angles:

$$\theta_{RA} = +90° + 180°/3 \text{ offset (depending on convention)}, \quad \theta_{LA}, \quad \theta_{LL}$$

A common convention places the triangle with the **RA at the top, LA at the lower-left, and LL at the lower-right**, oriented with the horizontal axis being Lead I. Under this convention:

- The midpoint of the **RA–LA side** is at angle **+60°** from the centroid (this is the **Lead II axis**).
- The midpoint of the **RA–LL side** is at angle **+120°** (this is the **Lead III axis**).
- The midpoint of the **LA–LL side** is at angle **0°** (this is the **Lead I axis**).

Derivation: the **lead vector** for a bipolar lead points from the (−) electrode to the (+) electrode, through the centroid. For Lead I (LA positive, RA negative):

$$\hat{u}_{I} = \frac{\vec{r}_{LA} - \vec{r}_{RA}}{\vert \vec{r}_{LA} - \vec{r}_{RA} \vert}$$

With the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $a$ at:

$$\vec{r}_{RA} = \left(0, +\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\right), \quad \vec{r}_{LA} = \left(-\frac{a}{2}, -\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}\right), \quad \vec{r}_{LL} = \left(+\frac{a}{2}, -\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

Compute Lead I direction:

$$\vec{r}_{LA} - \vec{r}_{RA} = \left(-\frac{a}{2}, -\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(-\frac{a}{2}, -\frac{3a}{2\sqrt{3}}\right) = \left(-\frac{a}{2}, -\frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

The angle:

$$\theta_{I} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-a\sqrt{3}/2}{-a/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{3}) = 60° \text{ in 3rd quadrant} = 180° + 60° = 240°$$

But the *physiological* Lead I axis is taken as **0°** (pointing from RA→LA, i.e., to the patient's left). Subtracting 240° by rotating the coordinate frame by 240° gives the standard convention where Lead I = 0°.

Similarly the *augmented* lead axes bisect the angles between the bipolar leads, e.g.:

$$\theta_{aVR} = \frac{\theta_{I} + \theta_{II} + 180°}{3} = -150°$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Mean Electrical Axis (MEA) from Two Leads

**Given:** Net QRS area in Lead I = $A_I$, net QRS area in Lead III = $A_{III}$ (or in aVF as an alternative).

**Step 1:** The cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ has components along the two perpendicular axes (I and aVF):

$$M_{I} = A_I, \qquad M_{aVF} = A_{III} \cdot \cos(30°) = A_{III}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text{ (projection of III onto aVF)}$$

A simpler textbook approach: use the **hexaxial reference system** and read the axis directly.

**Step 2 (Hexaxial method):** The lead that records the **maximum positive QRS deflection** lies *closest* to the cardiac axis. The perpendicular lead to it records a **biphasic** (zero-net-area) QRS.

**Step 3 (Algebraic method using I and III):** The MEA $\theta_M$ measured from Lead I axis (0°) is:

$$\theta_M = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A_{III}\sin 60° + A_{I}\sin 0°}{A_{I}\cos 0° + A_{III}\cos 60°}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A_{III}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{A_{I} + A_{III}\cdot\frac{1}{2}}\right)$$

**Worked numerical example:**

Let $A_I = +25$ mm·s, $A_{III} = +10$ mm·s (net positive QRS areas).

$$\text{Numerator} = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 10 \cdot 0.866 = 8.66$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 25 + 10 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 25 + 5 = 30$$

$$\theta_M = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{8.66}{30}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.2887) = +16.1°$$

**Result:** Normal axis (within −30° to +105°). $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Derivation of the Augmented Lead Sum Identity

**Goal:** Show that $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$.

**Step 1:** Sum the three expressions:

$$V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = \left(V_R - \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}\right) + \left(V_L - \frac{V_R + V_F}{2}\right) + \left(V_F - \frac{V_R + V_L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Group terms:

$$= (V_R + V_L + V_F) - \left(\frac{V_L + V_F}{2} + \frac{V_R + V_F}{2} + \frac{V_R + V_L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The second bracket is:

$$\frac{(V_L + V_F) + (V_R + V_F) + (V_R + V_L)}{2} = \frac{2(V_R + V_L + V_F)}{2} = V_R + V_L + V_F$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = (V_R + V_L + V_F) - (V_R + V_L + V_F) = 0$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0}$.  $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Einthoven's Triangle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below renders the **electrical interconnection architecture** of the 12-lead ECG system, with the equilateral-triangle geometry mapped onto a flow graph.

```mermaid
graph TB
    heartC[Cardiac Dipole at Triangle Centroid]
    eRA[Electrode RA - Right Arm]
    eLA[Electrode LA - Left Arm]
    eLL[Electrode LL - Left Leg]
    eRL[Electrode RL - Right Leg - DRL Ground]

    heartC --> eRA
    heartC --> eLA
    heartC --> eLL

    eRA --> diffI[Subtractor V_L minus V_R]
    eLA --> diffI
    diffI --> lead1[LEAD I - Axis 0 degrees]

    eRA --> diffII[Subtractor V_F minus V_R]
    eLL --> diffII
    diffII --> lead2[LEAD II - Axis plus 60 degrees]

    eLA --> diffIII[Subtractor V_F minus V_L]
    eLL --> diffIII
    diffIII --> lead3[LEAD III - Axis plus 120 degrees]

    eRA --> refVR[Reference Average of LA plus LL]
    eLA --> refVR
    eLL --> refVR
    eRA --> avr[Augmented Lead aVR - Axis minus 150 degrees]
    refVR --> avr

    eLA --> refVL[Reference Average of RA plus LL]
    eRA --> refVL
    eLL --> refVL
    eLA --> avl[Augmented Lead aVL - Axis minus 30 degrees]
    refVL --> avl

    eLL --> refVF[Reference Average of RA plus LA]
    eRA --> refVF
    eLA --> refVF
    eLL --> avf[Augmented Lead aVF - Axis plus 90 degrees]
    refVF --> avf

    eRA --> wct[Wilson Central Terminal - V_R plus V_L plus V_F divided by 3]
    eLA --> wct
    eLL --> wct

    wct --> precordial[Precordial Leads V1 through V6 - Chest Electrodes]

    eRL --> drl[Driven Right Leg Circuit - Active Grounding]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The cardiac dipole (top center) is the *source* feeding potentials to the three limb electrodes. The lower blocks form three parallel signal-processing chains: the **bipolar chain** (3 leads), the **augmented chain** (3 leads), and the **precordial chain** (6 leads from the chest). The right-leg electrode is isolated to a separate **DRL** (driven-right-leg) safety/grounding circuit and does *not* appear in the triangle.

## 4.2 The Frontal Plane Hexaxial Reference System

The three bipolar leads and three augmented leads together form a **hexaxial reference system** — six axes at 30° spacing in the frontal plane, used to read the cardiac vector's mean axis.

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisOrder[Frontal Plane Hexaxial Reference System]
    ax1[aVL: minus 30 degrees]
    ax2[Lead I: 0 degrees]
    ax3[aVR: minus 150 degrees]
    ax4[Lead II: plus 60 degrees]
    ax5[aVF: plus 90 degrees]
    ax6[Lead III: plus 120 degrees]
    axisOrder --> ax1
    axisOrder --> ax2
    axisOrder --> ax3
    axisOrder --> ax4
    axisOrder --> ax5
    axisOrder --> ax6
```

| Lead | Frontal Axis Angle |
|---|---|
| aVL | −30° |
| Lead I | 0° |
| aVR | −150° |
| Lead II | +60° |
| aVF | +90° |
| Lead III | +120° |

> All six axes are **equally spaced at 60° intervals**, with the augmented axes bisecting the gaps between the bipolar axes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Einthoven's triangle. State Einthoven's law with its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Einthoven's triangle is an equilateral triangle whose vertices represent the three standard limb electrode positions — **Right Arm (RA)**, **Left Arm (LA)**, and **Left Leg (LL)** — with the **heart assumed to lie at the electrical centroid** of the triangle. The triangle is used to model the body's electrical field as a homogeneous volume conductor so that the cardiac dipole vector at the center can be projected onto the sides, giving the three bipolar limb leads.

**Einthoven's Law (1 mark):**

$$V_I + V_{III} = V_{II} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad V_R + V_L + V_F = 0$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Wilson Central Terminal? Write its mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)** is a reference point used for the unipolar (chest) leads in the ECG. It is formed by connecting the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) through equal high-value resistors (typically 5 kΩ each) to a common node, producing a near-zero potential that acts as a **virtual ground** during the cardiac cycle.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$V_W = \frac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3} \approx 0 \text{ V}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** The WCT serves as the **indifferent (reference) electrode** for the six precordial chest leads V1–V6, allowing each chest electrode to record the *unipolar* cardiac potential relative to this stable reference.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive **Einthoven's law** $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ starting from the lead definitions. Show also that $V_R + V_L + V_F = 0$ for a centroid-referenced system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **augmented unipolar limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF)** with their derivation, reference configuration, and angular placement in the frontal plane. Compare them with the standard bipolar limb leads. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Einthoven's Law Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the three bipolar lead potentials:**

$$V_I = V_L - V_R, \qquad V_{II} = V_F - V_R, \qquad V_{III} = V_F - V_L$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Add $V_I$ and $V_{III}$:**

$$V_I + V_{III} = (V_L - V_R) + (V_F - V_L) = -V_R + V_F$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Recognize the right-hand side as $V_{II}$:**

$$V_I + V_{III} = V_F - V_R = V_{II} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Derive the centroid reference identity:**

The centroid of the equilateral triangle is the arithmetic mean of the three vertex potentials:

$$V_{\text{centroid}} = \frac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3}$$

Since the centroid is taken as the *reference potential* (cardiac dipole origin), $V_{\text{centroid}} = 0$, hence:

$$V_R + V_L + V_F = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Note equivalence:** The two identities are mathematically equivalent; the latter is the *node-voltage* form and the former is the *branch-voltage* form of the same conservation law.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Concept:** The augmented leads are **unipolar** measurements where the *exploring* electrode is one limb and the *reference* is the average of the *other two* limbs (Goldberger modification, 1942). They provide a better signal-to-noise ratio and are placed in the gaps between the bipolar lead axes.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Derivation of aVR (representative):**

The reference for aVR is the average of LA and LL potentials:

$$V_{\text{ref,aVR}} = \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$$

The unipolar lead reading is:

$$V_{aVR}^{\text{unipolar}} = V_R - \frac{V_L + V_F}{2}$$

The **augmentation factor of 1.5** compensates for the reduced reference (only 2 electrodes averaged rather than 3 in the WCT), and is built into the ECG amplifier gain. By cyclic permutation:

$$V_{aVL} = V_L - \frac{V_R + V_F}{2}, \qquad V_{aVF} = V_F - \frac{V_R + V_L}{2}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Angular placement:** The augmented lead axes bisect the angles between the bipolar lead axes:

- aVR at **−150°** (bisects Lead I at 0° and Lead II at 60° on the opposite side)
- aVL at **−30°** (bisects Lead I at 0° and Lead III at 120° on the upper side)
- aVF at **+90°** (bisects Lead II at 60° and Lead III at 120°)

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Comparison with bipolar leads:**

| Aspect | Bipolar (I, II, III) | Augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | One limb electrode | Average of two other limbs |
| Polarity | Bipolar (true differential) | Unipolar (with augmented gain) |
| Signal amplitude | Smaller | ~50% larger (1.5×) |
| Angular placement | 0°, +60°, +120° | −150°, −30°, +90° |
| Inventor | Einthoven | Goldberger |

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Identity:** $V_{aVR} + V_{aVL} + V_{aVF} = 0$ (same KVL property as Einthoven's law).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, describe the architecture of the **standard 12-lead ECG system** based on Einthoven's triangle. Explain the role of the Wilson Central Terminal and the Driven Right Leg circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **mean electrical axis of the heart**. Given a net QRS area of **+30 mm² in Lead I** and **+15 mm² in Lead III**, calculate the mean electrical axis. Comment on whether the axis is normal. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 12-Lead ECG Architecture [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Block diagram and architecture:** The 12-lead ECG is built from **10 electrodes**:
- **3 limb electrodes** (RA, LA, LL) form Einthoven's triangle.
- **1 right-leg electrode (RL)** forms the driven-right-leg (DRL) ground.
- **6 chest electrodes** (V1–V6) form the precordial leads.

The 12 leads are:
- **Bipolar limb leads (I, II, III):** direct voltage differences between pairs of limb electrodes.
- **Augmented limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF):** unipolar measurements referenced to the *average* of the two non-exploring limbs, with **1.5× augmentation** built into the amplifier.
- **Precordial leads (V1–V6):** unipolar chest measurements referenced to the **Wilson Central Terminal (WCT)**.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Wilson Central Terminal (WCT):** The WCT is the common node formed by connecting the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) through three equal high-value resistors (~5 kΩ) to a single reference point. Its potential is:

$$V_W = \frac{V_R + V_L + V_F}{3} \approx 0 \text{ V}$$

It acts as a **stable virtual ground** for the chest leads because, by Einthoven's law, the *average* of the three limb potentials is zero. This means the chest electrode only measures the *true local* cardiac potential without contamination from the limb electrode positions.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit:** The RL electrode is the *safety ground* and is *not* part of the triangle. The DRL circuit:
- Senses the **common-mode voltage** (e.g., 50/60 Hz mains interference picked up by the body).
- **Inverts and feeds it back** to the body through the RL electrode.
