# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define EEG. List the five standard frequency bands with their frequency ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Electroencephalogram (EEG) is a non-invasive electrophysiological recording of the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain, captured via surface electrodes placed on the scalp, representing the summated post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons.

**Five Frequency Bands (2 Marks — 0.4 each):**

| Band | Frequency |
|------|-----------|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz |
| Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz |
| Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz |
| Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is Ag/AgCl preferred over pure silver or stainless steel as an EEG electrode material?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Ag/AgCl is preferred because it forms a **non-polarizable, reversible** electrode interface. The half-cell potential is **stable, low, and reproducible** because the Ag/AgCl half-cell depends only on the chloride ion concentration (governed by the Nernst equation). When two Ag/AgCl electrodes are used in a differential pair, their nearly identical half-cell potentials cancel out in the differential amplifier, eliminating DC offset drift. Pure Ag polarizes rapidly (charge accumulates), and stainless steel has an unstable, drifting half-cell potential, making both unsuitable for low-amplitude microvolt EEG signals.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the biophysical origin of the EEG signal with a neat block diagram. Discuss why synchronization and parallel orientation of pyramidal neurons are essential. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the minimum CMRR (in dB) required for an EEG amplifier to suppress 50 Hz line noise of 3 mV amplitude to within 5% of a 30 µV EEG signal. Assume differential gain $A_{d} = 5000$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Cellular Origin (2 Marks):**
The EEG signal originates from the **post-synaptic potentials (PSPs)** — primarily excitatory (EPSPs) and inhibitory (IPSPs) — generated at the **dendrites** of **pyramidal neurons** in the **cerebral cortex (layers III, V, VI)**. Individual action potentials are too brief (~1 ms) and too small to be detected at the scalp.

**Step 2 — Spatial Summation Requirement (2 Marks):**
For a scalp-detectable signal (~10–100 µV), three conditions are necessary:
- **Parallel orientation:** Pyramidal neurons are aligned perpendicularly to the cortical surface, allowing their dipoles to add constructively.
- **Synchronous firing:** Thousands of neurons must fire in phase; asynchronous activity averages to zero.
- **Open field geometry:** Dendrites extend in a single direction, creating a net extracellular dipole.

**Step 3 — Volume Conduction (1.5 Marks):**
The signal propagates through the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), skull (which attenuates and smears the signal), and scalp to reach the surface electrode. The skull's high resistivity (~80 Ω·m vs 0.65 Ω·m for scalp) limits high-frequency resolution and creates spatial smearing.

**Step 4 — Transduction and Amplification (1.5 Marks):**
Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes transduce ionic current into electronic current. A differential amplifier (CMRR ≥ 100 dB) extracts the biopotential difference (typically 10–100 µV) and amplifies it to volt levels for ADC and display.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — State the constraint (1 Mark):**
Residual noise ≤ 5% of signal:
$$A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} \leq 0.05 \times V_{signal}$$

**Step 2 — Express $A_{cm}$ in terms of CMRR (1.5 Marks):**
$$A_{cm} = \frac{A_{d}}{\text{CMRR}_{linear}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for CMRR (2 Marks):**
$$\text{CMRR}_{linear} \geq \frac{A_{d} \cdot V_{cm}}{0.05 \times V_{signal}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (1.5 Marks):**
$$\text{CMRR}_{linear} \geq \frac{5000 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{linear} \geq \frac{15}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{7}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB (1 Mark):**
$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10^{7}) = 20 \times 7 = 140 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum required CMRR} = 140 \text{ dB}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the International 10-20 electrode placement system with a labeled diagram. Explain the naming convention (odd/even numbers, "z" suffix). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete EEG signal conditioning chain. Justify the choice of bandpass filter range (0.1 – 100 Hz) and the need for a 50 Hz notch filter. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Anatomical Landmarks (1.5 Marks):**
The 10-20 system uses four reference points: **nasion** (front), **inion** (back), and **left and right pre-auricular points** (ears). The total head circumference is divided into proportional **10% and 20%** segments.

**Step 2 — Electrode Placement Grid (2.5 Marks):**
- **Frontal Pole:** Fp1, Fp2 (10% from nasion)
- **Frontal:** F3, F4, Fz, F7, F8
- **Central:** C3, C4, Cz (over the motor cortex)
- **Temporal:** T3, T4, T5, T6
- **Parietal:** P3, P4, Pz
- **Occipital:** O1, O2, Oz

**Step 3 — Naming Convention (2 Marks):**
- **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7)** = **LEFT hemisphere**
- **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8)** = **RIGHT hemisphere**
- **"z" suffix** = **MIDLINE** (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz)
- **"A" prefix (A1, A2)** = **AURICULAR (earlobe) reference electrodes**
- **"Fpz"** = **Ground electrode location**

**Step 4 — Clinical Significance (1 Mark):**
The 10-20 system ensures **reproducibility** across patients and laboratories, enabling longitudinal comparison and standardized clinical reporting.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram of Signal Conditioning Chain (3 Marks):**

`Electrode → Preamplifier (Instrumentation Amp) → HPF (0.1 Hz) → Main Amplifier (Gain 1000–10000) → LPF (100 Hz) → 50 Hz Notch → ADC → DSP → Display`

**Step 2 — Justification of High-Pass Filter at 0.1 Hz (1.5 Marks):**
The 0.1 Hz high-pass filter removes **DC electrode offset** and very slow baseline drifts caused by skin polarization and slow sweat-related potential changes. Frequencies below 0.1 Hz are not of neurological origin and would otherwise saturate the amplifier.

**Step 3 — Justification of Low-Pass Filter at 100 Hz (1.5 Marks):**
The 100 Hz low-pass filter limits the bandwidth to the **diagnostically relevant EEG range** (delta through gamma), removes high-frequency EMG (muscle) artifacts, and satisfies the **Nyquist criterion** for the chosen sampling rate.

**Step 4 — Justification of 50 Hz Notch Filter (1 Mark):**
The 50 Hz notch filter (very narrow band-reject filter with Q-factor ≥ 30) suppresses **50 Hz power-line interference** that is capacitively and inductively coupled into the electrode leads. The notch width is typically ±2 Hz around 50 Hz to preserve nearby EEG content (e.g., high-beta at 30 Hz and harmonic frequencies).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — EEG Questions:**
> 1. **CMRR trap:** Students write "CMRR = 100 dB" without showing the **20 log** conversion step. **Always show the linear-to-dB conversion explicitly.**
> 2. **Band range trap:** Some students write "alpha = 8–12 Hz" instead of the IFCN standard **8–13 Hz**. **Memorize the official range.**
> 3. **Electrode naming trap:** Do NOT write "Fp1 = frontal pole 1 = right side." It is **LEFT side** (odd). **This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if confused.**
> 4. **Signal amplitude trap:** EEG is **microvolts (µV)**, not millivolts (mV). State the unit **every time**.
> 5. **10-20 trap:** The "10" and "20" refer to **percentages of head dimensions**, not millimetres or centimetres.
> 6. **Notch filter trap:** A notch filter removes **only 50/60 Hz**; it does NOT replace proper grounding and shielding.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision before the exam.**

- ✅ EEG = **Electroencephalogram** = brain's summed post-synaptic potentials recorded at scalp.
- ✅ Origin = **dendritic PSPs of cortical pyramidal neurons** (NOT action potentials).
- ✅ Three requirements for scalp EEG: **parallel orientation, synchronous firing, volume conduction**.
- ✅ Amplitude = **10 – 100 µV**; Frequency = **0.1 – 100 Hz**.
- ✅ Five bands: **Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30) Hz**.
- ✅ **Alpha = posterior dominant rhythm (PDR), eyes closed, occipital.**
- ✅ Standard electrode = **Ag/AgCl** (non-polarizable, Nernstian, reversible).
- ✅ **CMRR ≥ 100 dB clinical, ≥ 140 dB for high-noise environments.**
- ✅ Input impedance ≥ **100× source impedance** (in practice **≥ 10 GΩ**).
- ✅ Sampling rate: **256 Hz (clinical) / 512 Hz (research) / 1024 Hz (high-fidelity)**.
- ✅ Filter chain: **HPF 0.1 Hz → Gain → LPF 100 Hz → 50 Hz Notch → ADC**.
- ✅ 10-20 system: **Odd = Left, Even = Right, "z" = Midline, "A" = Auricular reference**.
- ✅ Fp1 (left frontal pole) / Fp2 (right frontal pole) / Fpz (ground).
- ✅ **C3, C4 = motor cortex** (used for BCI and motor-imagery).
- ✅ **O1, O2 = visual cortex** (alpha rhythm dominant).
- ✅ Montages: **Bipolar (chain), Referential (vs common ref), Average reference**.
- ✅ Differential amplifier equation: $V_{out} = A_{d}(V_{1} - V_{2}) + A_{cm}V_{cm}$.
- ✅ CMRR formula: $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_{d}/A_{cm} \vert)$.
- ✅ Clinical uses: **epilepsy, sleep staging, BCI, anesthesia depth, ERPs**.
- ✅ Nernst equation governs Ag/AgCl half-cell potential; identical electrodes cancel in differential mode.
- ✅ **P300 ERP** = positive deflection ~300 ms after rare target stimulus.
- ✅ Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit actively reduces common-mode voltage at patient body.
- ✅ **NEVER confuse EEG (µV) with ECG (mV) — examiners test this directly.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brain Action Potentials and Characteristics of Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Brain Action Potentials and Characteristics of Signal # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Brain Action Potential — Formal Definition A **brain action potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none electrochemical wave of depolarization that propagates along the membrane of an excitable cell (neuron) in the central nervous system. It is generated by the coordinated opening and closing of voltage-gated sodium (Na⁺) and potassium (K⁺) ion channels embedded in the neuronal lipid bilayer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > The action potential is the fundamental unit of information transfer in the nervous system. Every thought, sensation, and motor command is encoded as a temporally-modulated train of action potentials. From a biosensor perspective, understanding APs is critical because **electroencephalography (EEG), electrocorticography (ECoG), and neural implants** all depend on detecting these extracellular field potentials. Mathematically, the action potential is a transient excursion of the transmembrane potential $V_m$ from its resting value of approximately **−70 mV** to a peak of approximately **+30 mV**, and back, over a duration of **1–2 milliseconds (ms)**. > [!NOTE] > **Resting Membrane Potential Definition** > The resting membrane potential ($V_{rest}$) is the steady-state voltage difference between the intracellular fluid (ICF) and the extracellular fluid (ECF) of a neuron when no stimulus is applied. Standard accepted value: $V_{rest} \approx -70$ mV (inside negative relative to outside). ## 1.2 Characteristics of Signal — Formal Definition A **biomedical signal** is a measurable electrical, chemical, mechanical, or thermal quantity that carries information about a physiological process. The **characteristics of a signal** are the descriptive parameters (amplitude, frequency, phase, signal-to-noise ratio, bandwidth) that define its behavior in the time and frequency domains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > In Module 2, the *characteristics of signal* specifically refers to neural and biosensor signals. Students must learn the difference between **excitatory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs)**, **inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (IPSPs)**, **action potentials**, and the derived composite signals like the **EEG**. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Domino Voltage Wave" Imagine a long row of dominoes standing in a dark corridor. The first domino represents the **axon hillock** of a neuron (the trigger zone). Each domino has a small weight holding it in place — this is the **resting membrane potential** maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**. When a stimulus strong enough to push the first domino overcomes this weight (the **threshold**, typically around **−55 mV**), it falls. As it falls, it knocks over the next domino. This knocking action represents the **local current flow** that depolarizes the adjacent membrane segment. | Biological Component | Domino Analogy | |---|---| | Resting potential | Small weight holding each domino upright | | Threshold stimulus | Push strong enough to topple the first domino | | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels | A hinged joint that opens only when pushed | | Local circuit currents | The physical impact between adjacent dominoes | | Refractory period | A delay timer that prevents the domino from being re-toppled immediately | > [!TIP] > **Real-world engineering parallel** — The action potential behaves like a **regenerative digital pulse** propagating down a transmission line. Like a digital signal in a CMOS inverter chain, it is "reshaped" at every node (axonal segment) so that it does not attenuate over distance. This is a critical concept for **neural prosthetics** and **brain-computer interface (BCI)** design. ## 1.4 The Three Pillar Equations (Definitions) **Pillar 1 — Nernst Equation:** The equilibrium potential of a single permeant ion. $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$ **Pillar 2 — Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation:** The steady-state membrane potential considering multiple permeant ions. $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$ **Pillar 3 — Cable Equation (1D):** Describes how voltage spreads passively along a nerve fiber. $$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = r_m \cdot c_m \cdot \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + r_m \cdot i$$ where $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length, $c_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit length, $i$ = injected current. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Ionic Basis of the Resting Potential The neuronal membrane is a **phospholipid bilayer** (~5 nm thick) that is selectively permeable. At rest, the membrane is **~25× more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺. The asymmetric distribution of ions is maintained by: 1. **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** — actively pumps **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** per ATP hydrolyzed. 2. **K⁺ leak channels** — allow passive K⁺ efflux down its concentration gradient. 3. **Large anionic proteins** (e.g., albumin, phosphate esters) trapped inside the cell. Standard concentration values for a mammalian neuron at **37 °C (310 K)**: | Ion | Intracellular [mM] | Extracellular [mM] | Nernst Potential [mV] | |---|---|---|---| | K⁺ | 140 | 5 | −92 | | Na⁺ | 12 | 145 | +61 | | Cl⁻ | 4 | 120 | −91 | | Ca²⁺ | 0.0001 | 1.2 | +136 | ## 2.2 Phases of the Action Potential — Step-by-Step Logic The AP is conventionally divided into **five phases**. Each phase corresponds to a specific change in membrane permeability driven by voltage-gated ion channels. **Phase 0 — Depolarization (Rapid Upswing)** - Stimulus drives $V_m$ from −70 mV toward **threshold (−55 mV)**. - Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels** open rapidly. - Na⁺ rushes into the cell down its electrochemical gradient. - $V_m$ shoots from −70 mV to +30 mV in **~0.5 ms**. **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization (Brief Dip)** - A sub-population of Na⁺ channels **inactivates**. - Some **transient outward K⁺ current (I_A)** activates. - Brief hyperpolarizing dip or plateau (more prominent in some neurons). **Phase 2 — Repolarization (Downstroke)** - Voltage-gated **K⁺ channels** (delayed rectifier) fully open. - K⁺ efflux drives $V_m$ back toward $E_K$ (−92 mV). - Na⁺ channels remain inactivated. **Phase 3 — After-hyperpolarization (Undershoot)** - K⁺ channels are slow to close. - $V_m$ briefly dips below resting potential (toward −80 mV). - This is the **relative refractory period** zone. **Phase 4 — Return to Rest** - K⁺ channels close. - Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and leak channels restore ionic gradients. - $V_m$ returns to −70 mV. > [!NOTE] > **Threshold and the "All-or-None" Principle** > If the stimulus depolarization does not reach the **threshold potential of −55 mV**, no action potential is generated — only a graded **local (electrotonic) potential**. If threshold IS reached, a full-amplitude AP is produced, regardless of stimulus strength. This is the **all-or-none principle**, analogous to a digital 0/1 bit. ## 2.3 The Hodgkin-Huxley Formalism (Simplified) Hodgkin and Huxley (1952) modeled the AP using four coupled ODEs: $$I = C_m \frac{dV}{dt} + g_K n^4 (V - E_K) + g_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na}) + g_L (V - E_L)$$ where: - $m$ = Na⁺ activation gate variable - $h$ = Na⁺ inactivation gate variable - $n$ = K⁺ activation gate variable - $g_{ion}$ = maximum conductance for that ion - $g_L$ = leak conductance - $E_L$ = leak reversal potential This 1963 **Nobel Prize-winning work** is the foundation of modern computational neuroscience. ## 2.4 Refractory Periods | Period | Duration | Mechanism | Physiological Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Absolute refractory | ~1 ms | Na⁺ channels inactivated; no stimulus, no matter how strong, can trigger AP | Sets the **upper frequency limit** of firing | | Relative refractory | ~2–5 ms | K⁺ channels still open; requires stronger-than-normal stimulus | Limits firing rate, enforces unidirectional propagation | Maximum theoretical firing rate ≈ **1000 Hz** (1 ms AP + 0 ms absolute refractory). ## 2.5 Signal Characteristics — Engineering Parameters In biosignal analysis, the action potential is described by several measurable characteristics: **Amplitude:** Peak-to-peak voltage of the AP. Typical intracellular amplitude = **100 mV** (from −70 to +30 mV). Extracellular AP amplitude = **50–500 µV** (this is what EEG/ECoG electrodes detect). **Duration:** Time from threshold crossing to return to rest. **1–2 ms** for most mammalian neurons. **Rise Time:** Time from 10% to 90% of peak amplitude. **~0.2–0.5 ms**. **Conduction Velocity:** Speed of AP propagation along the axon. Depends on axon diameter and myelination. $$v \propto d \quad \text{(for unmyelinated axons)}$$ $$v \approx 6 \cdot d \quad \text{(for myelinated axons, m/s, d in µm)}$$ Range: **0.5 m/s** (small unmyelinated C-fibers) to **120 m/s** (large myelinated Aα fibers). **Frequency Content:** A single AP contains frequency components from **DC to ~3 kHz**, with most energy in the **100 Hz–2 kHz** band. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** $$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering utility in biosensors** — The SNR is the defining parameter of every neural recording electrode. State-of-the-art **microelectrode arrays (MEAs)** achieve SNRs of 20–40 dB for single-unit recordings. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table) | # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst equilibrium potential for a single ion | Volts [V] | | 2 | $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst at 37 °C (physiological) | mV | | 3 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz voltage equation | Volts [V] | | 4 | $I = C_m \frac{dV}{dt} + \sum I_{ion}$ | Membrane current = capacitive + ionic | Amperes | | 5 | $i = \frac{V_0}{R_m} e^{-x/\lambda}$ | Passive (electrotonic) voltage spread along axon | Amps [A] | | 6 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Length constant of the axon | meters [m] | | 7 | $\tau_m = r_m \cdot c_m$ | Membrane time constant | seconds [s] | | 8 | $v = 6 \cdot d$ | Conduction velocity in myelinated axon (Hursh) | m/s, d in µm | | 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | | 10 | $f_{max} = 1/T_{ARP}$ | Maximum firing frequency | Hz | **Constants:** - **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K) — Universal gas constant - **F** = 96,485 C/mol — Faraday's constant - **T** = 310 K at 37 °C (physiological temperature) - **RT/F** at 37 °C = **26.73 mV** > [!WARNING] > **Common sign convention pitfall:** The Nernst equation gives the *equilibrium* potential of the ion channel (the voltage at which there is no net flow). The *driving force* on the ion is $V_m - E_{ion}$. The direction of ion flow depends on the SIGN of this difference. Always double-check signs in numerical problems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (From First Principles) The Nernst equation arises from equating the **chemical (osmotic) work** required to move an ion against its concentration gradient with the **electrical work** performed by the membrane potential. **Step 1 — Chemical Work (Free Energy of Diffusion):** Consider moving 1 mole of ions from concentration $C_1$ to concentration $C_2$. The Gibbs free energy change is: $$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{C_2}{C_1}\right)$$ For our neuron, moving an ion from outside ($[ion]_{out}$) to inside ($[ion]_{in}$): $$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Electrical Work:** Moving 1 mole of a monovalent ion (charge $zF$ per mole) across a potential difference $E$ requires: $$\Delta G_{elec} = zFE$$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition:** At the Nernst potential, there is no net flux, so $\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$. $$RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) + zFE_{ion} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $E_{ion}$:** $$zFE_{ion} = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$ $$zFE_{ion} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Conversion to log₁₀ and substitution of constants at T = 310 K:** $$E_{ion} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{z(96485)} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ [mV] at 37 °C}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the most frequently used form in KTU numerical problems. The factor 61.5 mV comes from $(RT/F) \cdot \ln(10) \approx 26.73 \times 2.303 \approx 61.54$ mV. ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺ **Problem:** A mammalian neuron has $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM at 37 °C. Calculate the Nernst potential for K⁺. **Solution:** $$E_{K^+} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) \text{ mV}$$ $$E_{K^+} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03571) \text{ mV}$$ $$\log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.4472$$ $$E_{K^+} = 61.5 \times (-1.4472) = -89.00 \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{E_{K^+} \approx -89 \text{ mV}}$$ This matches the standard textbook value of **−92 mV** (small differences arise from temperature assumptions and use of activity vs. concentration). ## 3.3 Derivation of the Cable Equation (1D Passive Axon) Consider a small cylindrical axonal segment of length $\Delta x$ and radius $a$. Define: - $r_i$ = intracellular axial resistance per unit length [Ω/m] - $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length [Ω·m] - $c_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit length [F/m] - $v(x,t)$ = intracellular potential at position $x$, time $t$ **Step 1 — KCL at node x:** Current entering from the left = current leaving to the right + current leaking across the membrane + current charging the membrane. $$-\frac{\partial i}{\partial x} = i_m + i_c = \frac{v}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ where $i$ is the axial current. **Step 2 — Axial current is related to voltage gradient:** $$i = -\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and differentiate:** $$-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{v}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ $$\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = \frac{v}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard form:** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = r_i r_m c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + r_i r_m \cdot i_{ext}}$$ Or, defining the **length constant** $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ and **time constant** $\tau = r_m c_m$: $$\lambda^2 \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} - v = \tau \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ ## 3.4 Steady-State Solution of the Cable Equation In the steady state ($\partial v / \partial t = 0$), the equation reduces to: $$\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = \frac{v}{\lambda^2}$$ The general solution is: $$v(x) = A e^{-x/\lambda} + B e^{+x/\lambda}$$ For a semi-infinite cable (axons are effectively infinite compared to $\lambda$), $B = 0$ (otherwise voltage would blow up at $x \to \infty$). $$\boxed{v(x) = v_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}}$$ The voltage decays **exponentially** along the axon with characteristic length $\lambda$. For a typical unmyelinated axon: $\lambda \approx 1$ mm. For a myelinated axon: $\lambda \approx 10$ mm. > [!TIP] > **Engineering insight:** The length constant is the **biological analog of the skin depth** in electromagnetic wave propagation through a conductor. The time constant $\tau$ governs how fast the membrane can follow a step change in voltage — analogous to the RC time constant of an electronic low-pass filter. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Signal Characteristic vs. Biosensor Application | Signal Characteristic | Definition | Typical Value (Neural) | Biosensor / BCI Application | |---|---|---|---| | Amplitude | Peak voltage | 50–500 µV (extracellular) | Determines required preamp gain | | Frequency | Oscillation rate | 0.5–3 kHz | Sets ADC sampling rate (Nyquist ≥ 6 kHz) | | Bandwidth | Frequency range | 100 Hz – 2 kHz | Low-pass filter cutoff design | | SNR | Signal/Noise power | 20–40 dB | Determines recording quality | | Phase | Timing relative to ref | variable | Used in spike sorting, LFP alignment | | Rise time | 10–90% edge | 0.2 ms | Detector bandwidth requirement | | Refractory period | Dead time after AP | 1–2 ms | Sets unit separation limit | | Conduction velocity | Propagation speed | 1–120 m/s | Used in nerve conduction studies | ## 3.6 Derivation of Maximum Theoretical Firing Frequency The absolute refractory period (ARP) sets the maximum rate at which a neuron can fire. If the AP itself has duration $T_{AP}$ and the ARP adds dead time $T_{ARP}$: $$T_{cycle, min} = T_{AP} + T_{ARP}$$ $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle, min}}$$ For a typical neuron: $T_{AP} \approx 1$ ms, $T_{ARP} \approx 1$ ms. $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$ In practice, the relative refractory period and metabolic constraints reduce this to **~200–300 Hz** maximum sustainable firing rate. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Action Potential Generation Pipeline This diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an action potential from stimulus to propagation, in block-diagram form suitable for direct study. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[STIMULUS INPUT
Mechanical Chemical Electrical] --> B[DENDRITIC RECEPTION
Graded Potential] B --> C[AXON HILLOCK
Integration Zone] C --> D{V_m vs Threshold
55 mV Check} D -->|Below Threshold| E[NO ACTION POTENTIAL
Decay Electrotonically] D -->|At or Above Threshold| F[VOLTAGE GATED NA PLUS CHANNELS OPEN] F --> G[RAPID DEPOLARIZATION
V_m rises to plus 30 mV] G --> H[NA PLUS INACTIVATION
K PLUS CHANNELS OPEN] H --> I[REPOLARIZATION
V_m falls toward minus 90 mV] I --> J[AFTER HYPERPOLARIZATION
Undershoot] J --> K[NA PLUS K PLUS ATPASE
Restores Gradients] K --> L[RETURN TO RESTING
V_m equals minus 70 mV] L --> M[ABSOLUTE REFRACTORY
1 ms dead time] M --> N[RELATIVE REFRACTORY
2 to 5 ms reduced excitability] N --> O[READY FOR NEXT AP] O --> C E --> C ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Neural Signal Pathway to Biosensor ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[NEURON CELL BODY
Soma] --> P2[AXON HILLOCK
AP Initiation] P2 --> P3[AXON
Unmyelinated or Myelinated] P3 --> P4[SYNAPTIC TERMINAL
Neurotransmitter Release] P4 --> P5[POSTSYNAPTIC NEURON
New Graded Potential] P3 -.-> P6[EXTRACELLULAR FIELD
50 to 500 microvolt] P6 --> P7[MICROELECTRODE
MEA or ECoG] P7 --> P8[FRONT END AMPLIFIER
Gain equals 10000] P8 --> P9[BANDPASS FILTER
300 Hz to 3 kHz] P9 --> P10[ADC SAMPLING
at least 6 kHz] P10 --> P11[DIGITAL PROCESSOR
Spike Sorting] P11 --> P12[BCI OUTPUT
Decoded Command] ``` ## 4.3 State Diagram — Voltage-Gated Na⁺ Channel (Hodgkin-Huxley) The Na⁺ channel has **three functional states**. Transitions depend on membrane voltage and time. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Closed Closed --> Open : Depolarization above threshold Open --> Inactivated : Fast inactivation within 1 ms Inactivated --> Closed : Repolarization and time delay Closed --> Closed : Subthreshold stimulus no change Inactivated --> Inactivated : Cannot reopen during absolute refractory ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiner tip:** The Na⁺ channel inactivation gate ($h$-gate) is what creates the **absolute refractory period**. Until the membrane repolarizes and the $h$-gate resets, no stimulus — no matter how strong — can open the channel. This is the biophysical basis of **unidirectional AP propagation** along the axon. ## 4.4 Signal Characteristics — Time vs. Frequency Domain ```mermaid flowchart TB S[NEURAL SIGNAL] --> T[TIME DOMAIN] S --> F[FREQUENCY DOMAIN] T --> T1[Amplitude in mV] T --> T2[Duration in ms] T --> T3[Rise time and fall time] T --> T4[Inter spike interval] F --> F1[Power spectral density] F --> F2[Peak frequency in Hz] F --> F3[Bandwidth in Hz] F --> F4[Signal to noise ratio in dB] ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Block — Graded Potential vs Action Potential ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GP[GRADED POTENTIAL] G1[Variable amplitude] G2[Decremental with distance] G3[Can sum spatially and temporally] G4[No threshold] G5[No refractory period] end subgraph AP[ACTION POTENTIAL] A1[All or none fixed amplitude] A2[Non decremental regenerated] A3[Does not sum follows all or none] A4[Threshold at minus 55 mV] A5[Refractory period 1 to 5 ms] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Action Potential and State its Phases **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Model Answer:** An **action potential** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none change in the membrane potential of an excitable cell (neuron), caused by the sequential opening of voltage-gated sodium and potassium ion channels. It allows propagation of electrical signals along the neuron. **Five standard phases of an action potential:** | Phase | Name | Voltage Change | Ion Movement | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | Depolarization | −70 mV → +30 mV | Na⁺ influx | | 1 | Initial repolarization | Small dip | Na⁺ inactivation, transient K⁺ efflux | | 2 | Repolarization | +30 mV → −80 mV | K⁺ efflux | | 3 | After-hyperpolarization | Below resting | K⁺ channels still open | | 4 | Resting potential | Return to −70 mV | Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase activity | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **after-hyperpolarization** with a separate phase of further depolarization. The AHP is when the membrane potential becomes *more negative* than rest (i.e., closer to $E_K$), not more positive. Marks lost: **0.5 mark** for mislabeling the direction. --- ### Question 2: What are the Characteristics of a Neural Signal? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The characteristics of a neural (action potential) signal are: 1. **Amplitude:** ~100 mV intracellular; 50–500 µV extracellular. 2. **Duration:** 1–2 ms for the full AP. 3. **Rise time:** 0.2–0.5 ms. 4. **All-or-none nature:** Fixed amplitude once threshold is reached. 5. **Refractory period:** 1–2 ms absolute; 2–5 ms relative. 6. **Conduction velocity:** 0.5–120 m/s depending on axon diameter and myelination. 7. **Frequency content:** Energy concentrated in 100 Hz – 2 kHz. 8. **Signal-to-noise ratio:** 20–40 dB typical for extracellular recordings. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **frequency content / bandwidth**, which is critical for biosensor filter design. Marks lost: **1 mark** if the frequency domain parameters are missing. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an internal choice. Question A and Question B below are two independent alternatives from the same module. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Q. (a)** Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium potential of a permeant ion across a biological membrane. Hence, calculate the Nernst potential for potassium ion at 37 °C given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Statement of equilibrium condition:** At equilibrium, the chemical potential difference (driving ions to move) exactly balances the electrical potential difference (driving ions to move in the opposite direction). The net flux of the ion is zero. **Step 2 — Chemical work of moving 1 mole of ions:** $$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Electrical work of moving 1 mole of ions:** $$\Delta G_{elec} = zFE_{ion}$$ **Step 4 — Apply equilibrium:** $$RT \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right) + zFE_{ion} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $E_{ion}$:** $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Conversion to base-10 and insertion of physiological constants (T = 310 K):** $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$ $$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{z \times 96485} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ [V]}$$ $$E_{ion} = \frac{61.54}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ [mV]}$$ **Step 7 — Numerical calculation for K⁺ (z = +1):** $$E_{K^+} = 61.54 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) \text{ mV}$$ $$E_{K^+} = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(0.03571)$$ $$E_{K^+} = 61.54 \times (-1.4472)$$ $$\boxed{E_{K^+} \approx -89.05 \text{ mV}}$$ The negative sign indicates that the **inside of the cell is negative with respect to the outside** at equilibrium for K⁺. --- **Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various phases of a brain action potential. Discuss the role of voltage-gated ion channels in each phase. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Phase 0 — Depolarization:** - $V_m$ reaches threshold (−55 mV). - Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open** (m-gate activation, ~0.1 ms time constant). - Na⁺ rushes in → $V_m$ rises to +30 mV. **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization (in some neurons):** - Na⁺ channels inactivate. - A-type K⁺ channels (I_A) open transiently. - Brief hyperpolarizing dip. **Phase 2 — Repolarization:** - Delayed-rectifier K⁺ channels open fully. - K⁺ efflux dominates. - $V_m$ falls toward $E_K$ (−92 mV). **Phase 3 — After-hyperpolarization (AHP):** - K⁺ channels close slowly. - $V_m$ briefly undershoots below resting potential. - Lasts 2–5 ms. **Phase 4 — Resting potential restoration:** - Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores ionic gradients (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP). - Leak channels return membrane to −70 mV. **Role of voltage-gated ion channels — Summary:** | Channel | Activation | Inactivation | Function | |---|---|---|---| | Na⁺ (m-gate) | Fast, depolarization | Slow, voltage/time | Upstroke of AP | | Na⁺ (h-gate) | Slow | Fast, repolarization | Absolute refractory | | K⁺ (delayed rectifier) | Slow, depolarization | Slow, repolarization | Downstroke of AP | | K⁺ (A-type) | Fast, depolarization | Fast | Phase 1 dip | | Ca²⁺ (in some neurons) | Depolarization | Slow | Neurotransmitter release, AHP | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Common errors in 7-mark action potential questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the threshold value** (−55 mV). Marks lost: 0.5 > 2. **Confusing m-gate and h-gate** of the Na⁺ channel. The m-gate activates fast and inactivates slow; the h-gate is the opposite. Marks lost: 1.0 > 3. **Not mentioning Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** as the energy-consuming pump that restores gradients. Marks lost: 0.5 > 4. **Omitting the all-or-none principle** — the AP is either full or none; graded subthreshold responses are NOT action potentials. Marks lost: 0.5 --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Re-modelled for 2024 Scheme]** **Q. (a)** Explain the various characteristics of a neural signal in detail. Discuss how each characteristic affects biosensor design for EEG/neural recording. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The neural signal is characterized by several measurable parameters. Each has direct implications for biosensor design. **(i) Amplitude:** - **Intracellular AP:** 100 mV peak-to-peak. - **Extracellular AP:** 50–500 µV (very small). - **EEG (sum of many neurons):** 10–100 µV. - **Biosensor implication:** Requires a **front-end amplifier with gain of 10,000–100,000** and **low input-referred noise** (< 1 µV RMS). **(ii) Frequency content / Bandwidth:** - Single AP: energy in 100 Hz – 3 kHz. - EEG bands: Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (30–100 Hz). - **Biosensor implication:** Sampling rate must follow **Nyquist criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. For EEG: $f_s \geq 200$ Hz; for AP: $f_s \geq 6$ kHz. Bandpass filter design is module-specific. **(iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** - **Biosensor implication:** Determines **electrode material choice** (Ag/AgCl, Pt-Ir, carbon nanotube). High-impedance microelectrodes have low SNR; low-impedance EEG electrodes have high SNR but poor spatial resolution. **(iv) Rise time:** - 0.2–0.5 ms. - **Biosensor implication:** Sets the **minimum amplifier slew rate**. Slew rate ≥ 1 V/ms typical to avoid distortion. **(v) Phase / Timing:** - Spike timing carries information (rate coding + temporal coding). - **Biosensor implication:** **Jitter in ADC clock** must be < 10 µs for spike-timing-dependent applications. **(vi) Conduction velocity:** - 0.5–120 m/s. - **Biosensor implication:** Determines **electrode spacing** for compound nerve action potential recordings (e.g., nerve conduction studies). **(vii) Refractory period:** - 1–5 ms. - **Biosensor implication:** Sets the **unit-isolation window** in spike sorting algorithms (typically 1 ms dead time). --- **Q. (b)** Derive the 1D cable equation for passive signal propagation along an unmyelinated axon. Show that the steady-state solution decays exponentially with the length constant $\lambda$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define parameters:** - $r_i$ = axial (intracellular) resistance per unit length [Ω/m] - $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length [Ω·m] - $c_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit length [F/m] - $v(x,t)$ = membrane potential at position $x$, time $t$ **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at node $x$:** $$i(x) = i(x + \Delta x) + i_m + i_c$$ In differential form: $$-\frac{\partial i}{\partial x} = i_m + i_c$$ **Step 3 — Express membrane and capacitive currents:** $$i_m = \frac{v}{r_m}, \quad i_c = c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ **Step 4 — Express axial current via Ohm's law:** $$i = -\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and differentiate:** $$-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{v}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ $$\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = \frac{v}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange into the standard cable equation:** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = r_i c_m \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + \frac{r_i v}{r_m}}$$ **Step 7 — Define constants:** Length constant: $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ Time constant: $\tau = r_m c_m$ The equation simplifies to: $$\lambda^2 \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial x^2} = \tau \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + v$$ **Step 8 — Steady-state solution ($\partial v / \partial t = 0$):** $$\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = \frac{v}{\lambda^2}$$ Assume solution $v = A e^{kx}$: $$k^2 = \frac{1}{\lambda^2} \Rightarrow k = \pm \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ $$v(x) = A e^{-x/\lambda} + B e^{+x/\lambda}$$ **Step 9 — Apply boundary conditions for semi-infinite cable:** - $v(0) = v_0$ (applied voltage at origin) → $A + B = v_0$ - $v \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$ → $B = 0$ **Step 10 — Final result:** $$\boxed{v(x) = v_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}}$$ The voltage **decays exponentially** with distance, with characteristic length $\lambda$. For unmyelinated axons, $\lambda \approx 1$ mm; for myelinated axons, $\lambda \approx 10$ mm. This is why **saltatory conduction** in myelinated fibers is so efficient — the AP effectively "jumps" between Nodes of Ranvier spaced ~1 mm apart. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Common errors in cable equation derivations:** > 1. **Mixing up** $r_m$ [Ω·m] (resistance per length) with $R_m$ [Ω·cm²] (resistance per area). They differ by a factor of $2\pi a$ (the circumference). Marks lost: 1.0 > 2. **Forgetting to apply boundary conditions** — without $B = 0$, the general solution has an unphysical exponentially-growing term. Marks lost: 1.0 > 3. **Stating the steady-state equation without justifying** why $\partial v/\partial t = 0$ (it is a long-time limit after a step stimulus). Marks lost: 0.5 > 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the boundary** $v(0) = v_0$. Marks lost: 0.5 --- ## KTU Examiner's Master Warning Box > [!WARNING] > **TOP-WEIGHTED PITFALLS FOR THIS MODULE (Cumulative Loss if Missed):** > > 1. **Sign convention in Nernst equation:** Students often write $\ln([ion]_{in}/[ion]_{out})$ instead of the correct $\ln([ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in})$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.** > 2. **Resting vs. equilibrium potential:** The resting potential is **NOT** the same as $E_K$. It is the weighted average of $E_K$, $E_{Na}$, and $E_{Cl}$ via the GHK equation. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 3. **Confusing AP with graded potential:** Graded potentials (EPSPs/IPSPs) are decremental and summable; APs are all-or-none and regenerative. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 4. **Forgetting to convert T = 37 °C = 310 K** in Nernst problems. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 5. **Not distinguishing the two Na⁺ channel gates (m and h).** This is a favorite 2-mark question. **Loss: 2 marks.** --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST **Core Definitions:** - [ ] Action potential = rapid, transient, all-or-none depolarization of neuronal membrane. - [ ] Resting potential = $V_m \approx -70$ mV maintained by Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase. - [ ] Threshold potential = −55 mV. - [ ] Nernst equation = equilibrium potential of a single ion. - [ ] GHK equation = resting $V_m$ considering all permeant ions. **Phases to Memorize (5):** - [ ] Phase 0: Depolarization (Na⁺ in) - [ ] Phase 1: Initial repolarization (Na⁺ inactivates, A-type K⁺) - [ ] Phase 2: Repolarization (delayed K⁺ out) - [ ] Phase 3: After-hyperpolarization - [ ] Phase 4: Resting restoration **Key Channels:** - [ ] Na⁺ channel has TWO gates: m (activation) and h (inactivation). - [ ] K⁺ delayed-rectifier channel = repolarization. - [ ] Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase = 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in (electrogenic). **Refractory Periods:** - [ ] Absolute: ~1 ms; no stimulus can trigger AP. - [ ] Relative: 2–5 ms; stronger-than-normal stimulus needed. **Signal Characteristics:** - [ ] Intracellular AP amplitude: ~100 mV. - [ ] Extracellular AP amplitude: 50–500 µV. - [ ] Frequency content: 100 Hz – 3 kHz. - [ ] Conduction velocity: 0.5 m/s (C-fiber) to 120 m/s (Aα-fiber). - [ ] SNR for extracellular neural recording: 20–40 dB. **Key Equations to Memorize:** - [ ] $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln([ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in})$ - [ ] $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}([ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in})$ at 37 °C - [ ] $v(x) = v_0 e^{-x/\lambda}$ (passive spread) - [ ] $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m/r_i}$ - [ ] $\tau = r_m c_m$ - [ ] $f_{max} = 1/(T_{AP} + T_{ARP})$ - [ ] $v_{cond} = 6 \cdot d$ (myelinated, m/s, d in µm) **KTU 2024 Quick-Fire Facts:** - [ ] Hodgkin & Huxley shared the 1963 Nobel Prize for AP modeling. - [ ] Nernst equation is derived from $\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$. - [ ] All-or-none principle: graded below threshold; full AP at/above threshold. - [ ] Unidirectional propagation: ensured by absolute refractory period. - [ ] Saltatory conduction: AP jumps between Nodes of Ranvier in myelinated axons.

topics.title: Electrodes and 10-20 System for EEG Signal Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrodes and 10-20 System for EEG Signal Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrodes in Bioelectric Signal Acquisition

### Formal Definition
A **biopotential electrode** is an electrochemical transducer that converts ionic conduction in biological tissue into electronic conduction suitable for measurement instrumentation. In the context of **Electroencephalography (EEG)**, electrodes are specialized non-polarizable or minimally polarizable conductive interfaces that pick up the weak electrical activity of cortical neurons (~1–100 µV, 0.5–100 Hz) from the scalp surface with minimal distortion, drift, and noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBEBT304, Module 2):**  
> *"Electrodes for bioelectric signal acquisition – surface, needle, and micro-electrodes; electrode-skin interface; 10-20 system for EEG."*  
> The focus is on **material science**, **equivalent circuit modeling**, and **standardized placement protocol**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you are trying to listen to a tiny whisper (the EEG signal, ~50 µV) in the middle of a loud rock concert (muscle noise, mains hum, movement artifacts). The **electrode** is your hand cupped behind the ear — it must:
1. **Pick up the whisper** (high sensitivity, low impedance).
2. **Block the concert noise** (high common-mode rejection).
3. **Not itself generate noise** (low half-cell potential drift, stable chemistry).
4. **Sit comfortably for hours** (biocompatible, non-irritating).

A poorly chosen electrode is like a wet finger on a metal railing — it picks up 50 Hz mains hum instead of the brain signal.

### Types of EEG Electrodes (Taxonomy)

| Class | Sub-type | Application | Typical Impedance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface (Scalp) Electrodes** | Ag/AgCl disc | Clinical routine EEG | 1–10 kΩ |
| | Sintered Ag/AgCl cup | Long-term monitoring | < 5 kΩ |
| | Gold cup (research) | High-end labs | < 5 kΩ |
| **Needle Electrodes** | Subdermal needle | ICU, emergency | < 2 kΩ |
| **Dry Electrodes** | Ti/Si pin arrays | Wearable EEG | 10–100 kΩ |
| **Microelectrodes** | Depth (intracranial) | Epilepsy surgery (ECoG) | < 1 MΩ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Silver/Silver-Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** is the gold standard for EEG because it is a **non-polarizable** electrode — current passes across the electrode-electrolyte interface by a reversible redox reaction (Ag ⇌ Ag⁺ + e⁻) rather than by charging a double-layer capacitance, which dramatically reduces motion artifact and DC offset drift.

### Key Standardized Metrics (Bold Highlights)
- **Standard EEG signal amplitude:** **1–100 µV** (clinical band 10–50 µV).
- **Frequency range:** **0.5–100 Hz** (clinical: 0.5–30 Hz; high-frequency oscillations up to 500 Hz in research).
- **Recommended electrode-skin impedance:** **< 5 kΩ** at 10 Hz for clinical recordings.
- **Standard electrode diameter:** **10 mm** (adult cup electrode), **6 mm** (pediatric).
- **Number of electrodes in the standard 10-20 system:** **21** (including ground and references, expands to 64, 128, 256 in high-density arrays).

---

## 1.2 The 10-20 International System of EEG Electrode Placement

### Formal Definition
The **10-20 System** is the internationally standardized method for placing scalp electrodes, established by **Herbert H. Jasper (1958)** and adopted by the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)**. The system defines scalp electrode locations as percentages — **10% and 20%** — of the total distances between four anatomical landmarks: the **Nasion**, **Inion**, and the left and right **Preauricular points** (or the **Cz-equivalent** landmarks). The 21 standard positions are labeled by a letter (denoting the underlying cortical region) and a number (denoting the hemisphere), with "z" denoting the midline.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the head as a globe and the **10-20 system** as a standardized latitude/longitude grid. Just as **every weather station on Earth reports its coordinates**, every EEG lab in the world — from Tokyo to Trivandrum — places electrodes in the *exact same spots* relative to the head's geometry. This makes a recording from India **directly comparable** to one from the USA, enabling reproducible neuroscience and clinical neurology.

### The Four Anatomical Landmarks
1. **Nasion (Nz):** The depression at the bridge of the nose, between the eyes.
2. **Inion (Iz):** The bony prominence at the base of the occipital bone (back of the head).
3. **Left Preauricular point (A1):** The small depression in front of the tragus of the left ear.
4. **Right Preauricular point (A2):** The same on the right.

The **Cz position** is defined as the intersection of the **longitudinal midline** (Nasion → Inion) and the **transverse line** (A1 → A2).

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:**  
> **"Nose, Knot, Notches"** → **Nasion**, **Inion** (knot of occipital bone), **Preauricular notches** (A1, A2).  
> Memorize these four landmarks — every KTU exam question on 10-20 placement starts here.

### Electrode Nomenclature (Letter + Number/Letter)

| Letter | Cortical Region | Number Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Fp** | Frontal Pole (prefrontal) | Odd = Left, Even = Right, **z = Midline** |
| **F** | Frontal | Same as above |
| **C** | Central (sensorimotor strip) | Same as above |
| **P** | Parietal | Same as above |
| **T** | Temporal | Same as above |
| **O** | Occipital | Same as above |
| **A** | Auricular (ear reference: A1, A2) | Not used in 10-20 grid |
| **Pg / Fz / Cz / Pz / Oz** | Midline electrodes | Always labeled **z** |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Hemispheric Rule (KTU Examiner Favourite):**  
> - **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7) → LEFT hemisphere**  
> - **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8) → RIGHT hemisphere**  
> - **Lower number = closer to the midline** (e.g., F3 is medial to F7).  
> This rule is asked directly in almost every KTU Part-A question on 10-20 placement.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrode–Electrolyte Interface: Physical and Electrochemical Basis

When a metal (M) is immersed in an electrolyte containing its own ions (M^n+), an **electrical double layer** forms at the interface. Ions of opposite charge align on the electrolyte side, and a corresponding layer of charge accumulates on the metal side. This creates a **Half-Cell Potential** (also called **electrode potential**), $E$.

### Two Classes of Electrodes

1. **Perfectly Polarizable Electrodes** (e.g., Platinum in saline): Current across the interface is purely capacitive (charges the double layer like a capacitor); no actual charge crosses the interface.
2. **Perfectly Non-Polarizable Electrodes** (e.g., Ag/AgCl in KCl-saturated gel): Current crosses the interface freely by a reversible Faradaic reaction, so the half-cell potential remains stable and is independent of current density.

For EEG, **Ag/AgCl is preferred** because the non-polarizable behaviour minimises **motion artifact** and **DC drift** when the patient moves.

### 2.1.1 The Nernst Equation for Half-Cell Potential

For the general reversible half-cell reaction:

$$
\text{Ox} + n e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}
$$

The equilibrium half-cell potential is given by the **Nernst equation**:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}} \right)
$$

Where:
- $E^\circ$ = **standard reduction potential** (V)
- $R$ = **universal gas constant** = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $T$ = **absolute temperature** (K) — at body temperature $T = 310 \text{ K}$
- $n$ = **number of electrons transferred**
- $F$ = **Faraday constant** = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**
- $a_{\text{Ox}}, a_{\text{Red}}$ = activities (≈ concentrations) of oxidised/reduced species

For the **Ag/AgCl electrode** in NaCl electrolyte, the half-reaction is:

$$
\text{AgCl}_{(s)} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}^-
$$

So the **Ag/AgCl half-cell potential** is:

$$
E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^\circ_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln \left( \frac{1}{[\text{Cl}^-]} \right) = E^\circ_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} + \frac{RT}{F} \ln [\text{Cl}^-]
$$

At $T = 310 \text{ K}$, $\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.02671 \text{ V}$, and with $E^\circ_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = +0.222 \text{ V}$ at 25 °C, a typical body-temperature value is **+0.230 V vs. SHE**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why does this matter for EEG?** Two Ag/AgCl electrodes placed on the scalp ideally have *identical* half-cell potentials so their DC offset cancels in a differential amplifier. **Mismatch** between the two electrode half-cell potentials appears as a DC offset that can saturate the amplifier; this is why **identical gel, identical skin prep, and identical electrode material** are critical.

### 2.1.2 Equivalent Circuit of the Electrode–Skin Interface

The complete interface can be modelled as the following lumped-parameter circuit (from metal → electrolyte):

| Element | Symbol | Physical Origin | Typical Value (10 Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Half-cell source | $E_{hc}$ | Nernst potential | 200–250 mV |
| Charge-transfer resistance | $R_{ct}$ | Faradaic reaction kinetics | 1–100 kΩ |
| Double-layer capacitance | $C_{dl}$ | Electrical double layer | 1–50 nF |
| Spreading/series resistance | $R_s$ | Electrolyte gel, tissue | 0.1–2 kΩ |
| Skin impedance | $Z_{skin}$ | Stratum corneum (high, ~ 10–100 kΩ) | — |

The total **electrode-skin interface impedance** is dominated by the parallel combination of $R_{ct}$ and $C_{dl}$ in series with $R_s$ and $Z_{skin}$:

$$
Z_{\text{total}} = R_s + Z_{skin} + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j 2\pi f R_{ct} C_{dl}}
$$

At the **mains frequency of 50 Hz**, the parallel RC contributes roughly $\frac{1}{2\pi f C_{dl}} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi(50)(C_{dl})}$, so the impedance is still high — this is why **skin preparation (abrasion with gel)** is mandatory before EEG.

### 2.1.3 Skin Preparation & Impedance Reduction

Effective EEG recording requires **electrode-skin impedance < 5 kΩ**. The procedure includes:
1. **Abrasion** of the stratum corneum with a cotton-tipped applicator and abrasive paste (e.g., NuPrep).
2. **Application of conductive gel** (typically KCl-saturated, e.g., Ten20).
3. **Stabilisation time:** 5–10 minutes to allow the gel to equilibrate with skin electrolytes.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Short-Answer Trick:** When asked *“Why is skin preparation essential before EEG?”* — answer in three bullets: (1) reduces electrode-skin impedance below 5 kΩ; (2) stabilises the half-cell potential by saturating the gel with Cl⁻ ions; (3) reduces motion artifacts and improves signal-to-noise ratio.

---

## 2.2 The 10-20 System: Detailed Mathematical Construction

### 2.2.1 Head Circumference Measurements

Define:
- **Longitudinal (sagittal) distance** $L = \text{Nasion to Inion}$
- **Transverse (coronal) distance** $W = \text{Left preauricular (A1) to Right preauricular (A2)}$

The midline electrode positions are placed at the following fractions of $L$, **starting from the Nasion** and **moving towards the Inion**:

$$
0.10L,\ 0.30L,\ 0.50L,\ 0.70L,\ 0.90L
$$

These correspond to **Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz** respectively.

Transversely, the central chain is placed at fractions of $W$, **from left to right**:

$$
0.10W,\ 0.30W,\ 0.50W,\ 0.70W,\ 0.90W
$$

These yield the left-to-right chain **T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4** along the central coronal line.

### 2.2.2 General Position Formula

For an electrode at position $P$ on the longitudinal line, measured from the nasion:

$$
P = k \cdot L
$$

Where $k$ is the cumulative fraction. The full grid is constructed by combining longitudinal and transverse divisions, with intermediate electrodes placed at intersections of the resulting **10% and 20%** lines.

> [!NOTE]  
> **"10-20" literally means:** adjacent electrodes are separated by either **10%** or **20%** of the total head measurement. The system ensures that the inter-electrode distance is uniform across the head, giving **equal spatial coverage**.

### 2.2.3 Why Midline is "z"

The letter **z** is taken from the German word "**Zentrum**" (centre), reflecting the historical European roots of EEG methodology. All **z** electrodes (Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz) lie exactly on the longitudinal midline.

---

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical neurology:** Standardised placement allows comparison of a patient's record with population norms for **epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies**.
- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface):** Modern systems use **high-density extensions** (10-10, 10-5) of the same proportional logic for fine-grained source localisation.
- **ICU Monitoring:** Subdermal needle electrodes (used when scalp prep is impossible) use the same 10-20 anatomical sites.
- **Forensic / Ambulatory EEG:** Dry electrode arrays in headsets (Emotiv, OpenBCI) use a *simplified* 10-20 subset for consumer use.

---

## 2.4 The Complete EEG Signal Acquisition Chain (Block-Level Architecture)

A typical EEG signal acquisition system consists of the following cascaded stages:

1. **Scalp electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** → ionic pick-up.
2. **Electrode-skin interface** → impedance matching, half-cell stabilisation.
3. **Patient cable / connector box (headbox)** → 5–10 kΩ input isolation, safety resistors.
4. **Differential amplifier (instrumentation amplifier, INA)** → first stage, high CMRR (> 100 dB), gain ≈ ×10.
5. **Band-pass filter (0.5–100 Hz analogue)** → removes DC drift and high-freq noise.
6. **Notch filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz)** → rejects mains hum.
7. **Second-stage amplifier** → gain adjustable, total gain ≈ ×1,000 to ×100,000.
8. **ADC (16–24 bit, ≥ 256 Hz sampling)** → digitisation.
9. **Digital signal processing** → artefact rejection, FFT, source localisation.
10. **Display / storage** → waveform display, database archive.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why differential amplification is critical for EEG:** The EEG signal is buried in 50/60 Hz common-mode interference (mains noise) that may be **10,000× larger** than the brain signal. A **differential amplifier with CMRR ≥ 100 dB** (i.e., rejects common-mode signals by a factor of 100,000) is mandatory.

### 2.4.1 CMRR in Practice

If $A_d = 10^5$ and $A_c = 10$ (typical):

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{10^5}{10} \right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^4) = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

To handle EEG, modern amplifiers specify **CMRR ≥ 110 dB at 50 Hz**.

### 2.4.2 The Differential Measurement Concept

In an EEG, the signal is **bipolar** (or referential) and is computed as:

$$
V_{\text{EEG}}(t) = V_{\text{active}}(t) - V_{\text{reference}}(t)
$$

Common **montages** (ways of pairing electrodes):
- **Bipolar montage:** e.g., Fp1-F3, F3-C3, C3-P3, P3-O1 (chain).
- **Referential montage:** all electrodes referenced to a common point (e.g., linked ears A1+A2, or Cz-reference).
- **Average reference:** all electrodes referenced to the average of all channels.
- **Laplacian (current source density):** spatial high-pass filter for source localisation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for the Ag/AgCl Electrode

**Starting condition:** Consider the half-cell reaction in equilibrium:

$$
\text{AgCl}_{(s)} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}^-_{(aq)}
$$

The **free energy change** of the reaction is related to the cell potential by:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE
$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G$ is given by the **chemical potential** of products minus reactants:

$$
\Delta G = \mu_{\text{Ag}} + \mu_{\text{Cl}^-} - \mu_{\text{AgCl}} - \mu_{e^-}
$$

Using the chemical potential convention $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i$:

$$
\Delta G = \left( \mu_{\text{Ag}}^\circ + RT \ln a_{\text{Ag}} \right) + \left( \mu_{\text{Cl}^-}^\circ + RT \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-} \right) - \left( \mu_{\text{AgCl}}^\circ + RT \ln a_{\text{AgCl}} \right) - \mu_{e^-}
$$

Since **Ag and AgCl are pure solids**, $a_{\text{Ag}} = a_{\text{AgCl}} = 1$:

$$
\Delta G = \mu_{\text{Ag}}^\circ + \mu_{\text{Cl}^-}^\circ - \mu_{\text{AgCl}}^\circ - \mu_{e^-} + RT \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}
$$

The constant group $\mu_{\text{Ag}}^\circ + \mu_{\text{Cl}^-}^\circ - \mu_{\text{AgCl}}^\circ - \mu_{e^-} \equiv -nFE^\circ$:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}
$$

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}
$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{\text{Cl}^-}
$$

For Ag/AgCl, $n = 1$, so:

$$
E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^\circ_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{1}{[\text{Cl}^-]} = E^\circ_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} + \frac{RT}{F} \ln [\text{Cl}^-]
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:** The Ag/AgCl potential is **linearly proportional to the logarithm of chloride ion activity**. A 10-fold increase in [Cl⁻] shifts $E$ by $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \approx 59.2 \text{ mV}$ at 25 °C. This is why KCl-saturated gel stabilises the potential.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Evaluation of the Nernst Slope at Body Temperature

**Given:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $T = 310 \text{ K}$ (core body temperature), $F = 96485 \text{ C·mol}^{-1}$, $n = 1$.

The Nernst "RT/nF" term in volts is:

$$
\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} \text{ V}
$$

Multiplying numerator:

$$
8.314 \times 310 = 2577.34
$$

Dividing by $F$:

$$
\frac{2577.34}{96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V} = 26.71 \text{ mV}
$$

In **log₁₀ form** (i.e., the famous "59 mV/decade" slope at 25 °C, recomputed at 37 °C):

$$
\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = 2.303 \times 0.02671 = 0.06151 \text{ V} = 61.51 \text{ mV per decade}
$$

So for the Ag/AgCl electrode at body temperature:

$$
E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = 0.222 + 0.0615 \cdot \log_{10}[\text{Cl}^-] \text{ V}
$$

For typical KCl-saturated gel, $[\text{Cl}^-] \approx 3 \text{ M}$, so $\log_{10}(3) = 0.477$:

$$
E \approx 0.222 + 0.0615 \times 0.477 = 0.222 + 0.0293 = 0.2513 \text{ V vs. SHE}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is the **typical in-vivo half-cell potential** of an Ag/AgCl EEG electrode — useful for KTU numerical questions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Double-Layer Capacitance Reactance vs. Frequency

The **double-layer capacitance** $C_{dl}$ is in parallel with the charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$. The complex impedance of this parallel combination is:

$$
Z_{RC \parallel} = \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + j 2\pi f R_{ct} C_{dl}}
$$

Its magnitude is:

$$
\vert Z_{RC\parallel} \vert = \frac{R_{ct}}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}}
$$

**Numerical example:** $R_{ct} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{dl} = 10 \text{ nF}$.

At $f = 10 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
2\pi f R_{ct} C_{dl} = 2\pi \times 10 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} = 2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0314
$$

$$
(0.0314)^2 = 9.87 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 + 9.87 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.00049
$$

$$
\vert Z_{RC\parallel} \vert \approx \frac{50{,}000}{1.00049} \approx 49{,}975 \text{ }\Omega \approx 50 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

So at 10 Hz, the parallel RC behaves almost as a pure resistance of 50 kΩ.

At $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
2\pi f R_{ct} C_{dl} = 2\pi \times 50 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} = 0.157
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 + 0.0247} = 1.0123
$$

$$
\vert Z_{RC\parallel} \vert \approx \frac{50{,}000}{1.0123} \approx 49{,}400 \text{ }\Omega
$$

A noticeable drop. At very high frequencies, $C_{dl}$ short-circuits $R_{ct}$ and the impedance falls as $1/f$.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Application:** Because high-frequency noise (mains hum at 50 Hz, EMG at 100+ Hz) sees a *lower* electrode impedance than the EEG signal of interest, the noise is *more easily picked up* by the electrode. This is why skin prep and good amplifier design are both critical.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CMRR from First Principles

Consider a differential amplifier with two inputs $V_1$ and $V_2$. Define:

- **Differential input:** $V_d = V_1 - V_2$
- **Common-mode input:** $V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$

The output is:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = A_d V_d + A_c V_c
$$

CMRR (in linear form):

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert
$$

In dB:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert
$$

**Numerical Example:** A standard 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) has $A_d = 100$ and $A_c = 0.01$. Calculate CMRR in dB.

Step 1 — Linear CMRR:

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{100}{0.01} = 10{,}000
$$

Step 2 — Convert to dB:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

**Application to EEG:** EEG differential signal ≈ 50 µV. Mains common-mode noise ≈ 1 V. Ratio = $\frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 20{,}000 = 86 \text{ dB}$. So the amplifier must have **CMRR > 86 dB** to attenuate the mains hum to below the EEG signal level. Hence the KTU spec of **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** for clinical EEG.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Electrode-Skin Total Impedance as a Function of Frequency

Combining the elements: $Z_{\text{total}} = R_s + Z_{\text{skin}} + Z_{RC\parallel}$.

Assume $R_s = 0.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $Z_{\text{skin}} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ (post-abrasion), $R_{ct} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{dl} = 10 \text{ nF}$.

At $f = 10 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
Z_{\text{total}} \approx 0.5 + 2.0 + 49.975 = 52.475 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

This is **above** the clinical target of 5 kΩ. To reduce to 5 kΩ, the user must (a) abrade the skin aggressively to bring $Z_{\text{skin}}$ down, (b) use higher-chloride gel to lower $R_{ct}$, or (c) use sintered Ag/AgCl electrodes with much larger effective surface area (hence larger $C_{dl}$ and lower impedance).

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU derivation question (frequently asked):** "Derive the expression for the impedance of an EEG electrode-skin interface and explain why the impedance is frequency dependent."  
> The answer is this parallel RC in series with $R_s + Z_{\text{skin}}$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Material Selection

| Property | Ag/AgCl | Au (Gold) | Pt (Platinum) | Ti (Titanium) | Stainless Steel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Non-polarizable | Polarizable | Polarizable | Polarizable | Polarizable |
| Half-cell potential stability | Excellent (low drift) | Moderate | Moderate | Poor | Poor |
| Noise level | Very low | Low | Low | High | High |
| Biocompatibility | Good | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | High | High | Moderate | Very low |
| Use in EEG | **Standard** | Research | Less common | Dry electrodes | Disposable / needle |

---

| **F3, F4, Fz** | Frontal lobe | Motor planning, working memory |
