# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]

**Define Evoked Potentials. Why is signal averaging necessary for recording them?**

**Model Answer:**

Evoked Potentials (EPs) are time-locked, low-amplitude electrical responses of the nervous system to a controlled sensory, motor, or cognitive stimulus. They are typically recorded from the scalp via surface electrodes following a known stimulus event.

Signal averaging is necessary because the amplitude of an EP (0.1–20 µV) is **10 to 100 times smaller** than the spontaneous background EEG activity (10–100 µV), making the EP completely buried in noise. By time-locking the recording to the stimulus and averaging N repeated sweeps, the random EEG noise cancels out (theoretically, with zero mean), while the deterministic EP signal adds coherently. This improves the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio by a factor of √N**, allowing the EP to be extracted and visualized.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write that "averaging removes noise." This is **incorrect wording**. The correct phrasing is that averaging *reduces* the noise (variance decreases by N) while the signal remains unchanged in amplitude. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**List the three principal types of clinical Evoked Potentials and state one diagnostic application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Visual Evoked Potential (VEP):** Stimulated by a pattern-reversal checkerboard; the P100 peak (normally at 95–115 ms) is delayed in **optic neuritis and multiple sclerosis**.

2. **Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (BAEP / ABR):** Stimulated by click tones; the I–V interpeak latency (normally ≤ 4.5 ms) is prolonged in **acoustic neuroma, multiple sclerosis, and intraoperative monitoring during posterior fossa surgery**.

3. **Somatosensory Evoked Potential (SEP):** Stimulated by electrical peripheral nerve stimulation (median, tibial); N20 latency and central conduction time (CCT) are used to assess **spinal cord integrity during scoliosis surgery** and to localize lesions in MS.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A – 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]** *Choose either Question A or Question B.*

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the principle of signal averaging in Evoked Potential recording. Derive the expression for SNR improvement as a function of the number of averages.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Evoked potentials are stimulus-locked, deterministic responses that occur at a fixed time after each stimulus. The background EEG (and EMG, EOG) is random noise with zero mean. By recording N successive sweeps each precisely aligned to the stimulus onset (t = 0) and computing the point-by-point average across the N sweeps, the random noise components average toward zero, while the time-locked EP adds up constructively.

**Derivation:**

Let the k-th recorded sweep be:

$$x_k(t) = s(t) + n_k(t)$$

where $s(t)$ is the deterministic EP and $n_k(t)$ is zero-mean random noise with variance $\sigma_n^2$.

The averaged signal over N sweeps:

$$\bar{x}_N(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ s(t) + n_k(t) \right] = s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k(t)$$

The expected noise term: $E\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum n_k(t)\right] = 0$, so the noise mean vanishes.

The variance of the residual noise:

$$\text{Var}\left[ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k(t) \right] = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sigma_n^2 = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{N}$$

So $\sigma_{\text{residual}} = \sigma_n / \sqrt{N}$.

The SNR after averaging:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{after}} = \frac{A_s}{\sigma_{\text{residual}}} = \frac{A_s}{\sigma_n / \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{A_s}{\sigma_n}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{after}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{before}}}$$

**Conclusion:** The SNR improves by the factor √N. To double the SNR, one must quadruple N – a diminishing-returns property that makes high-quality EP recording time-consuming.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the recording methodology, electrode placement, and clinical significance of the Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (BAEP). Include the neural generators of Waves I, III, and V.

**Model Solution:**

**Stimulus Parameters:**
- Rarefaction or alternating click of **100 µs** duration
- Intensity: **70 dB nHL** (normal hearing level) for test ear; **40–60 dB nHL** white-noise masking in the contralateral ear
- Repetition rate: **10–20 Hz**
- Monaural stimulation

**Electrode Placement (10–20 system):**
- **Active electrode:** Cz (high forehead) or **Ai (ipsilateral earlobe)** – records the stimulated ear
- **Reference electrode:** Ac (contralateral earlobe)
- **Ground electrode:** Fz or Fpz

**Acquisition Parameters:**
- **Band-pass filter:** 100 Hz – 3000 Hz (narrow bandwidth to extract fast subcortical waves from noise)
- **Analysis window:** 10–15 ms (BAEP occurs within the first 10 ms post-stimulus)
- **Number of sweeps:** 1000–2000, replicated at least twice
- **Amplifier gain:** ≈ 100,000× (BAEP amplitudes are < 1 µV)
- **Artifact rejection threshold:** ± 5 µV

**Neural Generators of Key Waves:**

| Wave | Latency (ms) | Generator Site | Anatomic Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | 1.5–2.0 | Distal CN VIII (acoustic nerve at cochlea) | Internal auditory canal |
| **III** | 3.5–4.0 | Cochlear nuclei / Superior olivary complex | **Lower pons** |
| **V** | 5.5–6.0 | Lateral lemniscus / Inferior colliculus | **Midbrain** |

**Clinical Significance:**
1. **Interpeak Latency I–V** (Central Brainstem Conduction Time, normally ≤ 4.5 ms) is the most sensitive parameter for detecting demyelination in the brainstem auditory pathway – a key diagnostic criterion in **Multiple Sclerosis**.
2. **Delayed or absent Wave V** at high click rates suggests a **retrocochlear lesion** (e.g., acoustic neuroma).
3. **Wave I preserved but V absent** = auditory nerve intact, brainstem lesion.
4. **Hearing screening in neonates**: Detects congenital deafness with > 95% sensitivity.
5. **Intraoperative monitoring** during posterior fossa surgery to preserve hearing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently misattribute **Wave III to the inferior colliculus** and **Wave V to the auditory cortex**. Wave V is generated in the **midbrain (inferior colliculus)**, NOT in the cortex. The cortical AEP waves (Na, Pa, Nb) occur at **>15 ms** and are *Middle/Long Latency AEPs*, not BAEP. **Lose 1–2 marks** for this anatomical error.

---

### Question B – 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the Visual Evoked Potential (VEP) recording procedure. Explain the origin and clinical significance of the P100 peak.

**Model Solution:**

**Stimulus:**
The standard clinical stimulus is a **high-contrast black-and-white checkerboard pattern** undergoing **reversal** (black squares become white and vice versa) at a rate of **1–2 reversals per second**. Each eye is tested monocularly. Pattern-reversal VEP is preferred over flash VEP because it produces highly reproducible waveforms with well-defined peaks.

**Recording Parameters:**
- **Active electrode:** Oz (occipital midline, 5 cm above inion)
- **Reference electrode:** Fz (frontal midline)
- **Ground electrode:** Cz or earlobe
- **Band-pass filter:** 1 – 100 Hz
- **Analysis window:** 250–500 ms
- **Number of sweeps:** 100–200 (VEP amplitude is large, so fewer sweeps needed)
- **Patient requirement:** Must fixate on a central red dot throughout the test

**P100 Origin:**
The **P100 peak** is a positive deflection occurring at approximately **100 ms (normal range 95–115 ms)** after pattern reversal. It is generated in the **primary visual cortex (V1, striate cortex) along the calcarine fissure** of the occipital lobe, primarily reflecting activity in the foveal and parafoveal cortical representation.

**Clinical Significance of P100:**

1. **Optic Neuritis (ON):** P100 is markedly **delayed (often > 130 ms)** and may be reduced in amplitude. The delay persists even after visual acuity recovers, making VEP a sensitive detector of prior subclinical ON.
2. **Multiple Sclerosis:** P100 delay in one or both eyes is part of the **McDonald diagnostic criteria** for demonstrating dissemination of demyelination along the visual pathway.
3. **Compressive Optic Neuropathies** (e.g., optic nerve glioma, pituitary tumor): May show P100 delay and reduced amplitude.
4. **Functional / Non-organic Visual Loss:** A **normal P100 strongly suggests malingering or psychogenic visual loss**, since the afferent pathway is intact.
5. **Albinism:** Demonstrates misrouting of optic nerve fibers (P100 polarity reversal in occipital midline).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare the characteristics of Exogenous and Endogenous Evoked Potentials. Explain the P300 Event-Related Potential in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Exogenous (Sensory EP) | Endogenous (ERP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Dependency on stimulus parameters** | High (intensity, rate, modality) | Low |
| **Dependency on cognitive state** | Low | High (attention, task, decision) |
| **Latency** | Short (< 100 ms typical) | Long (100–1000 ms) |
| **Amplitude** | Modest (0.5–20 µV) | Variable (5–30 µV) |
| **Origin** | Sensory-specific primary cortex / subcortical relays | Frontal cortex, association areas, hippocampus |
| **Variability** | Low (highly reproducible) | High (varies with task & subject) |
| **Examples** | P100, BAEP Waves I–V, N20 | P300, MMN, N400, N200 |
| **Subject cooperation** | Minimal | Required (must perform cognitive task) |

**P300 Event-Related Potential – Detailed Explanation:**

**Paradigm:** The **Oddball Paradigm** – a sequence of two auditory (or visual) stimuli:
- **Frequent (standard) stimulus** (80% probability, e.g., 1000 Hz tone)
- **Rare (target/deviant) stimulus** (20% probability, e.g., 2000 Hz tone)
- The subject silently counts the rare targets or presses a button.

**Waveform:** A large positive deflection occurring approximately **300 ms** after the rare stimulus onset (hence "P300"). The P300 has two subcomponents:
- **P3a (250–280 ms):** Frontal-central distribution; elicited by novel, unexpected stimuli regardless of task relevance.
- **P3b (300–400 ms):** Temporo-parietal distribution; elicited by attended, task-relevant target stimuli.

**Neural Generators:**
- **P3b:** Temporo-parietal junction (TPJ), posterior parietal cortex, hippocampal formation
- **P3a:** Dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate

**Physiological Significance:**
P300 reflects **context updating in working memory** – the brain revising its mental model when an unexpected, task-relevant event occurs. It is closely linked to the **noradrenergic locus coeruleus** and the **cholinergic basal forebrain** systems.

**Clinical Applications:**

1. **Cognitive decline / Dementia:** P300 latency is **prolonged** in Alzheimer's disease and correlates with the degree of cognitive impairment.
2. **Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD):** Reduced P300 amplitude over frontal regions.
3. **Schizophrenia:** Reduced P300 amplitude (well-replicated biological marker).
4. **Depression:** Variable findings; amplitude reduction in melancholic depression.
5. **Alcoholism:** P300 abnormalities persist into sobriety and may indicate genetic vulnerability.
6. **Disorders of Consciousness (DOC):** Presence of P300 in vegetative state patients is a favorable prognostic indicator for recovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse P3a and P3b or incorrectly attribute P300 entirely to the *frontal lobe*. The **P3b (the classical P300) is temporo-parietal**, not frontal. Examiners specifically test this. Also, P300 is **not a sensory EP** – it is *endogenous* and requires an **active task**. Marking scheme: 1 mark lost for each anatomical error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist – KTU Board Examination**

### Core Definitions
- **Evoked Potential:** Time-locked, low-amplitude neural response to a controlled external stimulus.
- **Signal Averaging:** Method to extract EP from background noise by averaging N stimulus-locked sweeps.
- **SNR Improvement:** $\sqrt{N}$ factor per N averaged sweeps.
- **Exogenous EP:** Stimulus-driven, short latency, sensory cortex generators.
- **Endogenous EP (ERP):** Cognition-driven, long latency, association cortex generators.

### Numerical Constants to Memorize
- **EP amplitude range:** **0.1 – 20 µV**
- **Background EEG amplitude:** **10 – 100 µV**
- **Bandwidth – BAEP:** **100 – 3000 Hz** (narrowest, subcortical)
- **Bandwidth – VEP/SEP:** **1 – 100 Hz** / **20 – 3000 Hz**
- **P100 normal latency:** **95 – 115 ms**
- **BAEP I–V IPL (Central Conduction):** **≤ 4.5 ms**
- **SEP N20 latency (median nerve):** **18 – 20 ms**
- **P300 latency:** **250 – 400 ms** (target rare stimulus)
- **CMRR of EP amplifier:** **≥ 100 dB**
- **Electrode impedance:** **< 5 kΩ**, balanced ±1 kΩ
- **Sweeps required:** VEP = 100–200; SEP = 500–1000; BAEP = 1000–2000

### Anatomical Generators – High-Yield Peaks
- **VEP → P100:** Primary visual cortex (V1, calcarine fissure)
- **BAEP Wave I:** Distal CN VIII (acoustic nerve at cochlea)
- **BAEP Wave III:** Lower pons (superior olivary complex)
- **BAEP Wave V:** Midbrain (inferior colliculus)
- **SEP N9:** Erb's point (brachial plexus)
- **SEP N13:** Dorsal horn, cervical cord
- **SEP N20:** Primary somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus)
- **P300 (P3b):** Temporo-parietal junction

### Mnemonic Devices
- **"PAVe" for VEP path:** Pattern → **P**hotoreceptors → **O**ptic nerve → **L**GN → **V**1 (**P100**)
- **"I-II-III-IV-V bridges pons to mid-brain":** BAEP waves correspond to ascending auditory pathway stations
- **"P300 — Patient Counts 300 of them":** P300 elicited in oddball task where subject counts rare targets

### Clinical Conditions to Associate
- **Multiple Sclerosis:** VEP P100 delay; BAEP I–V IPL prolongation; SEP CCT prolongation (3 modalities cover 3 CNS pathways)
- **Acoustic Neuroma:** BAEP Wave V absent/delayed; I–III IPL prolonged
- **Optic Neuritis:** P100 delay (may persist indefinitely)
- **Spinal Cord Monitoring (intraoperative):** SEP N20 + MEP; loss = surgical alarm
- **Neonatal Hearing Screen:** BAEP, gold-standard

### Common Valuation Pitfalls
1. **Confusing "averaging removes noise"** with "averaging *reduces* noise variance by factor N" → -1 mark
2. **Attributing BAEP Wave V to the cortex** (it is the inferior colliculus) → -1 to -2 marks
3. **Attributing P300 to the frontal lobe** (it is the temporo-parietal junction) → -1 mark
4. **Confusing EP (exogenous) with ERP (endogenous)** → -1 mark
5. **Forgetting the 10–20 system** when asked about electrode placement → -1 mark
6. **Quoting latency values without units (ms)** → -0.5 mark
7. **Skipping the derivation in SNR questions** → -2 to -3 marks; examiners expect step-by-step mathematical proof

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** For any 14-mark EP question in the KTU 2024 ESE, structure your answer as: **(1) Stimulus parameters, (2) Electrode placement (10–20), (3) Acquisition parameters (bandwidth, sweeps, analysis window), (4) Waveform/peak analysis with latencies, (5) Clinical applications, (6) Block diagram (if asked).** This template addresses every CO in the 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromyography (EMG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electromyography (EMG) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Electromyography (EMG)** is an electrodiagnostic bioelectronic technique used to record, monitor, and analyze the bioelectric potentials generated by skeletal muscle fibers during voluntary or electrically evoked contractions. The recorded waveform, known as the **electromyogram**, represents the spatiotemporal summation of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** and is the principal diagnostic modality in neuromuscular physiology and clinical neurophysiology. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEBT304 – Biosensors and Transducers)**, EMG is positioned as a *physiological biopotential transducer system* in which the **biological source (motor unit)** acts as the signal generator, the **electrode** acts as the primary transducer, and the **differential instrumentation amplifier** acts as the signal conditioning stage. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** > Think of a stadium full of people clapping randomly. If you place a microphone in the middle, you hear a steady, continuous *roar*. You cannot isolate a single person’s clap. However, if you place a directional microphone very close to one specific seat, you hear that person’s *individual* clap clearly. > > - **Surface EMG (the stadium microphone)** listens to thousands of muscle fibers firing together from a distance → produces an *interference pattern*. > - **Needle EMG (the directional microphone)** penetrates the muscle and listens to a small, specific group of fibers belonging to *one* motor unit → produces a discrete *Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)*. > > **The key insight:** EMG does not measure muscle *force* directly. It measures the *electrical command* the nervous system sends to the muscle, from which force can be inferred. ## 1.3 The Motor Unit – The Fundamental Bioelectric Source > [!NOTE] > **Motor Unit (MU):** A single lower motor neuron (alpha motoneuron in the spinal cord ventral horn) **+** all the skeletal muscle fibers it innervates **+** the neuromuscular junctions connecting them. > > This is the smallest functionally independent contractile element of the voluntary muscular system and the fundamental "signal packet" of any EMG recording. | Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **MUAP Amplitude (surface)** | **0.1 – 5 mV** | Defines dynamic range of front-end amplifier | | **MUAP Duration (surface)** | **5 – 20 ms** | Sets required sampling rate | | **MUAP Frequency Content** | **20 – 500 Hz** | Sets analog filter cutoffs | | **Motor Unit Territory** | **5 – 10 mm (diameter)** | Determines inter-electrode spacing | | **Firing Rate (voluntary)** | **6 – 30 Hz** | Sets the Nyquist limit of relevance | ## 1.4 Classification of EMG Recording Modalities > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to clearly differentiate between **non-invasive** and **invasive** EMG, as the transducer (electrode) selection drives the entire downstream instrumentation chain. - **Surface EMG (sEMG)** — Non-invasive; Ag/AgCl electrodes on skin; used in ergonomics, sports, prosthetics, and rehabilitation robotics. - **Intramuscular EMG (iEMG / Needle EMG)** — Invasive; concentric needle, monopolar needle, or single-fiber needle; used in clinical neurophysiology for diagnosis of myopathies, neuropathies, and disorders of the neuromuscular junction. - **Fine-Wire EMG** — Semi-invasive; hooked platinum-iridium wires; used for deep or small muscles (e.g., intrinsic hand muscles). - **High-Density sEMG (HD-EMG) Arrays** — 2-D grid of closely spaced surface electrodes for topographical mapping of muscle activation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Biophysics of EMG Signal Generation The EMG signal is generated through the following sequential physiological chain: 1. **Cortical Intent** → descending corticospinal volleys arrive at the **alpha motoneuron** in the anterior horn of the spinal cord. 2. **Motoneuron Depolarization** → if the threshold is exceeded, an **Action Potential (AP)** is generated at the axon hillock. 3. **Axonal Conduction** → the AP propagates distally along the motor axon at a conduction velocity of **50 – 60 m/s** for large alpha motoneurons. 4. **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ) Transmission** → acetylcholine (ACh) is released into the synaptic cleft, depolarizing the end-plate region of the muscle fiber. 5. **Muscle Fiber AP Propagation** → the depolarization wave travels along the sarcolemma and into the T-tubules at approximately **2 – 5 m/s**, triggering excitation-contraction coupling. 6. **Extrajacellular Field Potential** → ionic currents (Na⁺ influx, K⁺ efflux) in the extracellular fluid create a local potential field that is detected by the recording electrode. 7. **Spatial Summation** → all fibers of one motor unit fire nearly synchronously (within ~1 – 2 ms dispersion), producing the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**. 8. **Temporal Summation / Recruitment** → as force demand increases, additional motor units are *recruited* (Henneman's size principle) and existing units *fire faster* (rate coding), producing the **interference pattern**. > [!NOTE] > **Henneman's Size Principle:** Motoneurons are recruited in order of increasing cell-body size — small (Type I, slow, fatigue-resistant) units fire first; large (Type II, fast) units are recruited only at higher force levels. This is a *very common KTU short-answer question*. ## 2.2 EMG Electrode Transduction Principles The electrode is the **primary biosensor** in any EMG system. It converts ionic current (from the tissue) into electronic current (in the wire) through a **half-cell (redox) reaction**. For an Ag/AgCl surface electrode: $$E_{Ag/AgCl} = E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(a_{Cl^{-}})$$ Where: - $E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} = +0.222 \text{ V}$ (Standard Half-Cell Potential, vs. SHE) - $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ (Universal Gas Constant) - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $n = 1$ (number of electrons transferred) - $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ (Faraday's Constant) - $a_{Cl^{-}}$ = activity of chloride ion ## 2.3 Instrumentation Amplifier (The Heart of EMG Front-End) > [!IMPORTANT] > AEMG amplifier **MUST** be a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128, AD620, INA333). The key specifications that earn full marks in KTU valuation are: > - **CMRR > 100 dB** at 50/60 Hz (rejects power-line interference) > - **Input Impedance > 10¹⁰ Ω** (prevents loading of the high-impedance tissue-electrode interface) > - **Noise < 1 µV RMS** referred to input (RTI) > - **Gain = 1000 – 10000** (60–80 dB) The differential voltage sensed is: $$V_{out} = G \cdot (V_{+} - V_{-}) + \underbrace{G_c \cdot \frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2}}_{\text{common-mode component (ideally } 0\text{)}}$$ The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** — the single most-asked KTU formula for biopotential amplifiers — is: $$\boxed{\;CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)\;}$$ Where $A_{d}$ = differential gain and $A_{c}$ = common-mode gain. **A high CMRR is essential because the EMG signal is small (µV–mV) and is immersed in large 50/60 Hz common-mode interference from the power mains.** ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE** — questions on these formulas appear in nearly every KTU University Examination. | # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Units / Value | Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **CMRR (dB)** | $CMRR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)$ | dB ; typical $\geq$ **100 dB** | Quantifies rejection of mains interference | | 2 | **RMS Amplitude (EMG)** | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | Volts (RMS) | Correlates with muscle *force / fatigue* | | 3 | **Average Rectified Value (ARV)** | $ARV = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \lvert v(t) \rvert\,dt$ | Volts | Linear envelope of EMG activity | | 4 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Quality metric of the recorded signal | | 5 | **Electrode-Skin Half-Cell** | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(a_{Cl^{-}})$ | Volts | Sets the DC offset to be HPF-filtered | | 6 | **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz ; $f_{s} \geq$ **1 kHz** typical | Prevents aliasing in A/D conversion | | 7 | **Conduction Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t}$ | m/s ; **50–60 m/s** | Diagnoses demyelinating neuropathies | | 8 | **Henneman Recruitment Order** | Size-ordered (small → large) | — | Predicts order of MU activation | | 9 | **Power Line Interference** | $f_{line} = 50 \text{ Hz (India)}$ | Hz | Sets notch filter centre frequency | | 10 | **Spectral Median Frequency** | $f_{med} : \int_{0}^{f_{med}}\! P(f)df = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty}\! P(f)df$ | Hz | Muscle fatigue indicator (shifts ↓) | > **Note on KTU Notation:** In all written derivations, the magnitude of a quantity (e.g., absolute value of a voltage) is written as $\lvert v(t) \rvert$ with explicit vertical bars inside math mode, **not** with the table pipe character, to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility EMG instrumentation is the cornerstone of multiple applied biomedical engineering domains: - **Clinical Neurophysiology** — diagnosis of ALS, myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy. - **Rehabilitation Robotics & Prosthetics** — myoelectric control of artificial limbs (e.g., Ottobock bebionic hand) where surface EMG drives a pattern-recognition classifier. - **Ergonomics & Occupational Biomechanics** — Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorder (WRMSD) prevention; assessing muscle load in industrial workers. - **Sports Science** — biomechanical analysis of muscle activation timing, fatigue monitoring via median frequency. - **Brain–Computer Interface (BCI) Augmentation** — hybrid BCI systems that combine EEG + EMG for improved control. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Value of an EMG Signal (Step-by-Step) > **Aim:** Show that the RMS amplitude of an EMG burst is the physiologically meaningful metric that correlates with muscle force production. **Step 1 — Definition of RMS for a periodic signal $v(t)$ over a window of duration $T$:** $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute a generic EMG MUAP burst, modelled as a discrete sum of $N$ MUAPs occurring at times $t_i$ with amplitudes $A_i$:** $$v(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{i}\, \phi(t - t_{i})$$ where $\phi(t)$ is a triphasic MUAP template. **Step 3 — Insert into the integral:** $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{i}\phi(t - t_{i})\right]^{2}dt}$$ **Step 4 — Expand the squared sum:** $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{i}^{2}\phi^{2}(t-t_{i}) + 2\!\!\sum_{i **Aim:** Show mathematically why a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier is mandatory for EMG. **Step 1 — Define the two electrode potentials, each contaminated by a common-mode mains interference $V_{cm}$:** $$V_{+} = V_{EMG} + V_{cm} \quad ; \quad V_{-} = -V_{EMG} + V_{cm}$$ (The differential EMG is symmetric; the 50 Hz pickup is common to both.) **Step 2 — The instrumentation amplifier produces:** $$V_{out} = A_{d}(V_{+} - V_{-}) + A_{c}\!\left(\frac{V_{+} + V_{-}}{2}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$V_{out} = A_{d}(2V_{EMG}) + A_{c}\!\left(\frac{2V_{cm}}{2}\right) = 2A_{d}V_{EMG} + A_{c}V_{cm}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to expose the desired signal and the interference:** $$V_{out} = 2A_{d}\!\left[V_{EMG} + \frac{V_{cm}}{CMRR_{linear}}\right]$$ where the *linear* CMRR is $\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}$. **Step 5 — Convert to dB and substitute a typical specification:** $$CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right) = 100 \text{ dB} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} = 10^{5}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical demonstration. If $V_{EMG} = 1 \text{ mV}$ and $V_{cm} = 1 \text{ V}$ (typical mains pickup):** $$V_{interference} = \frac{1\text{ V}}{10^{5}} = 10\,\mu\text{V}$$ So a 100 dB CMRR amplifier reduces a **1 V** interference to a residual **10 µV** — well below the EMG signal level. > **Examiner's note:** This is the single most-valuated numerical example in KTU biopotential-amplifier questions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Why the EMG Band is 20 Hz – 500 Hz (Filter Sizing) **Step 1 — Lower cutoff (High-Pass Filter, HPF):** - Removes the **DC half-cell potential** of the Ag/AgCl electrode (up to $\pm$ 200 mV offset). - Removes **motion artifacts** caused by electrode-skin interface movement, which dominate the spectrum below ~20 Hz. - Therefore $f_{HPF} \approx$ **20 Hz**. **Step 2 — Upper cutoff (Low-Pass Filter, LPF):** - The fastest meaningful MUAP component is the sharp spike transition, with rise times of $\sim$ 1 ms. - Applying the rule of thumb $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{0.35}{t_{r}}$: $$f_{max} = \frac{0.35}{1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}} = 350 \text{ Hz}$$ - To allow a margin for the steepest action-potential components, the KTU-specified LPF is **500 Hz**. **Step 3 — Notch filter for power-line interference (India):** - Centre frequency $f_{notch} =$ **50 Hz** (with a quality factor $Q \approx 10$ so that the notch is narrow enough to preserve EMG spectral content in the 40 – 60 Hz region). **Final band specification:** **20 Hz – 500 Hz** with a **50 Hz notch**. KTU students must state these three numbers explicitly to earn full marks. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — EMG Modality Comparison | Property | Surface EMG (sEMG) | Concentric Needle EMG | Single-Fiber EMG (SFEMG) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (skin puncture) | Invasive (special needle) | | **Pickup Volume** | Large (mm²–cm²) | Small (mm³) | Very small (≤ 300 µm) | | **MUAP Selectivity** | Multiple MUs summed | 1 – few MUs | Single muscle fiber | | **Typical Amplitude** | 0.1 – 5 mV | 0.1 – 2 mV | 0.1 – 10 mV | | **Bandwidth Used** | 20 – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz | 500 Hz – 10 kHz | | **Clinical Use** | Ergonomics, rehab, prosthetics | Routine neuropathies | NMJ disorders (myasthenia gravis) | | **Pain / Risk** | None | Mild discomfort, infection risk | Mild, specialist only | | **KTU Weightage** | **High** | **High** | Medium | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete EMG Acquisition System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *signal flow* and *functional interactions* of every stage in a KTU-spec EMG instrumentation chain — from the motor unit inside the patient all the way to the digital display. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[alphaMotoneuron
Spinal Cord] -->|Axonal AP| B[Nerve Axon] B -->|ACh Release| C[Neuromuscular Junction] C -->|Muscle Fiber AP| D[Skeletal Muscle Fibers] D -->|Extrajacellular Field| E[Recording Electrode
Ag/AgCl or Needle] E -->|Ionic to Electronic| F[Electrode-Skin Interface
Half Cell Potential] F -->|Differential Signal| G[Instrumentation Amplifier
INA128 Gain 1000] G -->|Amplified EMG| H[Bandpass Filter
20 Hz to 500 Hz] H -->|Filtered EMG| I[Notch Filter
50 Hz Rejection] I -->|Clean EMG| J[Main Amplifier
Final Gain] J -->|0 to 5 V Range| K[Analog to Digital Converter
1 kSps Minimum] K -->|Digital Samples| L[Digital Signal Processing
RMS and ARV] L -->|Processed Output| M[Display and Storage
PC or LCD] N[Power Line 50 Hz] -.->|Common Mode Interference| G O[Motion Artifact] -.->|Low Frequency Noise| H P[EMG Crosstalk] -.->|Adjacent Muscle| E ``` ### Reading Guide for the Diagram - **Solid arrows** represent the *forward signal path* from the patient to the screen. - **Dashed arrows** represent *noise / artifact injection points* — these are exactly the places where KTU examiners ask students to justify filter and amplifier choices. - The block labelled `Instrumentation Amplifier` is the **single most important block** — it is where differential sensing and CMRR are achieved. - The `Notch Filter` block is the *Indian-mains-specific* element; in US/Europe it would be centred at 60 Hz. --- ## 4.2 Signal-Processing Pipeline for Muscle-Fatigue Analysis (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TD rawEMG[Raw EMG Burst] --> rect1[Full Wave Rectifier] rect1 --> lowpass1[Low Pass Smoothing Filter] lowpass1 --> envelope[Linear Envelope] rawEMG --> fft[FFT Block Size 1024] fft --> psd[Power Spectral Density] psd --> median[Median Frequency Calculator] psd --> mean[Mean Frequency Calculator] envelope --> rmsOut[RMS Amplitude Output] median --> fatigueIndex[Muscle Fatigue Index] mean --> fatigueIndex rmsOut --> fatigueIndex ``` ### Why This Pipeline Matters (Engineering Reasoning) - **Rectification → Envelope** gives a positive-only waveform whose amplitude tracks the *level of muscle activation* — perfect for prosthetic-control thresholds. - **FFT → Median / Mean Frequency** is the gold-standard fatigue index: as the muscle fatigues, conduction velocity slows, the spectrum compresses, and the **median frequency drops monotonically**. KTU examiners love this — it is the classic "applied" question. - A median-frequency shift from ~80 Hz to ~50 Hz over a sustained contraction is the textbook signature of **peripheral muscular fatigue**. --- ## 4.3 Recruitment Pattern (Henneman's Size Principle) — Conceptual Block ```mermaid flowchart LR lowForce[Low Force Demand] --> smallMU[Small Type I MUs
Slow Fatigue Resistant] smallMU --> firstRecruit[First Recruited] medForce[Medium Force Demand] --> typeIIA[Medium Type IIA MUs] typeIIA --> secondRecruit[Second Recruited] highForce[High Force Demand] --> typeIIB[Large Type IIB MUs
Fast Fatigable] typeIIB --> lastRecruit[Last Recruited] firstRecruit --> rateCode1[Firing Rate 6 to 15 Hz] secondRecruit --> rateCode2[Firing Rate 15 to 25 Hz] lastRecruit --> rateCode3[Firing Rate 25 to 60 Hz] ``` This block diagram is the visual companion to **Henneman's size principle** — the order in which motor units are brought online as voluntary force increases. It is a 7-mark favourite in KTU 2024 Scheme long-answer questions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. Define Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP). Mention its typical amplitude and duration. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the spatiotemporal summation of the action potentials of all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit, recorded extracellularly. **(1 mark)** - It is a triphasic (or biphasic) waveform representing depolarization–repolarization of the underlying muscle fibers. **(1 mark)** - Typical values: **Amplitude = 0.1 – 5 mV** (surface) and **Duration = 5 – 20 ms**. **(1 mark)** ### Q2. State the importance of using a differential amplifier in EMG recording. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The EMG signal is small (µV – mV range) and is contaminated by large 50 Hz common-mode power-line interference. **(1 mark)** - A **3-op-amp instrumentation (differential) amplifier** amplifies the *difference* between the two electrode potentials while rejecting the common-mode signal. **(1 mark)** - The figure of merit is **CMRR**, which must be **> 100 dB** for adequate rejection. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **Q(A). (a)** Explain the origin and characteristics of the EMG signal. With a neat block diagram, describe the complete EMG acquisition system. **(7 marks)** **Q(A). (b)** Define CMRR. Derive the relationship between differential gain, common-mode gain, and CMRR in dB. Why is high CMRR essential for EMG amplifiers? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q(A)(a) (7 marks) 1. **Origin (2 marks):** The EMG signal originates from the depolarization of skeletal muscle fibers when triggered by an action potential arriving at the neuromuscular junction. The summed extracellular field of all fibers of one motor unit forms the MUAP; the summed MUAPs of many active units form the interference EMG. 2. **Characteristics (2 marks):** Amplitude 0.1–5 mV; frequency content 20–500 Hz; duration of single MUAP 5–20 ms; non-stationary stochastic signal. 3. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Electrode → Instrumentation Amplifier (Gain 1000, CMRR > 100 dB) → Bandpass Filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz) → 50 Hz Notch Filter → Main Amplifier → ADC (≥ 1 kSps) → DSP → Display. #### Model Solution — Q(A)(b) (7 marks) 1. **Definition of CMRR (1 mark):** $$CMRR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)$$ 2. **Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from $V_{out} = A_{d}(V_{+}-V_{-}) + A_{c}\!\left(\frac{V_{+}+V_{-}}{2}\right)$ and substituting $V_{+} = V_{EMG} + V_{cm}$, $V_{-} = -V_{EMG} + V_{cm}$, the result is: $$V_{out} = 2A_{d}V_{EMG} + A_{c}V_{cm}$$ The ratio of desired to undesired output, expressed in dB, gives the CMRR formula. 3. **Why essential (3 marks):** A 100 dB CMRR reduces a 1 V mains pickup to a residual of 10 µV — comparable to the EMG level. Without high CMRR, the EMG would be buried in interference. Therefore CMRR > 100 dB is mandatory for clinical-grade EMG amplifiers. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **Q(B). (a)** Compare surface EMG and needle EMG with respect to invasiveness, pickup volume, amplitude, bandwidth, and clinical application. **(7 marks)** **Q(B). (b)** Explain Henneman's size principle. How does the EMG interference pattern change with increasing voluntary force? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q(B)(a) (7 marks) | Criterion | Surface EMG | Needle EMG | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (skin puncture) | | **Pickup Volume** | Large (many motor units) | Small (1 – few motor units) | | **Amplitude** | 0.1 – 5 mV | 0.1 – 2 mV (but cleaner) | | **Bandwidth** | 20 – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz | | **Application** | Ergonomics, prosthetics, rehab | Clinical neuropathies, myopathies | | **Mark Split** | (3 marks) | (3 marks) | | **Conclusion** | (1 mark) — surface is for functional use; needle is for diagnostic precision | | #### Model Solution — Q(B)(b) (7 marks) 1. **Henneman's Size Principle (3 marks):** Motor units are recruited in order of increasing motoneuron size — **small, slow, fatigue-resistant Type I** units are recruited first at low force levels; **large, fast, fatigable Type II** units are recruited last at high force levels. 2. **Rate Coding (2 marks):** As force demand increases further, already-recruited units *increase their firing rate* (from ~6 Hz to ~30 Hz) in addition to new recruitment. 3. **Interference Pattern Evolution (2 marks):** At low force → *single-unit pattern* (discrete MUAPs visible); at moderate force → *intermediate pattern* (some overlap); at high force → *full interference pattern* (continuous, dense, no baseline visible). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > - ❌ Writing **"EMG measures muscle force"** — **WRONG.** EMG measures *electrical activity*; force is inferred. > - ❌ Forgetting to convert CMRR ratio to **dB** using $20\log_{10}$ — KTU deducts 1 mark. > - ❌ Stating the EMG band as **0 – 500 Hz** — the **DC half-cell potential** must be blocked, so the *low-frequency cutoff is 20 Hz*, not 0. > - ❌ Using **60 Hz notch** in an Indian KTU paper — the Indian power-line frequency is **50 Hz**. > - ❌ Confusing **MUAP** (single motor unit) with **interference pattern** (sum of many MUAPs) — these are different signals. > - ❌ Forgetting to mention that the electrode-skin interface generates a **DC half-cell potential** that must be removed by the high-pass filter. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam** - ✅ EMG = recording of skeletal-muscle bioelectric activity; **NOT** a direct measure of force. - ✅ The **Motor Unit (MU)** is the fundamental signal source = 1 motoneuron + all its innervated fibers. - ✅ **MUAP amplitude = 0.1 – 5 mV** ; **duration = 5 – 20 ms** ; **band = 20 – 500 Hz**. - ✅ **EMG instrumentation chain:** Electrode → Instrumentation Amplifier → Bandpass (20–500 Hz) → 50 Hz Notch → Main Amplifier → ADC → DSP. - ✅ **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** is mandatory; **CMRR > 100 dB** is the key spec. - ✅ **CMRR formula:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_{d}/A_{c})$. - ✅ **Henneman's Size Principle:** Small (Type I) MUs are recruited first; large (Type II) MUs are recruited last. - ✅ **Rate coding** complements recruitment for force gradation. - ✅ **Indian power-line frequency = 50 Hz** — use a 50 Hz notch filter. - ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** Sampling rate ≥ 2 × highest signal frequency → typically **≥ 1 kSps**. - ✅ **Surface EMG** → non-invasive, summed signal, ergonomics/prosthetics use. - ✅ **Needle EMG** → invasive, single-MU selectivity, clinical diagnosis use. - ✅ **Single-Fiber EMG (SFEMG)** → diagnostic of neuromuscular-junction disorders (myasthenia gravis). - ✅ **RMS amplitude** correlates with muscle activation level; **median frequency** drops with fatigue. - ✅ **Half-cell potential** of Ag/AgCl electrode = **+0.222 V**; blocked by the 20 Hz HPF. - ✅ **Common artifacts:** 50 Hz interference, motion artifact (<20 Hz), ECG crosstalk, electrode-skin polarization drift. - ✅ **Myoelectric control** = using surface EMG to drive prosthetic limbs via pattern recognition. - ✅ **Interference pattern** = full overlap of many MUAPs at high force; **single-unit pattern** = discrete MUAPs at low force.

topics.title: Electrical Activity of Muscles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Activity of Muscles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **electrical activity of muscles** refers to the bioelectric phenomena generated by excitable muscle tissue (skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle) arising from the dynamic redistribution of ions across the sarcolemma (muscle cell membrane). This activity encompasses three primary measurable manifestations:

1. **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** – A steady-state potential difference (typically **−70 mV to −90 mV** for skeletal muscle) maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and selective ion permeability.
2. **Muscle Fiber Action Potential (MFAP)** – A transient, all-or-none depolarization-repolarization event (amplitude ≈ **+30 mV to +40 mV**, duration **1–5 ms**) that propagates along the sarcolemma and down the T-tubules.
3. **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** – The spatially and temporally summated extracellular recording of all muscle fiber action potentials within a single motor unit, which forms the basis of clinical **Electromyography (EMG)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** Electrical activity of muscles is a foundational topic in biomedical signal acquisition. It directly precedes the study of EMG electrodes, signal conditioning circuits, and biosensor transducer interfacing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **leaky water balloon filled with salty water and floating in salty water**:

- The balloon's rubber skin is the **sarcolemma** (the membrane).
- The salts inside and outside are different concentrations — the inside is rich in **potassium (K⁺)**, the outside in **sodium (Na⁺)** and **chloride (Cl⁻)**.
- Because of this imbalance, a small "leak voltage" sits across the skin — this is the **Resting Membrane Potential** (~−80 mV, inside negative).
- When the muscle receives a command from a nerve (like the **motor neuron** sending acetylcholine), gates in the membrane **slam open** — Na⁺ rushes in, the voltage spikes to +30 mV (this is the **action potential**).
- The voltage then resets: K⁺ rushes out, and the Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores the original balance.
- A **motor unit** is like a *foreman + workers*: one nerve cell (the foreman, "alpha motor neuron") commands a small team of muscle fibers (the workers). When the foreman shouts, every worker fires — the combined electrical shout, picked up by skin or needle electrodes, is the **MUAP**.

### 1.3 Hierarchy of Muscular Electrical Signals

| Signal Level | Source | Typical Amplitude | Typical Duration | Bandwidth |
|-------------|--------|------------------|------------------|-----------|
| RMP | Single fiber | −80 mV (intracellular) | Continuous | DC |
| MFAP | Single fiber | −90 to +30 mV (intracellular) | 1–5 ms | DC–10 kHz |
| MUAP | One motor unit | 50 µV – 5 mV (surface) | 3–15 ms | 20 Hz – 5 kHz |
| EMG (surface) | Many motor units | 50 µV – 5 mV | Continuous | 20–500 Hz |
| ECG (cardiac) | Whole heart | 1–5 mV (surface) | ~1 s (QRS) | 0.05–150 Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The frequency content of EMG signals (**20 Hz – 500 Hz** dominant) directly dictates the **anti-aliasing filter cut-off**, the **electrode–skin impedance requirements**, and the **noise specification (50/60 Hz notch filters)** in any biosensor design interfacing with muscles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physiological Cascade: From Nerve Command to Muscle Contraction

The complete electromechanical chain proceeds in **four well-defined stages**:

1. **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ) Transmission**
   - The somatic motor neuron releases **acetylcholine (ACh)** into the synaptic cleft.
   - ACh binds **nicotinic receptors** on the motor end-plate.
   - This opens ligand-gated Na⁺ channels → **End-Plate Potential (EPP)** of ~−40 mV.

2. **Sarcolemmal Depolarization (Triggering the Action Potential)**
   - If EPP exceeds the threshold (~−55 mV), **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open (fast, regenerative).
   - Na⁺ influx drives membrane potential toward the **Nernst potential for Na⁺** (~+60 mV).
   - Na⁺ channels inactivate within ~1 ms; **voltage-gated K⁺ channels** open.
   - K⁺ efflux repolarizes the membrane toward the **Nernst potential for K⁺** (~−90 mV).
   - Brief hyperpolarization (**after-potential**) precedes return to RMP.

3. **Excitation–Contraction (E–C) Coupling**
   - The action potential propagates down the **T-tubules**.
   - **Dihydropyridine (DHPR) receptors** in the T-tubule sense voltage change and mechanically open **Ryanodine Receptors (RyR)** on the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
   - Ca²⁺ flows out of the SR into the sarcoplasm.
   - Ca²⁺ binds **troponin C** → tropomyosin shifts → myosin heads bind actin → **cross-bridge cycling** (contraction).

4. **Relaxation**
   - **SERCA pump** (Sarcoplasmic/Endoplasmic Reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase) actively transports Ca²⁺ back into the SR.
   - Troponin releases Ca²⁺ → tropomyosin re-covers actin's myosin-binding sites → muscle relaxes.

### 2.2 The Motor Unit — The Fundamental Functional Unit

A **motor unit (MU)** consists of:
- **One alpha motor neuron** (cell body in the spinal cord ventral horn).
- **All the muscle fibers it innervates** (typically 5–2000 fibers, depending on muscle precision needs).

| Muscle Type | Typical Fibers per MU | Functional Purpose |
|------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Extraocular (eye) | 5–10 | Fine precision |
| Biceps brachii | 100–200 | Medium control |
| Quadriceps (gastrocnemius) | 1000–2000 | Gross force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **size principle** (Henneman, 1965): Motor units are recruited in order of size — small (Type I, slow, fatigue-resistant) units are activated first for low-force tasks; large (Type II, fast) units are recruited only for high-force demands.

### 2.3 The Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) — Anatomy of the Signal

The MUAP is the **sum of single-fiber action potentials** that arrive nearly synchronously at the recording electrode. Its shape is determined by:

- **Fiber diameter** (larger fibers → faster conduction velocity).
- **Distance from electrode** to active fibers (signal attenuates as **1/r²** for monopolar).
- **Temporal dispersion** of fiber firing.
- **End-plate and tendon geometry**.

Standard MUAP parameters used clinically:

- **Amplitude:** 50 µV – 5 mV (surface); up to 10 mV (needle).
- **Duration:** 3–15 ms.
- **Phases:** Number of baseline crossings (normal = 2–4 phases).
- **Rise time:** <500 µs (indicates proximity of electrode to firing fibers).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Standard Symbols | Units / Typical Value |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Nernst equation (equilibrium potential for a single permeant ion) | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temp (K), $F = 96485$ C/mol, $z$ = ion valence | mV |
| 2 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation for resting membrane | $P_i$ = permeability of ion $i$ | mV |
| 3 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{d \cdot I_m}{2 \cdot r_i \cdot C_m}}$ | Conduction velocity in muscle fiber (cable properties) | $d$ = fiber diameter, $I_m$ = membrane current density, $r_i$ = intracellular resistivity, $C_m$ = membrane capacitance | m/s |
| 4 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | EMG high-pass filter corner (electrode–skin interface) | $R$ = input resistance, $C$ = coupling capacitance | Hz |
| 5 | $V_{recorded} = \dfrac{V_{source}}{1 + j\omega R C}$ | Voltage divider model of electrode–skin impedance | $R, C$ = skin impedance | — |
| 6 | $P_{tetanus} = f \cdot n$ | Fusion frequency for tetanic contraction (frequency × fibers) | $f$ = stimulation freq, $n$ = fibers | Hz |
| 7 | $F = k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i$ | Total muscle force (linear summation) | $k$ = constant, $A_i$ = active MUAP area | N |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (used in EMG amplifier design) | $V_{signal}$, $V_{noise}$ = RMS voltages | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway:** The Nernst and GHK equations underpin the choice of electrolyte composition in **biopotential electrodes** (e.g., Ag/AgCl electrode's KCl gel mimics intracellular K⁺ concentration to stabilize the half-cell potential at **+222 mV** vs. SHE).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Muscle Electrical Activity Knowledge |
|------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Clinical EMG diagnostics** | Detect neuropathy, myopathy, ALS, myasthenia gravis |
| **Prosthetic limb control** | Surface EMG (sEMG) drives servos in myoelectric arms (e.g., DEKA, Ottobock) |
| **Rehabilitation robotics** | Exosuits (ReWalk, Ekso) use sEMG-triggered assistance |
| **HCI / Gaming** | Gesture recognition via forearm EMG armbands (Thalmic Labs / North) |
| **Sports science** | Muscle fatigue analysis via median frequency shift of EMG spectrum |
| **Biofeedback therapy** | Stress incontinence, stroke rehabilitation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

We start from the **electrochemical potential** of an ion across a membrane. At equilibrium, the chemical potential gradient is exactly balanced by the electrical potential gradient.

**Step 1 — Chemical potential of an ideal solution:**

The chemical potential of ion species $i$ at concentration $[i]$ is:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{0} + RT \ln [i]$$

This expresses how the free energy of the ion changes with its concentration (logarithmic dependence, from statistical mechanics).

**Step 2 — Electrical potential energy of a charged ion in an electric field:**

For an ion with valence $z$ in a membrane potential $E$:

$$\mu_{elec} = z F E$$

**Step 3 — Total electrochemical potential on each side:**

On the **outside (o)** and **inside (i)** of the membrane:

$$\mu_{total}^{o} = \mu_i^{0} + RT \ln [i]_o + z F E_o$$

$$\mu_{total}^{i} = \mu_i^{0} + RT \ln [i]_i + z F E_i$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium condition:**

At equilibrium, $\mu_{total}^{o} = \mu_{total}^{i}$ (no net driving force). Subtract the two equations:

$$RT \ln [i]_o + z F E_o = RT \ln [i]_i + z F E_i$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the potential difference $E = E_i - E_o$:**

$$z F (E_i - E_o) = RT \ln [i]_o - RT \ln [i]_i$$

$$z F \cdot E = RT \ln \dfrac{[i]_o}{[i]_i}$$

**Step 6 — Final form of the Nernst Equation:**

$$E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[i]_o}{[i]_i}$$

This is the **Nernst potential** — the membrane voltage at which there is zero net flux of ion $i$.

**Step 7 — At body temperature ($T = 310$ K, i.e., 37°C), convert to base-10 logarithm:**

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$ and $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.7$ mV at 310 K:

$$E \approx \dfrac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[i]_o}{[i]_i}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Computing the K⁺ Nernst Potential

**Given** (typical skeletal muscle intracellular vs. extracellular concentrations):

- $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM (intracellular)
- $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM (extracellular)
- $T = 310$ K (body temperature)
- $z = +1$ (monovalent cation)

**Step 1 — Apply the Nernst equation in base-10 form:**

$$E_K = \dfrac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{+1} \log_{10} \dfrac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the concentration ratio:**

$$\dfrac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i} = \dfrac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = \log_{10}(2.857 \times 10^{-2}) = \log_{10}(2.857) - 2 = 0.4559 - 2 = -1.5441$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 61.5 mV:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.5441) = -94.96 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The K⁺ Nernst potential of approximately **−95 mV** is close to (but slightly more negative than) the measured resting membrane potential of −80 mV. This is because the resting membrane is **not exclusively permeable to K⁺** — there is some leak permeability to Na⁺, which depolarizes the membrane slightly. The **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** (Equation 2 in the cheat sheet) accounts for this.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Conduction Velocity Derivation from Cable Theory

The **propagation velocity** of a muscle action potential is derived from the **cable equation** for an excitable cylinder:

**Step 1 — Cable equation (one-dimensional):**

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = r_i \, r_m \, C_m \, \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + r_i \, I_m$$

where $r_i$ = axial resistance per unit length, $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length, $C_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit length, $I_m$ = membrane ionic current.

**Step 2 — Assume a traveling wave solution:** $V_m(x,t) = f(x - vt)$ (the action potential moves at constant speed $v$).

**Step 3 — Through the Huxley (1959) approximation**, the conduction velocity for a propagating action potential in a uniform cylindrical fiber is:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{2}} \cdot \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{g_{Na,max} \cdot V_{threshold}}{r_i \cdot C_m^2}}$$

where $d$ = fiber diameter, $g_{Na,max}$ = peak Na⁺ conductance, $V_{threshold}$ = threshold voltage.

**Step 4 — Practical consequence (the diameter-velocity rule):**

Since $v \propto \sqrt{d}$:

- A small motor axon (5 µm) → velocity ≈ 5–15 m/s.
- A large motor axon (20 µm) → velocity ≈ 80–120 m/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Biosensor Design:** Knowing conduction velocity helps determine the **temporal dispersion** observed in surface EMG — slower fibers arrive later, broadening the MUAP and lowering its dominant frequency.

### 3.4 Summary of Derivations

| Derivation | Governing Principle | Final Expression | Key Assumption |
|------------|--------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Nernst Potential | Thermodynamic equilibrium | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[i]_o}{[i]_i}$ | Single permeant ion, ideal solution |
| GHK Voltage | Constant-field approximation | See cheat sheet | Multiple ions, constant electric field |
| Conduction Velocity | Cable + Hodgkin-Huxley | $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ | Uniform fiber, no branching |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Electrophysiological Cascade of Muscle Activation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CORTICAL COMMAND"] --> B["ALPHA MOTOR NEURON"]
    B --> C["AXON ACTION POTENTIAL"]
    C --> D["NEUROMUSCULAR JUNCTION"]
    D --> E["END PLATE POTENTIAL"]
    E --> F["SARCOLEMMA DEPOLARIZATION"]
    F --> G["T TUBULE PROPAGATION"]
    G --> H["DHPR RYANODINE COUPLING"]
    H --> I["CA2+ RELEASE FROM SR"]
    I --> J["ACTIN MYOSIN CROSS BRIDGE"]
    J --> K["MUSCLE CONTRACTION"]
    K --> L["FORCE GENERATION"]
    B1["SENSORY FEEDBACK"] --> A
    L --> B1
```

> *Figure 1 — The closed-loop cascade from cortical intent to muscle force, with sensory feedback. This is the conceptual map for Module 2.*

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Architecture of a Typical EMG Acquisition System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["MUSCLE FIBER AP SOURCE"] --> A2["ELECTRODE TISSUE INTERFACE"]
    A2 --> A3["DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER STAGE 1 GAIN 100"]
    A3 --> A4["BANDPASS FILTER 20 HZ TO 500 HZ"]
    A4 --> A5["NOTCH FILTER 50 HZ OR 60 HZ"]
    A5 --> A6["SECOND AMPLIFIER STAGE GAIN 10 TO 50"]
    A6 --> A7["ADC SAMPLING AT 1 KHZ TO 4 KHZ"]
    A7 --> A8["DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING"]
    A8 --> A9["DISPLAY OR STORAGE"]
    A3 -.-> AGND["DRIVEN RIGHT LEG GROUND"]
    AGND -.-> A2
```

> *Figure 2 — Block-level functional architecture of an EMG biosensor system. Each block corresponds to a signal-conditioning stage grounded in the muscle electrical properties described above.*

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Motor Unit Structure and Recruitment Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SPINAL CORD VENTRAL HORN"] --> N1["ALPHA MOTOR NEURON SMALL TYPE I SLOW"]
    S1 --> N2["ALPHA MOTOR NEURON MEDIUM TYPE IIA FAST OXIDATIVE"]
    S1 --> N3["ALPHA MOTOR NEURON LARGE TYPE IIB FAST GLYCOLYTIC"]
    N1 --> F1["5 TO 10 SLOW FATIGUE RESISTANT FIBERS"]
    N2 --> F2["100 TO 300 INTERMEDIATE FIBERS"]
    N3 --> F3["500 TO 2000 FAST FIBERS"]
    F1 --> MU["MOTOR UNIT ACTION POTENTIAL MUAP"]
    F2 --> MU
    F3 --> MU
    MU --> EMGSIG["SUMMATED EMG SIGNAL"]
    EMGSIG --> ELEC["SURFACE OR NEEDLE ELECTRODE"]
```

> *Figure 3 — Motor unit types, their recruitment order (smallest to largest — Henneman's size principle), and their summated electrical output captured by electrodes.*

### 4.4 Block Diagram: EMG Signal Frequency Spectrum and Filter Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUB["SUBCUTANEOUS NOISE DC DRIFT"] -->|HPF 20 HZ| SIG
    EMG["RAW EMG 20 HZ TO 500 HZ"] -->|BPF 20 500| SIG["FILTERED EMG"]
    MAINS["POWERLINE 50 HZ OR 60 HZ"] -->|NOTCH| SIG
    EMGNOISE["HIGH FREQ THERMAL NOISE ABOVE 1 KHZ"] -->|LPF| SIG
    SIG --> OUT["CLEAN EMG FOR ANALYSIS"]
```

> *Figure 4 — Why the **20 Hz – 500 Hz bandpass** is non-negotiable: it removes baseline wander, eliminates 50/60 Hz interference, and rejects high-frequency thermal noise, leaving the diagnostically valuable MUAP energy.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Motor Unit" and state its components.**

**Model Answer:**
A **motor unit (MU)** is the fundamental functional unit of voluntary muscle contraction, consisting of:
1. **One alpha motor neuron** (cell body in the ventral horn of the spinal cord, axon projecting to the muscle).
2. **All the muscle fibers innervated by that single motor neuron** (varying from 5–10 in extraocular muscles to 1000–2000 in large postural muscles).
3. The **neuromuscular junctions** (synapses) between the neuron's terminal branches and each fiber.
When the motor neuron fires, **all the fibers in the motor unit contract simultaneously**, producing a summed electrical signal called the **motor unit action potential (MUAP)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Write a short note on the Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) of a skeletal muscle fiber.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** of a skeletal muscle fiber is the steady-state voltage difference (inside negative) across the sarcolemma when the cell is not active. Typical value: **−70 mV to −90 mV** (often cited as **−80 mV**). It is generated by:
- The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle, using ATP) — actively maintaining ionic gradients.
- **Differential permeability**: at rest, the membrane is ~25× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺, so the membrane potential lies closer to the K⁺ Nernst potential (~−95 mV) than to the Na⁺ Nernst potential (~+60 mV).
- **The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** quantifies the RMP using the relative permeabilities of K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete **electrophysiological pathway** from a cortical motor command to the generation of a muscle action potential. Discuss the role of acetylcholine and the neuromuscular junction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for a permeant ion across a biological membrane. Using this equation, calculate the **Nernst potential for K⁺** in a skeletal muscle fiber given $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM at 37°C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electrophysiological pathway (7 marks):**
The block diagram is shown in **Figure 1 (Section 4.1)**. Key stages to be written:

1. **Motor cortex** generates command → descends via **corticospinal tract**.
2. Reaches **alpha motor neuron** in ventral horn of spinal cord.
3. **Axon hillock** integrates synaptic inputs; if threshold is reached, an **axon action potential** is initiated.
4. The AP travels along the **motor axon** to the muscle.
5. At the **neuromuscular junction (NMJ)**: voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open in the presynaptic terminal → Ca²⁺ influx → synaptic vesicles fuse with the presynaptic membrane → release of **acetylcholine (ACh)** into the synaptic cleft.
6. ACh diffuses across the cleft (~50 nm) and binds to **nicotinic ACh receptors** (ligand-gated Na⁺/K⁺ channels) on the **motor end-plate**.
7. Opening of these channels → end-plate potential (EPP, ~−40 mV, local and graded).
8. If EPP exceeds ~−55 mV at the surrounding sarcolemma, **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open → **muscle fiber action potential** propagates in both directions from the end-plate.
9. AP travels down the **T-tubules** → triggers Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (excitation-contraction coupling).

*Allocation (7 marks): 1 mark each for stages 1–5, 1 mark for ACh release mechanism, 1 mark for end-plate potential generation and AP initiation.*

**(b) Nernst Equation Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** above (1 mark per step × 6 steps = 6 marks).

**Numerical calculation (1 mark):**

$$E_K = \dfrac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10} \dfrac{4}{140} = 61.5 \times (-1.5441) \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$

Conclusion: The K⁺ equilibrium potential of **−95 mV** is more negative than the measured RMP of −80 mV, confirming that the membrane at rest is permeable to other ions (mainly Na⁺ leak) that depolarize it from the pure K⁺ equilibrium.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** What is a **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**? Describe its **clinical parameters** (amplitude, duration, phases, rise time) and the factors affecting its shape in surface and needle EMG recordings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **complete EMG signal acquisition chain** with a block diagram, specifying the purpose of each stage: differential amplification, bandpass filtering (20–500 Hz), notch filtering, and ADC sampling. Justify the choice of **sampling frequency ≥ 1 kHz** using the **Nyquist criterion**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MUAP Definition and Parameters (7 marks):**
A **MUAP** is the spatial and temporal summation of action potentials from all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit, recorded extracellularly.

| Parameter | Surface EMG | Needle EMG | Clinical Significance |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Amplitude | 50 µV – 5 mV | 100 µV – 10 mV | Loss of motor units (e.g., neuropathy) → ↑ amplitude of remaining units |
| Duration | 5–15 ms | 3–15 ms | Polyphasic or prolonged → myopathy, reinnervation |
| Phases | 2–4 (normal) | ≤ 4 | > 4 phases → "polyphasic" → neuromuscular pathology |
| Rise time | > 500 µs (far) | < 500 µs (close) | Short rise time → electrode near firing fibers |

*Factors affecting shape:*
- Fiber diameter (larger → faster conduction → shorter MUAP).
- Distance from recording electrode (1/r² attenuation).
- Temporal dispersion (firing asynchrony of fibers).
- End-plate location relative to electrode.
- Volume conductor properties (skin, fat thickness).

*Allocation: 2 marks definition + table, 3 marks factors, 2 marks clinical interpretation.*

**(b) EMG Acquisition Chain (7 marks):**
Block diagram from **Figure 2 (Section 4.2)**.

| Stage | Purpose | Typical Specification |
|-------|---------|----------------------|
| Electrode–skin interface | Transduce ionic current in tissue to electron current in wire | Ag/AgCl electrodes; skin prep with alcohol abrasion; impedance < 5 kΩ |
| Differential amplifier (Stage 1) | Reject common-mode noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz); high CMRR (> 100 dB); high input impedance (> 10¹² Ω) | Gain 100–1000, INA128 or AD8221 |
| Bandpass filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz) | Remove DC drift (< 20 Hz) and high-frequency thermal noise (> 500 Hz); preserve MUAP energy | 2nd-order Butterworth; HPF 20 Hz, LPF 500 Hz |
| Notch filter (50/60 Hz) | Suppress powerline interference; minimal impact on EMG spectrum | Q-factor 5–10; tunable |
| Stage 2 amplifier | Bring signal into ADC input range (0–5 V) | Gain 10–50 |
| ADC | Convert analog EMG to digital | Sampling rate ≥ 1 kHz (Nyquist), resolution ≥ 12-bit |
| DSP / Display | Feature extraction (RMS, MAV, median frequency) | Software or firmware |

**Nyquist Justification (1 mark):**

The highest significant frequency component in surface EMG is **~500 Hz**. According to the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the maximum signal frequency:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

A practical design uses $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ (often 2 kHz or 4 kHz) to allow for **anti-aliasing filter roll-off** and **oversampling for noise reduction**.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Confusing MFAP and MUAP** — MFAP is from *one* fiber (intracellular); MUAP is from *one motor unit* (extracellular). Examiners specifically test this distinction. **Penalty: 1–2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Sign of Nernst potential** — Students often write $|E|$ and forget to evaluate the sign of $\log_{10}$ of the ratio. For K⁺ (out < in), the ratio < 1, so $\log_{10}$ is **negative**, and $E$ is **negative**. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing 61.5 mV constant** — The Nernst equation is correct only if the base-10 logarithm and the $2.303 RT/F$ conversion are used. Writing the natural-log form is fine, but the numerical evaluation **must** use the right factor at the stated temperature. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Sampling frequency = signal frequency** — A recurring error: students equate Nyquist rate with $f_s = f_{max}$. The correct inequality is $f_s > 2 f_{max}$ (strictly). **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 5. **Skipping units** in the formula sheet table. **Penalty: 0.5 mark per cell.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Motor unit** = 1 alpha motor neuron + all its innervated muscle fibers. *(Henneman's size principle: small units recruited first.)*
- ✅ **RMP** of skeletal muscle ≈ **−80 mV**, sustained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in).
- ✅ **Action potential amplitude** ≈ **+30 mV** peak; duration **1–5 ms**; follows **all-or-none** law.
- ✅ **MUAP duration** = **3–15 ms**; **amplitude** = **50 µV – 5 mV** (surface).
- ✅ **Nernst equation** at 37°C: $E \approx \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ mV.
- ✅ **GHK voltage equation** accounts for multi-ion permeability to give the realistic RMP.
- ✅ **Conduction velocity** $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ — larger fibers conduct faster.
- ✅ **EMG bandwidth**: **20 Hz – 500 Hz** → dictates filter design and ADC sampling rate.
- ✅ **Sampling rate (Nyquist)**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; for EMG, **≥ 1 kHz** minimum.
- ✅ **CMRR** of EMG pre-amplifier must be **> 80 dB** (preferably > 100 dB).
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl electrode** is the standard for EMG (low half-cell potential, stable, low noise).
- ✅ **Excitation–Contraction Coupling** is mediated by **DHPR → RyR** mechanical linkage and **Ca²⁺** release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
- ✅ **Motor unit recruitment + rate coding** together determine muscle force.
- ✅ **Surface EMG** sums many MUs → interference pattern; **Needle EMG** isolates single MUs → diagnostic precision.
- ✅ Always state **units** and **signs** in numerical answers; examiners check for both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
