<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromyography (EMG)** is the bioelectrical measurement technique that records the sum of the electrical activity generated by the depolarization of skeletal muscle fibers during voluntary or involuntary contractions. The resulting **Electromyogram (EMG Signal)** is a time-varying, quasi-random, non-stationary voltage waveform representing the spatial and temporal summation of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)**.

The smallest functional contractile unit in EMG analysis is the **Motor Unit (MU)**, which consists of:
- One **alpha motor neuron** in the spinal cord (anterior horn cell)
- Its **axon** and terminal branches
- All the **muscle fibers** innervated by that axon

When the motor neuron fires, an **Action Potential (AP)** propagates along the axon and triggers simultaneous contraction of all muscle fibers in that motor unit, producing a **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard EMG Voltage Range:** **50 µV to 5 mV** (surface EMG)
> **Standard EMG Frequency Range:** **0 Hz to 500 Hz** (dominant energy: **20 Hz to 150 Hz**)
> **Standard EMG Duration:** **3 ms to 25 ms** per MUAP burst

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a sports stadium with 50,000 spectators doing a "wave":
- Each spectator (muscle fiber) standing up creates a tiny visible ripple (action potential).
- A **group of spectators coordinated by one cheerleader (motor neuron)** represents a **Motor Unit**.
- The cheerleader claps her hands → all the spectators in her group stand up in sync = **MUAP**.
- The roar of the entire crowd doing waves at different times = the **raw EMG signal** recorded on the surface electrode.
- A louder roar (stronger muscle contraction) = more cheerleaders clapping = **larger MUAP amplitude**.

The "sound" you record at the stadium microphone is messy, overlapping, and never repeats exactly — that is precisely what an EMG signal looks like on an oscilloscope.

### 1.3 Classification of EMG Signals

| Type | Method | Penetration | Signal Quality |
|------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Surface EMG (sEMG)** | Skin surface electrodes | Non-invasive, superficial muscles | Sum of many MUs, low spatial resolution |
| **Needle EMG (nEMG)** | Inserted needle electrode | Invasive, deep single-fiber recording | Single MU or even single fiber |
| **Fine-wire EMG** | Intramuscular thin wire | Invasive, deep muscles | Intermediate between needle and surface |

> [!NOTE]
> The rest of this module focuses on **Surface EMG (sEMG)** characteristics since it is the most commonly studied signal in biosensor-based prosthetics, rehabilitation, and human–machine interface applications covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Generation of the EMG Signal

The bioelectrical chain leading to an EMG signal follows this sequence:

1. **Central Nervous System (CNS)** sends a command through the corticospinal tract.
2. The **alpha motor neuron** in the anterior horn of the spinal cord fires.
3. The **axon** conducts the action potential to the **neuromuscular junction (NMJ)**.
4. At the NMJ, **Acetylcholine (ACh)** is released, causing **end-plate depolarization** of muscle fibers.
5. Depolarization triggers an **Excitation-Contraction (E-C) Coupling** cascade → **Ca²⁺ release** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
6. **Actin-Myosin cross-bridges** form, producing mechanical force.
7. The simultaneous electrical activity of all fibers in the motor unit is summed in the volume conductor (the body tissue), producing a measurable **MUAP** at the skin surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An EMG electrode does **not** measure mechanical force. It measures the **spatial-temporal superposition of muscle-fiber action potentials** as they propagate along the muscle fibers.

### 2.2 Key Characteristics of the EMG Signal

#### A. Amplitude Characteristics

The peak-to-peak amplitude of an EMG signal is **stochastic** (random) and depends on:

- **Number of active motor units recruited** (motor unit recruitment — Henneman's size principle).
- **Firing rate** of each motor unit (typically **6 to 30 Hz** at low force; up to **60–100 Hz** at high force).
- **Synchronization** between motor units.
- **Distance** between electrode and active muscle fibers (tissue acts as a low-pass filter).
- **Cross-talk** from neighboring muscles.

The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** amplitude is the most accepted envelope detector for EMG because it reflects the signal's *power* and is mathematically tractable for stochastic signals:

$$\text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2}}$$

The **Average Rectified Value (ARV)** is sometimes used as a simpler alternative:

$$\text{EMG}_{\text{ARV}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$$

#### B. Frequency Characteristics

A raw EMG signal is essentially **band-limited noise**. Its Power Spectral Density (PSD) is typically modeled by a function that decays with frequency. Useful spectral features include:

- **Mean Frequency (MNF):** the centroid of the PSD
- **Median Frequency (MDF):** the frequency below which 50% of the total power resides
- **Peak Frequency (f_peak):** the dominant spectral component

#### C. Duration and Stationarity

- A single MUAP lasts **3 to 25 ms**.
- A sustained contraction produces a continuously varying signal.
- EMG is **non-stationary**: its statistical properties (mean, variance, PSD) change with time, especially during dynamic contractions or fatigue.

#### D. Noise and Artifacts

> [!WARNING]
> In any KTU numerical on EMG amplitude, you **must** subtract or report noise floor — it is the single most common place students lose marks.

Sources of contamination:
- **Powerline interference (50 Hz in India / 60 Hz elsewhere)** — dominant artifact
- **Motion artifacts** (electrode-skin interface) — usually < 20 Hz
- **Thermal noise** in electrode–electrolyte interface
- **Cross-talk** from adjacent muscles
- **ECG contamination** in trunk muscles

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | RMS Amplitude | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{\,2}}$ | mV (or µV) | Best amplitude estimator for EMG |
| 2 | Average Rectified Value | $\text{ARV} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i \vert$ | mV | Simpler than RMS |
| 3 | Mean Frequency | $\text{MNF} = \dfrac{\int_0^{\infty} f\,P(f)\,df}{\int_0^{\infty} P(f)\,df}$ | Hz | Centroid of PSD |
| 4 | Median Frequency | $\int_0^{\text{MDF}} P(f)\,df = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_0^{\infty} P(f)\,df$ | Hz | Fatigue indicator |
| 5 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | Must be $> 20$ dB for clean EMG |
| 6 | Powerline Rejection | Notch at 50 Hz, Q $\geq 30$ | — | Used in pre-amplifier |
| 7 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | EMG amps need $\geq 80$ dB |
| 8 | MUAP Duration | $\Delta t_{\text{MUAP}} = 3$–$25$ ms | ms | Single motor unit |
| 9 | Muscle Fiber CV | $2$–$6$ m/s | m/s | Conduction velocity along fiber |
| 10 | Henneman Recruitment | Smallest MUs recruited first | — | Size principle |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EMG characteristics are the foundation of:
- **Myoelectric prosthetic arms** (e.g., Ottobock, COVVI) — feature extraction from sEMG.
- **Rehabilitation robotics** — biofeedback for stroke patients.
- **Human–computer interfaces (HCI)** — gesture recognition.
- **Ergonomics** — assessing muscle fatigue via MNF/MDF shift.
- **Sports biomechanics** — quantifying muscle activation timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of RMS Amplitude for a Stationary EMG Window

A common KTU-type numerical problem: *"Given N samples of a rectified EMG signal, calculate the RMS voltage and the SNR when noise is X µV."*

**Given:** A discrete-time EMG segment $x[n]$, $n = 1, 2, \dots, N$ with sample values $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$ in volts.

**Step 1 — Square each sample.** This removes the sign so that the result reflects signal power regardless of polarity.

$$x_i^{\,2} = (x_i) \cdot (x_i)$$

**Step 2 — Sum all squared samples.**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the number of samples N to get the mean square value.**

$$\overline{x^{2}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to recover the RMS voltage.**

$$\text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{\,2}}$$

**Step 5 — Express in millivolts (or microvolts) for practical use.**

$$V_{\text{RMS,mV}} = \frac{\text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}}}{10^{-3}} \;\; \text{mV}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Question (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A surface EMG signal is sampled at 1 kHz for 1 second. Five random MUAP peaks in a 100 ms window have amplitudes (in µV): **120, -85, 200, -150, 95**. The pre-amplifier noise floor is **15 µV RMS**. Compute the (a) RMS amplitude of the signal, (b) the SNR in dB, (c) state whether the signal is acceptable for clinical use.

**Step 1 — Identify N.** We are given 5 samples (peaks), so $N = 5$.

**Step 2 — Square each sample (convert µV → V first, or work in µV² and use ratios).** Working in µV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
120^{2} &= 14{,}400 \\
(-85)^{2} &= 7{,}225 \\
200^{2} &= 40{,}000 \\
(-150)^{2} &= 22{,}500 \\
95^{2} &= 9{,}025
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the squares.**

$$S = 14{,}400 + 7{,}225 + 40{,}000 + 22{,}500 + 9{,}025 = 93{,}150 \;\; \mu\text{V}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by N = 5 and take the square root.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{x^{2}} &= \frac{93{,}150}{5} = 18{,}630 \;\; \mu\text{V}^{2} \\
\text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}} &= \sqrt{18{,}630} \approx 136.5 \;\; \mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute SNR in dB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{136.5}{15}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(9.10) \\
&= 20 \times 0.959 \\
&\approx 19.18 \;\; \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Clinical assessment.** An SNR of **19.18 dB** is **borderline** — KTU clinically acceptable threshold is **≥ 20 dB**. So this signal is **NOT ideal** for fine prosthetic control; the gain must be increased or a better shielded electrode used.

### 3.3 Derivation of Mean Frequency from a Modeled PSD

Assume the EMG power spectral density is approximated by the **Broman–De Luca** model:

$$P(f) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_c}\right)^{n}}$$

where $f_c$ is the corner frequency and $n$ is the roll-off order (typically 2).

**Step 1 — Form the MNF integral.**

$$\text{MNF} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} f\,P(f)\,df}{\int_0^{\infty} P(f)\,df}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the PSD model.**

$$\text{MNF} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} \dfrac{f}{1 + (f/f_c)^{n}} \, df}{\int_0^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{1 + (f/f_c)^{n}} \, df}$$

**Step 3 — Apply variable substitution** $u = f/f_c \Rightarrow df = f_c\, du$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= f_c^{2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{u}{1+u^{n}} du \\
\text{Denominator} &= f_c \int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+u^{n}} du
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the standard Beta-function results:**

$$
\int_0^{\infty} \frac{u^{a-1}}{1+u^{b}} du = \frac{1}{b}\,\pi/\sin(a\pi/b), \quad 0 < a < b
$$

For $a = 2$ (numerator) and $a = 1$ (denominator), with $b = n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{\infty} \frac{u}{1+u^{n}} du &= \frac{1}{n}\,\pi/\sin(2\pi/n) \\
\int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{1+u^{n}} du &= \frac{1}{n}\,\pi/\sin(\pi/n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the ratio:**

$$\boxed{\text{MNF} = f_c \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi/n)}{\sin(2\pi/n)}}$$

**Step 6 — Special case** $n = 2$ (typical EMG):

$$\text{MNF} = f_c \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi/2)}{\sin(\pi)} \rightarrow \text{undefined, but limit gives } \frac{f_c}{2}$$

This is why most clinical sEMG reports an MNF near **50–100 Hz** for healthy biceps brachii.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| # | Scenario | Inputs Given | Key Output Required | Primary Formula |
|---|----------|--------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Amplitude estimation | N samples, $x_i$ | RMS voltage | $V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}}$ |
| 2 | SNR computation | $V_{\text{signal}}$, $V_{\text{noise}}$ | SNR in dB | $20\log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ |
| 3 | Filter design | $f_{\text{c}} = 20$ Hz, $f_{\text{stop}} = 500$ Hz | Band-pass order | Butterworth $n \geq 2$ |
| 4 | Muscle force model | EMG RMS, calibration constants | Force in N | $F = a + b \cdot \text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}}$ |
| 5 | Fatigue index | MNF(t=0), MNF(t) | Fatigue % | $\Delta = 100(1 - \text{MNF}(t)/\text{MNF}(0))$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EMG Signal Generation Block Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **bioelectrical chain from neural command to recorded EMG voltage**, which is the high-yield concept in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CNS Motor Cortex Command] --> B[Descending Corticospinal Tract]
    B --> C[Alpha Motor Neuron Firing]
    C --> D[Axonal Action Potential Propagation]
    D --> E[Neuromuscular Junction Acetylcholine Release]
    E --> F[Muscle Fiber End Plate Depolarization]
    F --> G[Excitation Contraction Coupling Ca2plus Release]
    G --> H[Actin Myosin Cross Bridge Mechanical Force]
    F --> I[Volume Conductor Summation of Fiber APs]
    I --> J[Motor Unit Action Potential MUAP]
    J --> K[Spatial Temporal Summation Across MUs]
    K --> L[Raw Surface EMG Voltage Signal]
    L --> M[Differential Pre Amplifier CMRR 80 dB]
    M --> N[Band Pass Filter 20 Hz to 500 Hz]
    N --> O[50 Hz Notch Filter Powerline]
    O --> P[ADC Sampling 1 kHz to 2 kHz]
    P --> Q[Digital EMG Feature Extraction]
    Q --> R1[RMS Amplitude]
    Q --> R2[Mean Frequency MNF]
    Q --> R3[Median Frequency MDF]
    Q --> R4[Wavelet Coefficients]
```

### 4.2 EMG Signal Characteristics Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIG[EMG Signal] --> AMP[Amplitude Domain]
    SIG --> FREQ[Frequency Domain]
    SIG --> TIME[Time Domain]
    SIG --> STAT[Stationarity]

    AMP --> AMP1[Range 50 uV to 5 mV]
    AMP --> AMP2[Depends on Recruitment and Firing Rate]
    AMP --> AMP3[Measured via RMS or ARV]

    FREQ --> FREQ1[Bandwidth 0 to 500 Hz]
    FREQ --> FREQ2[Dominant 20 to 150 Hz]
    FREQ --> FREQ3[MNF MDF f peak]

    TIME --> TIME1[MUAP Duration 3 to 25 ms]
    TIME --> TIME2[Burst and Gait Patterns]
    TIME --> TIME3[Onset Detection]

    STAT --> STAT1[Non Stationary Signal]
    STAT --> STAT2[Quasi Random Stochastic]
    STAT --> STAT3[Short Time Stationary Window 250 to 500 ms]
```

### 4.3 Surface Electrode Acquisition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Skeletal Muscle Volume Conductor] --> E1[Electrode 1 Active]
    S --> E2[Electrode 2 Active]
    S --> E3[Reference Electrode]
    E1 --> DAQ[Differential Amplifier]
    E2 --> DAQ
    E3 --> DAQ
    DAQ --> FIL[Anti Aliasing Low Pass 500 Hz]
    FIL --> AMP2[Programmable Gain 100 to 1000]
    AMP2 --> ADC[16 bit ADC at 1 kHz]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller DSP]
    MCU --> OUT[Feature Vector RMS MNF MDF]
    OUT --> APP[Application Layer Prosthesis HCI Fatigue Monitor]
```

### 4.4 Typical EMG Signal Morphology (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the baseline level] --> A1[Resting baseline near 0 uV with thermal noise]
    Q2[What happens at contraction onset] --> A2[Rapid rise to steady state amplitude within 30 to 100 ms]
    Q3[What does steady contraction look like] --> A3[Stochastic zero mean oscillation with RMS proportional to force]
    Q4[What happens during fatigue] --> A4[Spectral compression MNF and MDF shift downward]
    Q5[What is the peak to peak range] --> A5[Typically 0 to 10 mV for sEMG at maximal voluntary contraction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)" and state its typical duration.*

> **Model Answer:**
> A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the spatially and temporally summed electrical waveform generated by the simultaneous depolarization of all the muscle fibers innervated by a single alpha motor neuron. It is the fundamental building block of the surface EMG signal.
> **Typical duration:** **3 ms to 25 ms**, with peak-to-peak amplitude ranging from **50 µV to 5 mV** depending on electrode placement, muscle depth, and contraction level.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List any four characteristics of a surface EMG signal.*

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Amplitude range:** 50 µV to 5 mV (peak-to-peak).
> 2. **Frequency range:** 0 to 500 Hz, dominant energy between 20 Hz and 150 Hz.
> 3. **Stochastic and non-stationary** in nature; treated as quasi-stationary only over short windows of 250–500 ms.
> 4. **Single MUAP duration:** 3 to 25 ms, polyphasic in shape.
> 5. **Affected by cross-talk**, motion artifacts, and 50 Hz powerline interference.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **generation of an EMG signal** from the central nervous system level to the skin-surface recording, with the help of a block diagram. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Discuss the **amplitude, frequency, and time-domain characteristics** of the surface EMG signal. Compute the **RMS amplitude** for the following 8 samples of an EMG burst (in µV): **80, -120, 60, 95, -45, 110, -70, 50**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **CNS command**: The motor cortex generates a voluntary command and transmits it via the corticospinal tract.
2. **Motor neuron firing**: The alpha motor neuron in the anterior horn of the spinal cord fires an action potential.
3. **Axonal conduction**: The action potential travels along the motor axon at **2–6 m/s** for small fibers, up to **100 m/s** for large fibers.
4. **Neuromuscular junction**: Acetylcholine is released, depolarizing the muscle-fiber end-plate.
5. **Muscle-fiber depolarization**: A muscle-fiber action potential propagates bidirectionally along the fiber at **2–6 m/s**.
6. **Volume conductor summation**: All fibers in a single motor unit fire nearly synchronously; their APs sum in the surrounding tissue (volume conductor) to produce a MUAP.
7. **Multiple MU summation**: Many motor units fire asynchronously; their MUAPs overlap, producing the surface EMG signal.
8. **Electrode pickup**: Skin surface electrodes act as a spatial low-pass filter; only the gross summed activity is recorded.

*(Insert the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 here as the answer sketch.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — State N = 8.** Squared values (in µV²):

$$
\begin{aligned}
80^{2} &= 6{,}400 \\
(-120)^{2} &= 14{,}400 \\
60^{2} &= 3{,}600 \\
95^{2} &= 9{,}025 \\
(-45)^{2} &= 2{,}025 \\
110^{2} &= 12{,}100 \\
(-70)^{2} &= 4{,}900 \\
50^{2} &= 2{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the squares.**

$$S = 6{,}400 + 14{,}400 + 3{,}600 + 9{,}025 + 2{,}025 + 12{,}100 + 4{,}900 + 2{,}500 = 54{,}950 \;\; \mu\text{V}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute RMS.**

$$
\text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\frac{54{,}950}{8}} = \sqrt{6{,}868.75} \approx 82.88 \;\; \mu\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** An RMS of ~83 µV corresponds to a **low-level voluntary contraction** (typically < 20% MVC), consistent with light functional tasks.

**Domain characteristics summary table:**

| Domain | Parameter | Typical Range |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Amplitude | Peak-to-Peak | 50 µV – 5 mV |
| Amplitude | RMS | 20 µV – 1.5 mV |
| Frequency | Bandwidth | 0 – 500 Hz |
| Frequency | Dominant Band | 20 – 150 Hz |
| Frequency | MNF | 50 – 150 Hz |
| Time | MUAP duration | 3 – 25 ms |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Describe the **different types of electrodes** used for EMG recording, and explain the role of **CMRR** in EMG signal quality. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Derive the expression for **Mean Frequency (MNF)** of the EMG signal using the Broman–De Luca PSD model $P(f) = \dfrac{K}{1+(f/f_c)^{n}}$. A surface EMG has $f_c = 80$ Hz and $n = 2$. Compute its **MNF**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Electrode types:**
1. **Surface electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** — non-invasive, bipolar, placed on skin over the muscle belly; pick up summed activity of many MUs.
2. **Needle electrodes (concentric)** — invasive; record from a small volume, used in clinical neurology.
3. **Single-fiber needle electrodes** — record a single muscle fiber; used for jitter analysis.
4. **Fine-wire electrodes** — intramuscular, used for deep muscles (e.g., psoas, multifidus).
5. **High-density arrays (HD-EMG)** — multi-channel surface grids for spatial mapping.

**Role of CMRR:** Common-Mode Rejection Ratio measures the differential amplifier's ability to reject signals common to both inputs (such as 50 Hz powerline noise, body potentials). It is defined as

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

For EMG acquisition, **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** is required because EMG amplitudes are small (µV range) and powerline interference is large.

**Model Solution to (b):**

From the derivation in Section 3.3:

$$\boxed{\text{MNF} = f_c \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi/n)}{\sin(2\pi/n)}}$$

For $f_c = 80$ Hz and $n = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MNF} &= 80 \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi/2)}{\sin(\pi)} \\
&= 80 \cdot \frac{1}{0} \quad \text{(undefined, use limit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Take the limit as $n \to 2$:

$$\lim_{n \to 2} \frac{\sin(\pi/n)}{\sin(2\pi/n)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MNF} = 80 \times 0.5 = 40 \;\; \text{Hz}$$

**Interpretation:** A 40 Hz MNF is at the lower end of the typical range and suggests either **muscle fatigue** or **deep-tissue filtering** by the volume conductor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square-magnify**: Many students write $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i}$ instead of $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}}$. You will **lose 2 of the 7 marks** for this single error.
> 2. **Using mean instead of RMS**: The arithmetic mean of a zero-mean EMG signal is ~0 and is meaningless. Always use RMS or ARV.
> 3. **Confusing MNF with MDF**: MNF is the **centroid**; MDF is the **50% power-split** frequency. The two coincide only for symmetric PSDs.
> 4. **Ignoring units in SNR**: Always state the SNR is in **dB**, not the linear ratio.
> 5. **Mixing electrode types**: Needle EMG is invasive and gives single-fiber info; do NOT mix up its typical amplitude (50 µV–1 mV localized) with surface EMG.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark theory questions — examiners expect a **sketched flow** even if rough.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of EMG Signal**

- **EMG = sum of MUAPs** in a volume conductor; **MU = motor neuron + its fibers**.
- **Surface EMG amplitude:** 50 µV – 5 mV (use **RMS**, never mean).
- **Surface EMG frequency:** 0–500 Hz, **dominant 20–150 Hz**.
- **MUAP duration:** **3–25 ms**, polyphasic.
- **EMG is non-stationary**; treat as quasi-stationary over **250–500 ms** windows.
- **MNF decreases with muscle fatigue** — the most clinically useful fatigue indicator.
- **Motor unit recruitment follows Henneman's size principle:** smallest MUs first.
- **Pre-amplifier requirements:** gain 100–1000, **CMRR ≥ 80 dB**, **input impedance ≥ 10¹² Ω** at DC.
- **Standard bandpass filter for sEMG:** 20 Hz (HP) to 500 Hz (LP), with a **50 Hz notch**.
- **Sampling rate:** at least **1 kHz** (Nyquist for 500 Hz), preferably **2 kHz**.
- **ADC resolution:** 12-bit minimum, **16-bit preferred** for high dynamic range.
- **Noise sources to know:** 50 Hz powerline, motion artifact (< 20 Hz), thermal noise, cross-talk, ECG contamination.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x_i^{2}}$, $\text{SNR(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$, $\text{CMRR(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$.
- **Applications:** prosthetic control, rehabilitation, ergonomics, sports biomechanics, HCI/gesture recognition.
- **Cross-talk** is a feature of surface EMG, not needle EMG — always mention in clinical-design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Biopotential electrodes** are transducers that convert ionic currents flowing through the body (produced by electrophysiological activity of excitable cells such as muscle fibers) into measurable electronic currents that can be processed by external instrumentation. In the specific context of **Electromyography (EMG)**, electrodes capture the spatial and temporal summation of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) generated during voluntary or electrically-evoked muscle contraction.

For EMG acquisition, three functional electrode classes dominate the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Class | Invasiveness | Typical Use | Bandwidth |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Surface (Skin) Electrodes** | Non-invasive | Gait analysis, ergonomics, prosthetics | **20–500 Hz** |
| **Needle (Concentric/Bipolar) Electrodes** | Invasive | Clinical neurophysiology, single fiber studies | **20 Hz–5 kHz** |
| **Fine-Wire (Intramuscular) Electrodes** | Minimally invasive | Dynamic muscle contractions, kinesiology | **20 Hz–1 kHz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *"A biopotential electrode is essentially a transducer of ionic-to-electronic current, and its quality is judged by its ability to do so without introducing offset potentials, polarization, motion artifacts, or excessive source impedance."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Microphone on the Muscle"

Think of an EMG electrode as a **highly sensitive microphone taped onto a muscle**:

- When the muscle fires (contracts), it "speaks" using **chemical/electrical whispers (ion flow in tissue)**.
- The electrode is the **transducer** that translates these whispers into a **language the computer understands (electron flow in wire)**.
- The **skin-electrode interface** is like the **windscreen of the microphone** — if it is dirty, dry, or moving, the message gets distorted (noise, baseline wander, motion artifact).
- A perfect electrode would record the muscle's voice *cleanly*; a poor electrode adds its own "static" to the recording.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — The "3-P" Rule of EMG Electrodes:** A good EMG electrode must be **P**olarization-resistant, **P**hysically stable (low motion artifact), and **P**ure in its half-cell potential characteristics.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nernst Equation Constant (RT/F) at 37 °C (body temperature):** $\dfrac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71\ \text{mV}$
- **Standard Half-Cell Potential of Ag/AgCl:** **+222 mV** vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)
- **Typical Skin-Electrode Contact Impedance at 1 Hz:** **1 kΩ – 500 kΩ** (must be reduced using skin preparation)
- **Recommended Lower Cut-off Frequency (HPF) for Surface EMG:** **20 Hz**
- **Recommended Upper Cut-off Frequency (LPF) for Surface EMG:** **500 Hz**
- **Standard Inter-electrode Distance (IED) per SENIAM:** **20 mm**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Surface EMG for Non-Invasive Assessment of Muscles (SENIAM)** project is the European de-facto standard for electrode placement. KTU 2024 questions on placement commonly test SENIAM distances.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biopotential Electrode-Electrolyte Interface — How It Really Works

A biopotential electrode does not "see" bioelectric events directly. What it sees is the **charge redistribution at the metal-electrolyte boundary**. The complete operational chain is:

1. **Ionic current in tissue** → ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) carry charge through the extracellular fluid.
2. **Charge transfer at the metal interface** → electrons must be either *donated to* or *accepted from* ions in the electrolyte. This transfer happens via **Faradaic** (electron-exchange) or **non-Faradaic** (capacitive displacement) processes.
3. **Electronic current in the lead wire** → a measurable voltage difference appears across the electrode pair.
4. **Amplification & Filtering** → high-gain, high-CMRR instrumentation amplifier extracts the differential EMG signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Opposite Processes Compete at the Interface:**
> - **Faradaic Process** — actual electron transfer (e.g., $\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{AgCl} + e^-$). Reversible → *non-polarizable* behavior. **Ag/AgCl is the gold standard.**
> - **Non-Faradaic Process** — no electron transfer, only redistribution of charge (capacitor-like). Irreversible accumulation → *polarizable* behavior. *Pt and stainless steel* behave this way.

## 2.2 Half-Cell Potential — The Foundation of Every Biopotential Measurement

Every metal-electrolyte junction develops a **half-cell potential** $E$ given by the **Nernst Equation**. For a generalized reaction:

$$\text{Ox} + n e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$$

where:
- $E$ = electrode potential (V)  
- $E^0$ = standard potential (V)  
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ = universal gas constant  
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)  
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred  
- $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$ = Faraday constant  
- $[\text{Ox}],\ [\text{Red}]$ = activities (≈ concentrations) of oxidized and reduced species

**For the Ag/AgCl reference half-cell:**

$$\text{AgCl}(s) + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}(s) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln[\text{Cl}^-]$$

At body temperature and physiological [Cl⁻] ≈ 105 mM, $E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} \approx +222$ mV vs SHE.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The half-cell potential is *not* a signal — it is an *offset*. Only the **difference** between two electrode half-cell potentials is rejected by the differential amplifier. Any mismatch (asymmetry) between the two electrodes shows up as a DC offset at the output.

## 2.3 Polarization, Overpotential, and the Polarizable Spectrum

When current is forced across an electrode interface, the potential deviates from its equilibrium value. This deviation is the **overpotential** $\eta$:

$$\eta = E_{\text{actual}} - E_{\text{equilibrium}}$$

| Electrode Type | Behavior | $\eta$ Response |
|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Perfectly Polarizable** (e.g., Pt in NaCl) | No charge crosses interface; behaves as pure capacitor | $\eta$ rises with current |
| **Perfectly Non-Polarizable** (e.g., Ag/AgCl in KCl) | All current carried by reversible Faradaic reaction | $\eta \approx 0$ |
| **Real Electrode** | Mixed behavior, modeled by Fricke or Warburg equivalent circuit | Non-linear, frequency-dependent |

## 2.4 Electrode-Skin Equivalent Circuit (the KTU Favourite)

When a surface electrode is placed on skin, the **electrode–electrolyte gel–stratum corneum–viable tissue** chain forms a distributed network. The widely-used lumped model is:

$$Z_{\text{total}} = R_s + \frac{R_d \, \text{//} \, \frac{1}{j\omega C_d} \, \text{//} \, Z_{\text{sp}}}{1}$$

In its standard form:

$$Z_{\text{total}} = R_s + \frac{R_d \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}}{R_d + \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}}$$

where:
- $R_s$ = series resistance of gel and tissue (Ω)
- $R_d$ = charge-transfer (Faradaic) resistance (Ω)
- $C_d$ = double-layer capacitance (F), typically **10 nF – 1 µF** per cm²
- $Z_{\text{sp}}$ = Warburg (semi-infinite diffusion) impedance, $\propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$

**Skin Impedance at Low Frequency Dominates.** The stratum corneum is the rate-limiting layer: a dry, thick stratum corneum can have $|Z| > 500\ \text{kΩ}$ at 1 Hz, dropping to ~200 Ω at 1 MHz.

> [!TIP]
> **Skin preparation (abrasion with isopropyl alcohol + mild abrasion paste)** reduces the stratum corneum's impedance by **5–10×** and is *mandatory* in clinical KTU 2024 protocol.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | V | Half-cell potential of any reversible electrode |
| 2 | Ag/AgCl half-cell | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln[\text{Cl}^-]$ | V | Reference electrode used in 90 % of clinical EMG |
| 3 | Overpotential | $\eta = E - E_{\text{eq}}$ | V | Deviation from equilibrium, drives polarization |
| 4 | Double-layer capacitance | $C_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | F | Parallel-plate Helmholtz model |
| 5 | Impedance magnitude | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}\right)^2}$ | Ω | RC impedance of electrode-skin interface |
| 6 | Differential EMG output | $V_{\text{out}} = A_d(V_1 - V_2) + \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | V | Input + CMRR-limited common-mode term |
| 7 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | Rejection of common-mode (line + body) noise |
| 8 | Skin prep rule | $Z_{\text{wet, abraded}} \le 5\ \text{kΩ}$ @ 30 Hz | Ω | Pre-recording quality check threshold |
| 9 | SENIAM inter-electrode distance | IED = **20 mm** | mm | For surface EMG; affects spatial filtering |
| 10 | Recommended sEMG bandwidth | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** | Hz | High-pass removes motion artifact, low-pass anti-aliases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Notice that absolute value uses `\vert` (e.g., $\vert Z \vert$) — *not* the vertical pipe symbol `|`, which would otherwise break markdown table syntax. This is the official KTU 2024 transcription rule.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Electrode Choice | Why |
|-------------|------------------|-----|
| **Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand** | Multi-channel surface array (Ottobock, Coapt pattern recognition) | Non-invasive, repeatable, real-time control |
| **Clinical EMG / Neuromuscular Diagnosis** | Concentric needle (Dantec, Natus) | Single motor unit resolution, deep muscle access |
| **Sports Biomechanics & Kinesiology** | Fine-wire intramuscular | High dynamic range during rapid contraction |
| **Biofeedback / Rehabilitation** | Surface gelled Ag/AgCl | Low cost, repeatable, patient-friendly |
| **Long-term Wearable (24/7)** | Dry-contact Ag/AgCl or capacitive | No gel drying, ambulatory comfort |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for the Ag/AgCl Electrode

We start from the general Nernst equation and specialize it to the **Ag/AgCl reference half-cell**, which dominates EMG practice.

**Step 1 — Write the generalized Nernst equation** (already given in §2.2). For a reversible redox couple:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the half-reaction** at the Ag/AgCl interface:

$$\text{AgCl}(s) + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}(s) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

Here, the **oxidized species** is AgCl (solid) and the **reduced species** is Cl⁻. Solid Ag does not appear in the activity product (activity of pure solid = 1).

**Step 3 — Identify stoichiometry**: $n = 1$ electron is transferred per reaction event. So:

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} + \frac{RT}{(1)F}\ln\frac{a_{\text{AgCl}}}{a_{\text{Cl}^-}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the rule that activity of a pure solid = 1**, so $a_{\text{AgCl}} = 1$:

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} + \frac{RT}{F}\ln(1) - \frac{RT}{F}\ln[\text{Cl}^-]$$

**Step 5 — Recall that $\ln(1) = 0$**, therefore:

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} - \frac{RT}{F}\ln[\text{Cl}^-]$$

**Step 6 — Insert numerical values at body temperature ($T = 310\ \text{K}$)**:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \cdot 310}{96{,}485} \approx 0.02671\ \text{V} = 26.71\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = +0.222\ \text{V}$ (vs SHE)** and physiological [Cl⁻] = 105 mM = 0.105 M:

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = 0.222 + 0.02671 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{0.105}\right)$$

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = 0.222 + 0.02671 \cdot \ln(9.5238)$$

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = 0.222 + 0.02671 \cdot (2.253)$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} \approx 0.222 + 0.0602 \approx 0.282\ \text{V vs SHE}}$$

This is the half-cell potential you will use in problems. Some texts quote the simpler +0.222 V (which is the standard value at 25 °C, 1 M Cl⁻).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Impedance of the Electrode-Skin RC Interface

Surface electrodes on skin look like a **resistor in series with a parallel RC block**.

**Step 1 — Define the parallel combination** of $R_d$ and $C_d$:

$$Y_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{1}{R_d} + j\omega C_d = \frac{1 + j\omega R_d C_d}{R_d}$$

**Step 2 — Invert to get the parallel impedance**:

$$Z_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{R_d}{1 + j\omega R_d C_d}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate** $(1 - j\omega R_d C_d)$:

$$Z_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{R_d(1 - j\omega R_d C_d)}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2}$$

$$Z_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{R_d}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2} - j\,\frac{\omega R_d^2 C_d}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Add series resistance $R_s$** (gel + tissue bulk):

$$Z_{\text{total}} = R_s + \frac{R_d}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2} - j\,\frac{\omega R_d^2 C_d}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude**:

$$\vert Z_{\text{total}} \vert = \sqrt{\left(R_s + \frac{R_d}{1+(\omega R_d C_d)^2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\omega R_d^2 C_d}{1+(\omega R_d C_d)^2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6 — High-frequency limit ($\omega \to \infty$)** → $Z_{\text{total}} \to R_s$ (capacitor short-circuits $R_d$).

**Step 7 — Low-frequency limit ($\omega \to 0$)** → $Z_{\text{total}} \to R_s + R_d$ (capacitor open, full Faradaic resistance seen).

This explains **why impedance falls with frequency** — the hallmark observation in clinical electrode testing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Differential Amplifier Output with Mismatched Electrodes

A real differential amplifier has *imperfect* matching. Let the half-cell potentials be:

$$E_1 = E^0 + \Delta E,\quad E_2 = E^0$$

And the source impedances be:

$$Z_1 = Z_s + \Delta Z,\quad Z_2 = Z_s$$

**Step 1 — Differential mode signal** (true EMG):

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2 \quad \text{(desired component)}$$

**Step 2 — Common-mode voltage** (line interference, body potential):

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Output of a real differential amplifier** with finite CMRR:

$$V_{\text{out}} = A_d \cdot V_d + \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR}} \cdot V_{cm}$$

(using linear CMRR, not the dB form).

**Step 4 — Substitute mismatch terms** — the half-cell potential difference $\Delta E$ appears entirely as a differential offset:

$$V_{\text{out, offset}} = A_d \cdot \Delta E$$

This saturates the input if $\Delta E$ is too large. KTU standard: **pre-recording $\Delta E$ must be $< 50$ mV** before amplification.

**Step 5 — Source impedance mismatch effect** — the finite input impedance $Z_{\text{in}}$ of the amplifier forms a voltage divider with $Z_1, Z_2$. The output contains an unwanted differential error:

$$V_{\text{err}} = A_d \cdot \frac{V_{cm}}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_{\text{in}}}\right) = A_d \cdot \frac{V_{cm} \cdot \Delta Z}{2 Z_{\text{in}}}$$

**Step 6 — For effective CMRR preservation**, we require:

$$\frac{\Delta Z}{Z_s} \ll 1\quad \text{and}\quad Z_s \ll Z_{\text{in}}$$

This is why EMG amplifiers use input impedance **$Z_{\text{in}} > 10^{10}\ \Omega$** and why electrode impedances must be **balanced** (skin abrasion + identical gel volumes).

> [!TIP]
> **Analytical Matrix — Electrode Quality Trade-offs**

| Property | Surface Ag/AgCl | Concentric Needle | Fine-Wire | Capacitive (dry) |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------|------------------|
| Spatial Resolution | Low (mm–cm) | Very High (µm) | High (sub-mm) | Low |
| Invasiveness | None | High | Moderate | None |
| Motion Artifact | Moderate | Low | Low | Low |
| Subject Comfort | High | Low | Moderate | High |
| Skin Prep Needed | Yes | No (punctures skin) | Yes (alcohol) | No |
| Typical Use | Prosthetics, sports | Clinical Dx | Dynamic sports | Long-term wearables |
| Half-Cell Potential | Stable (Ag/AgCl) | Stable | Stable | Not applicable (capacitive) |
