# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between a perfectly polarizable and a perfectly non-polarizable electrode. Give one example of each, in the context of EMG acquisition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Perfectly Polarizable | Perfectly Non-Polarizable |
|----------|-----------------------|----------------------------|
| Charge transfer | None — only capacitive | Reversible Faradaic |
| Overpotential $\eta$ | Large for any current | Approximately zero |
| Impedance | Capacitor-like, frequency-dependent | Pure resistance |
| Example (EMG) | Platinum, Stainless Steel | **Ag/AgCl (gold standard for EMG)** |

A perfectly **polarizable** electrode (e.g., Pt in saline) does not allow electrons to cross the metal-electrolyte boundary; all current is displacement current through the double-layer capacitor. A perfectly **non-polarizable** electrode (e.g., Ag/AgCl in KCl) permits fully reversible Faradaic reaction $\text{AgCl} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^-$, so current flows without overpotential. Ag/AgCl is therefore preferred in EMG for its **low, stable half-cell potential, low noise, and minimal polarization** under the small currents encountered in biopotential recording. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**State the Nernst equation. Calculate the half-cell potential of an Ag/AgCl electrode in contact with body tissue at 37 °C, given physiological [Cl⁻] = 105 mM and $E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = +0.222$ V.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nernst equation:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$$

For Ag/AgCl with $n = 1$ and $T = 310$ K:

$$E = 0.222 - \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(1)(96485)}\ln(0.105)$$

$$E = 0.222 - (0.02671)(\ln 0.105)$$

$$E = 0.222 - (0.02671)(-2.254)$$

$$E = 0.222 + 0.0602 = 0.282\ \text{V vs SHE}$$

**Half-cell potential ≈ +0.282 V vs SHE** (1 mark for equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final value with correct units). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **electrode-skin interface equivalent circuit** and explain the role of each component. Discuss the **frequency dependence** of the interface impedance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Block diagram description (3 marks):**  
   The electrode-skin interface is modeled as: $R_s$ (series resistance: gel + bulk tissue) **in series with** a parallel combination of $R_d$ (charge-transfer / Faradaic resistance) and $C_d$ (double-layer capacitance). Sometimes a Warburg element $Z_W$ is added in series with $R_d$ to model diffusion. The total impedance is:

$$Z_{\text{total}} = R_s + \frac{R_d}{1 + j\omega R_d C_d}$$

2. **Role of each element (2 marks):**
   - **$R_s$**: Resistive path through gel and subcutaneous tissue. Dominates at high frequency.
   - **$R_d$**: Resistance to Faradaic charge transfer across the metal-electrolyte interface. Inversely related to exchange current density.
   - **$C_d$**: Helmholtz double-layer capacitance, typically 10 nF – 1 µF per cm².
   - **$Z_W$ (Warburg)**: Models semi-infinite linear diffusion of ions; $\vert Z_W \vert \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$.

3. **Frequency dependence (2 marks):**  
   At DC / very low frequency ($\omega \to 0$): $Z \to R_s + R_d$ (capacitor open, full Faradaic resistance).  
   At high frequency ($\omega \to \infty$): $Z \to R_s$ (capacitor short-circuits $R_d$).  
   At the **corner frequency** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_d C_d)$, the impedance falls to $|Z| = R_s + R_d/\sqrt{2}$.  
   This is why **abrasion + electrolytic gel** is critical: it lowers $R_d$ (by increasing effective surface area) and boosts $C_d$ (by hydrating the stratum corneum), flattening the impedance vs. frequency curve.

**(b)** Compare **surface, concentric-needle, and fine-wire EMG electrodes** in terms of: (i) spatial selectivity, (ii) signal bandwidth, (iii) subject comfort, (iv) typical clinical / engineering application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

| Feature | Surface (Ag/AgCl) | Concentric Needle | Fine Wire |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **(i) Spatial Selectivity** | Low — records from many MUs under a wide area | Very High — single MU at the tip | High — captures 2–5 MUs near the wire |
| **(ii) Bandwidth** | **20 – 500 Hz** (lower high-cut to attenuate motion) | **20 Hz – 5 kHz** (sharp spikes need high LPF) | **20 Hz – 1 kHz** (compromise) |
| **(iii) Comfort** | Excellent (non-invasive) | Painful; requires medical supervision | Moderate (insertion discomfort, but well-tolerated) |
| **(iv) Application** | Myoelectric prostheses, sports, ergonomics | **Clinical neurophysiology diagnosis** (myopathies, neuropathies) | Dynamic kinesiology, deep muscles inaccessible to surface |

Conclusion: **For non-invasive repeatable applications, surface electrodes are preferred; for single-unit clinical diagnosis, concentric needles are gold standard; for dynamic deep-muscle kinesiology, fine-wire electrodes strike the best compromise.** **(7 marks: 1 mark per row in table, 3 marks for justification prose)**

---

### Question B — Option (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**(a)** Define **half-cell potential** and **overpotential**. With reference to the **Nernst equation**, derive the half-cell potential of the **Ag/AgCl electrode** at body temperature and physiological chloride concentration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Half-cell potential** $E$ is the electric potential developed at the metal-electrolyte interface at equilibrium, governed by the Nernst equation.
- **Overpotential** $\eta = E - E_{\text{eq}}$ is the deviation of the actual electrode potential from its equilibrium value when net DC current is forced across the interface.

**Derivation (5 marks):** See §3.1 of these notes for the full 7-step derivation. Key result:

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = E^0_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} - \frac{RT}{F}\ln[\text{Cl}^-]$$

At $T = 310$ K, [Cl⁻] = 0.105 M:

$$E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} = 0.222 - 0.02671 \ln(0.105) = 0.282\ \text{V vs SHE}$$

**(b)** Explain **motion artifact** in surface EMG recording. Discuss the **three engineering strategies** used to suppress it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Motion artifact is a low-frequency (typically **< 20 Hz**) voltage disturbance in the EMG signal caused by **mechanical movement of the electrode relative to the skin** and by **deformation of the skin-electrolyte interface**, which modulates the half-cell potential and the skin's piezo-electric behavior. It appears as baseline wander and can saturate amplifiers.

**Three suppression strategies (5 marks — distribute ~1.5–2 marks each):**

1. **High-Pass Filtering (Hardware):** A 20 Hz first-order or second-order HPF removes the bulk of the artifact. Care: too high a corner attenuates genuine MUAP components.
2. **Mechanical Stabilization (Electrode Design):**
   - Use of **adhesive hydrogel rings** that hold the gel column rigidly.
   - **Concentric ring** or **double-sided adhesive collars**.
   - **Pre-gelled disposable electrodes** with stable electrolyte column.
3. **Electrical & Algorithmic Compensation:**
   - **Impedance balancing** (impedance of both electrodes within 5 % of each other to reject common-mode motion).
   - **Active shielding** of the lead wires (driven-right-leg circuit) to cut cable-induced pickup.
   - **Adaptive filtering / Wavelet denoising** in software post-acquisition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert T = 310 K** in the Nernst equation at body temperature — a common error leads to a wrong constant (29.58 mV instead of 26.71 mV), propagating a ~10 % error in $E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}}$.
> 2. **Confusing half-cell potential with EMG signal amplitude.** $E_{\text{Ag/AgCl}} \approx 0.282$ V is an offset, *not* a signal.
> 3. **Writing vertical pipes (`|Z|`) in markdown tables** — this breaks KTU's automated parsing and forfeits presentation marks. Use `\vert Z \vert` instead.
> 4. **Omitting the skin preparation step** in motion-artifact answers — examiners look for the **abrasion + alcohol + gel** triad explicitly.
> 5. **Stating "Ag/AgCl is polarizable"** — it is *non-polarizable* (Faradaic). This is a 2-mark error in any KTU 2024 paper.
> 6. **Wrong bandwidth citation** for surface EMG — KTU 2024 standard is **20 Hz – 500 Hz**, not "DC – 1 kHz".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Nernst Equation** governs every reversible electrode's half-cell potential: $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl is the gold-standard EMG electrode**: non-polarizable, low offset (~282 mV vs SHE at 37 °C), low noise.
- ✅ **$RT/F$ at 37 °C = 26.71 mV** — memorize this for Nernst problems.
- ✅ **Electrode-skin model:** $Z = R_s + (R_d \parallel 1/j\omega C_d)$; impedance is highest at low frequency.
- ✅ **Skin preparation** (abrasion + isopropyl alcohol) reduces $Z$ by 5–10× — *mandatory* before clinical sEMG.
- ✅ **Surface EMG bandwidth = 20 Hz – 500 Hz** (HPF removes motion, LPF prevents aliasing).
- ✅ **Needle EMG bandwidth = 20 Hz – 5 kHz** (spikes need higher LPF).
- ✅ **Motion artifact is a low-frequency (< 20 Hz) disturbance** from electrode-skin mechanical movement; suppressed by HPF, mechanical stabilization, and impedance balancing.
- ✅ **SENIAM standard inter-electrode distance = 20 mm** for surface EMG.
- ✅ **CMRR of EMG amplifier must exceed 90 dB** (preferably > 100 dB) to reject 50/60 Hz line noise.
- ✅ **Half-cell potential mismatch between paired electrodes = DC offset** that must be < 50 mV before amplification.
- ✅ **Perfectly polarizable electrodes** (Pt, SS) → capacitive, high $\eta$.
- ✅ **Perfectly non-polarizable electrodes** (Ag/AgCl) → Faradaic, $\eta \approx 0$.
- ✅ **Always use `\vert` and `\mid` (not `|`) for absolute value and conditional probability** when typesetting for KTU 2024 markdown submission.
- ✅ **Mermaid node IDs must be alphanumeric and prefixed** — never use reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) as node names.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word — KTU 2024 Mindset:** Every question on EMG electrodes ultimately tests one of three ideas: **(1)** Can you derive the half-cell potential using the Nernst equation? **(2)** Can you model the electrode-skin interface and predict its frequency response? **(3)** Can you justify the choice of Ag/AgCl for clinical EMG using the polarizability argument? Master these three, and the full 14-mark question is yours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Biosensors: Amperometric, Potentiometric
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Electrochemical Biosensors: Amperometric & Potentiometric

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Biosensors: Amperometric, Potentiometric

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Electrochemical Biosensor?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 3.1):**
> An **Electrochemical Biosensor** is an analytical device that integrates a biological recognition element (enzyme, antibody, nucleic acid, cell, or tissue) with an electrochemical transducer to convert a biochemical interaction into a measurable electrical signal — namely **current (A)**, **potential (V)**, **impedance (Ω)**, or **conductance (S)**.

Electrochemical biosensors are the **most commercially successful** class of biosensors, accounting for approximately **~85% of the global biosensor market** (especially glucose meters). They are favored for their **high sensitivity, low cost, fast response, and compatibility with microfabrication (MEMS/CMOS)**.

### 1.2 The Three Electrochemical Sub-Families (KTU 2024 Module 3)

| Sub-Family | Signal Measured | Driving Force | Classical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amperometric** | Current ($i$) | Constant applied **Potential (E)** | Glucose Oxidase (GOx) electrode |
| **Potentiometric** | Voltage / Potential ($E$) | **Zero / negligible** current | pH glass electrode, ISFET |
| **Impedimetric / Conductometric** | Impedance ($Z$) or Conductance ($G$) | AC sinusoidal excitation | DNA / Antibody affinity sensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> - **Amperometric** = "Watching how fast water flows through a pipe (current) when you keep a fixed water pressure (potential)." The flow tells you how much "sugar" is in the pipe.
> - **Potentiometric** = "Measuring the voltage of a battery without letting any current flow — the open-circuit voltage tells you the battery's state of charge (concentration)."
> - **Conductometric** = "Measuring how easily electricity passes through a solution — more ions = more conductivity."

### 1.3 General Three-Electrode Electrochemical Cell (Foundation of Amperometry)

> [!NOTE]
> Almost every amperometric biosensor uses a **three-electrode configuration** connected to a **Potentiostat**. This is the heart of every KTU numerical/model question on this topic.

| Electrode | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Electrode (WE)** | Site of the biorecognition + redox reaction; current is measured here | Pt, Au, Glassy Carbon, Screen-Printed Carbon (SPCE) |
| **Reference Electrode (RE)** | Provides a **stable, known reference potential** (E° = const.) | Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl), Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) |
| **Counter / Auxiliary Electrode (CE)** | Completes the circuit; carries the balancing current | Pt wire, Pt mesh |

The transducer output is then conditioned through a **Current-to-Voltage (I-to-V) converter** (e.g., op-amp transimpedance amplifier) before ADC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 AMPEROMETRIC BIOSENSORS

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle

In an amperometric biosensor:
1. A **fixed DC potential** is applied between the Working Electrode (WE) and the Reference Electrode (RE) by a **potentiostat**.
2. The potential is chosen at the **diffusion-limited plateau** of the analyte's cyclic voltammogram — this ensures every analyte molecule arriving at the electrode surface is **immediately oxidized or reduced** (mass-transport limited).
3. The **faradaic current** $i$ that flows is **directly proportional to the bulk concentration** $C$ of the electroactive species, per the **Cottrell Equation**.

The general reaction is:

$$ \text{Ox} + ne^- \xrightarrow{\text{applied } E} \text{Red} $$

### 2.1.2 Why a Potentiostat is Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **potentiostat** is an electronic feedback instrument that:
> - Maintains WE-vs-RE potential at a **user-set value** ($E_{app}$).
> - Sinks/sources the resulting current through the **Counter Electrode**, so that **no appreciable current flows through the Reference Electrode** (preserving its $E°$).
> - Outputs a **voltage proportional to the current** via a transimpedance amplifier (TIA).

**Op-Amp Implementation of a Potentiostat (for KTU derivations):**

The classic design uses **three op-amps**:
1. **Control amplifier** – compares $E_{app}$ with $E_{RE}$ and drives CE.
2. **Current-measuring (TIA) amplifier** – converts $i_{WE}$ into a measurable voltage: $V_{out} = -i_{WE} \cdot R_f$.
3. **Reference buffer** – high-impedance follower at RE.

### 2.1.3 The Cottrell Equation (MOST IMPORTANT FORMULA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cottrell Equation** (1929) governs the **chronoamperometric current** at a planar electrode after a potential step:

$$ i(t) = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot C_b \cdot \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot t}} $$

Where:
- $i(t)$ = faradaic current (Amperes, **A**)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of analyte
- $F$ = **Faraday constant = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (bold key constant)
- $A$ = electrode area (cm² or m²)
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration of electroactive species (mol·cm⁻³ or mol·m⁻³)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of the analyte (cm²·s⁻¹)
- $t$ = time elapsed after the potential step (s)
- $\pi$ = 3.14159...

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive meaning of Cottrell:** Right after a potential step, the current is **huge** (everything near the surface reacts at once). As time progresses, the diffusion layer grows ($\propto \sqrt{t}$), so the current **decays as $1/\sqrt{t}$**. This is the time-domain fingerprint of a pure diffusion-controlled process.

### 2.1.4 Steady-State (Disk Microelectrode) Amperometry

For **microelectrodes** (radius $r \le 25 \, \mu m$), a **steady-state current** is reached because radial diffusion dominates:

$$ i_{ss} = 4 \cdot n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot C_b \cdot r $$

This is **time-independent** — a huge advantage for continuous monitoring biosensors (e.g., implantable glucose sensors).

### 2.1.5 The Glucose Oxidase (GOx) Biosensor — Canonical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **first commercial amperometric biosensor** (Yellow Springs Instruments, 1975) and the personal glucose meter are both GOx-based. KTU repeatedly asks this as a 7- or 14-mark question.

**Enzymatic reaction at the GOx electrode (oxygen-dependent, "first generation"):**

$$ \text{Glucose} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOx}} \text{Gluconic Acid} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 $$

Two detection strategies:
1. **Measure O₂ consumption** → current from $\text{O}_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \to 2H_2O$ decreases.
2. **Measure H₂O₂ oxidation** → at $+0.6$ V vs Ag/AgCl: $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \to \text{O}_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$; current increases.

**Second-generation (mediator-based, e.g., ferrocene):**

$$ \text{Glucose} + \text{GOx(FAD)} \to \text{Gluconolactone} + \text{GOx(FADH}_2) $$

$$ \text{GOx(FADH}_2) + 2 \text{Med}_{ox} \to \text{GOx(FAD)} + 2 \text{Med}_{red} + 2H^+ $$

$$ 2 \text{Med}_{red} \xrightarrow{E_{app}} 2 \text{Med}_{ox} + 2e^- \quad (\text{current measured}) $$

Mediators (ferrocene, methylene blue, Prussian Blue) shuttle electrons to the electrode at **low overpotential** → eliminates $\text{O}_2$ interference.

### 2.1.6 Chronoamperometry vs. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)

| Technique | Waveform | Output | Use in Biosensors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chronoamperometry** | Single potential step | $i(t)$ | Concentration vs. time |
| **Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV)** | Linear ramp | $i(E)$ | Peak identification |
| **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)** | Forward + reverse ramp | $i(E)$ loop | Reversibility, $E°$ determination |
| **Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV)** | Pulses superimposed on ramp | Peak current | Ultra-low detection limits |
| **Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV)** | Square pulses | Peak current | Fast, high-sensitivity scans |

### 2.1.7 Butler–Volmer Equation (Kinetics Backbone)

The current–potential relationship at an electrode (without mass-transport limitation) is:

$$ i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{(1-\alpha) n F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( -\frac{\alpha n F \eta}{RT} \right) \right] $$

Where:
- $i_0$ = exchange current density (A·cm⁻²) — rate at equilibrium
- $\alpha$ = charge transfer coefficient (0 < $\alpha$ < 1; typically ≈ 0.5)
- $\eta$ = overpotential = $E_{app} - E_{eq}$ (V)
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

When $|\eta| \gg RT/F$ (large overpotential), the Butler–Volmer equation reduces to the **Tafel equation**:

$$ \eta = a + b \log(i) $$

---

## 2.2 POTENTIOMETRIC BIOSENSORS

### 2.2.1 Operational Principle

In a potentiometric biosensor:
1. **No (or negligible, < 1 pA) current** flows through the cell.
2. The **open-circuit potential (OCP)** between an **ion-selective electrode (ISE)** and a reference electrode is measured with a **high-input-impedance voltmeter** ($Z_{in} > 10^{12} \, \Omega$).
3. The measured potential follows the **Nernst Equation** and is proportional to the **logarithm** of the analyte activity (or concentration).

### 2.2.2 The Nernst Equation (FUNDAMENTAL FORMULA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nernst Equation** — the cornerstone of every potentiometric sensor:

$$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}} \right) $$

For an **ion-selective electrode** responding to a primary ion $X^{n+}$:

$$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{z_X F} \ln(a_X) = E^0 + \frac{0.0592}{z_X} \log_{10}(a_X) \quad \text{at } 25^\circ \text{C} $$

Where:
- $E$ = cell potential (V)
- $E^0$ = standard potential of the system (V)
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $T$ = 298.15 K (25 °C, standard)
- $F$ = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**
- $n$ or $z_X$ = number of electrons / charge of the ion
- $a_{ox}, a_{red}, a_X$ = activities of oxidized / reduced / analyte species (dimensionless, often ≈ concentration in mol/L for dilute solutions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nernst Slope at 25 °C:**
> - Monovalent ion ($z=1$): **59.16 mV / decade** of activity
> - Divalent ion ($z=2$): **29.58 mV / decade**
> - Trivalent ion ($z=3$): **19.72 mV / decade**

### 2.2.3 Components of a Potentiometric Biosensor

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Ion-Selective Membrane (ISM)** | Selective binding of target ion (glass, polymer, crystalline) |
| **Inner Reference Solution** | Constant activity of a reference ion (often 0.1 M KCl) |
| **Inner Reference Electrode** | Ag/AgCl |
| **External Reference Electrode** | Stable contact with sample (e.g., double-junction Ag/AgCl) |
| **High-$Z$ Voltmeter** | Measures $E$ without polarization |

### 2.2.4 Selectivity — The Nikolsky–Eisenman Equation

Real ISEs respond to multiple ions. The **Nicolsky–Eisenman equation** extends the Nernst equation:

$$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \left( a_i + \sum_{j \ne i} K_{ij} \cdot a_j^{z_i/z_j} \right) $$

Where $K_{ij}$ is the **selectivity coefficient** of the primary ion $i$ over the interferent $j$. A **smaller $K_{ij}$** means **better selectivity** (ideally $K_{ij} \ll 1$).

### 2.2.5 The pH Glass Electrode (Canonical Example)

- **Membrane**: thin pH-sensitive glass (e.g., Corning 015 — 72% SiO₂, 22% Na₂O, 6% CaO).
- **Mechanism**: H⁺ ions exchange with Na⁺ in the glass's outer gel layer, creating a **phase-boundary potential**.
- **Slope**: 59.16 mV / pH unit at 25 °C (Nernstian).
- **Alkaline error** at pH > 12 (Na⁺ interference) and **acid error** at pH < 1.

### 2.2.6 ISFET — Ion-Selective Field Effect Transistor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISFET** (Bergveld, 1970) replaces the metal gate of a MOSFET with an **ion-sensitive membrane + reference electrode**. As analyte ions bind to the gate, the **threshold voltage** $V_{th}$ shifts by:

$$ \Delta V_{th} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln(a_{ion}) $$

- Advantages: **miniaturizable, CMOS-compatible, fast response (< 1 s), low impedance output**.
- Used in **DNA sequencing (Ion Torrent / pH-mediated detection)** and **wearable pH sweat sensors**.

### 2.2.7 Biocatalytic Potentiometric Sensors (Enzyme-Based)

A common KTU design — the **enzyme** is immobilized on the ISE surface; the enzyme reaction produces or consumes an ion detected by the ISE:

| Target Analyte | Enzyme | Detected Ion (ISE) |
|---|---|---|
| Urea | Urease | NH₄⁺ (NH₃-sensing) |
| Glucose | GOx | H⁺ (pH) |
| Penicillin | β-lactamase | H⁺ (pH) |
| Creatinine | Creatininase | NH₄⁺ |
| Acetylcholine | Acetylcholinesterase | H⁺ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake: writing that "the enzyme directly measures the analyte" — it does not! The enzyme **catalyzes a reaction** that changes the local concentration of a **secondary ion (H⁺, NH₄⁺, CO₂...)**, which is what the ISE actually measures.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i(t) = nFAC_b \sqrt{D/\pi t}$ | Cottrell current | A |
| 2 | $i_{ss} = 4nFDC_b r$ | Steady-state microelectrode current | A |
| 3 | $E = E^0 + (RT/nF)\ln(a_{ox}/a_{red})$ | Nernst equation | V |
| 4 | $E = E^0 + (0.0592/z)\log_{10}(a_X)$ at 25 °C | Nernst (decade form) | V |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{th} = (RT/zF)\ln(a_{ion})$ | ISFET threshold shift | V |
| 6 | $i = i_0[\exp((1-\alpha)nF\eta/RT) - \exp(-\alpha nF\eta/RT)]$ | Butler–Volmer | A |
| 7 | $\eta = a + b\log(i)$ | Tafel equation | V |
| 8 | $E = E^0 + (RT/z_iF)\ln(a_i + \sum K_{ij}a_j^{z_i/z_j})$ | Nikolsky–Eisenman | V |
| 9 | $Q = nFN$ (Faraday's Law) | Total charge passed | C |
| 10 | $i = nFAkC$ (1st-order surface rxn) | Kinetic amperometric current | A |
| 11 | $S_{Nernst} = 59.16/z$ mV/decade | Nernstian slope at 25 °C | mV |
| 12 | $V_{out} = -i_{WE} R_f$ | TIA output voltage | V |

**Standard Constants to Memorize:**
- $F$ = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $RT/F$ at 25 °C = **0.02569 V**
- $(RT/F)\ln(10) = (2.303RT/F)$ = **0.05916 V** at 298.15 K

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - **Clinical diagnostics** — glucose, lactate, urea biosensors
> - **Environmental monitoring** — heavy-metal (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺) ISEs in water
> - **Food industry** — freshness (biogenic amines), pesticide detection
> - **Biodefense / Point-of-Care** — paper-based amperometric strips (e.g., COVID-19 antigen)
> - **Process control** — fermenter monitoring (glucose, ethanol)
> - **Wearable health** — sweat pH/Na⁺/K⁺ ISFET patches

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: From Fick's Laws to the Cottrell Equation (Amperometric)

**Goal:** Show that for a **planar electrode** with an instantaneously applied potential step, the resulting current is $i(t) = nFAC_b\sqrt{D/\pi t}$.

**Step 1 — Initial Condition (Semi-Infinite Diffusion).**
At $t = 0$, the potential $E_{app}$ is stepped to a value that instantly consumes every electroactive species that reaches the electrode. The boundary conditions are:

- At $t = 0$: $C(x, 0) = C_b$ (uniform bulk concentration)
- At $x = 0$ (electrode surface), $t > 0$: $C(0, t) = 0$ (instantaneous consumption)
- As $x \to \infty$: $C(\infty, t) = C_b$ (bulk unchanged)

**Step 2 — Apply Fick's Second Law (1-D diffusion):**

$$ \frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^2 C(x,t)}{\partial x^2} $$

**Step 3 — Use the Laplace Transform.** 
Let $C(x, s)$ be the Laplace transform of $C(x,t)$. With the boundary conditions, the transformed ODE solves to:

$$ \overline{C}(x, s) = \frac{C_b}{s} - \frac{C_b}{s} \exp\!\left( -x\sqrt{s/D} \right) $$

**Step 4 — Compute the surface concentration gradient.**
The flux at the electrode surface $x = 0$ is given by Fick's first law:

$$ J(0,t) = -D \left. \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} $$

Evaluating the derivative of $\overline{C}$ at $x=0$:

$$ \left. \frac{\partial \overline{C}}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = \frac{C_b}{\sqrt{Ds}} $$

**Step 5 — Invert the Laplace transform.**
The inverse transform of $1/\sqrt{s}$ is $1/\sqrt{\pi t}$. Therefore:

$$ \left. \frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = \frac{C_b}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} $$

**Step 6 — Compute the current density.**
Faraday's law gives the flux-to-current conversion. Current density $j = -nFJ$:

$$ j(t) = nF \cdot D \cdot \frac{C_b}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} = nF C_b \sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}} $$

**Step 7 — Multiply by electrode area $A$ to get total current:**

$$ \boxed{\,i(t) = \frac{n F A C_b \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi t}} = n F A C_b \sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}}\, } $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Cottrell equation**. The $1/\sqrt{t}$ decay is the **Cottrell tail** — a hallmark of pure planar diffusion.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Nernst Equation from Electrochemical Thermodynamics (Potentiometric)

**Goal:** Derive $E = E^0 + (RT/nF)\ln(a_{ox}/a_{red})$ from Gibbs free energy.

**Step 1 — Chemical reaction at the electrode:**

$$ \text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red} $$

**Step 2 — Free energy change of the electrochemical cell.**
The Gibbs free energy change for the cell reaction is:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q $$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient: $Q = a_{red}/a_{ox}$ (activities of products/reactants, raised to stoichiometric powers).

**Step 3 — Relate $\Delta G$ to the cell potential.**
The maximum (reversible) electrical work obtainable from the cell is:

$$ W_{elec} = -n F E \quad \text{at constant T, P} $$

At equilibrium, $W_{elec} = \Delta G$, hence:

$$ \Delta G = -n F E \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^0 = -n F E^0 $$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the $\Delta G$ expression:**

$$ -n F E = -n F E^0 + RT \ln\!\left( \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}} \right) $$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $-nF$ and flip the log argument:**

$$ E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left( \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}} \right) = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left( \frac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}} \right) $$

**Step 6 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (KTU-friendly form) at T = 298.15 K:**

$$ \boxed{\,E = E^0 + \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}} \right) \quad \text{(volts, at 25 °C)}\, } $$

> [!NOTE]
> For an ion-selective electrode responding to $X^{n+}$, the activity $a_X$ appears in the numerator (oxidized form is the analyte ion) and $E^0$ absorbs all constant terms including those from the reference electrode, internal filling solution, and junction potentials.

---

## 3.3 WORKED NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A chronoamperometric experiment is performed on a planar Pt electrode (area = 0.07 cm²) immersed in a 5.0 mM solution of $\text{K}_3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]$ (analyte, $n = 1$, $D = 7.6 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²·s⁻¹). Calculate the current at $t = 2.0$ s after the potential step, and determine the Nernst slope for this redox couple at 25 °C and at 37 °C.

**Given:**
- $A = 0.07$ cm²
- $C_b = 5.0$ mM = $5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ mol·cm⁻³
- $D = 7.6 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²·s⁻¹
- $n = 1$
- $F = 96{,}485$ C·mol⁻¹
- $t = 2.0$ s

**Step 1 — Plug into the Cottrell equation:**

$$ i(t) = \frac{n F A C_b \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{\pi t}} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{D}$:**

$$ \sqrt{D} = \sqrt{7.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.7568 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm·s}^{-1/2} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sqrt{\pi t}$:**

$$ \sqrt{\pi t} = \sqrt{\pi \times 2.0} = \sqrt{6.2832} = 2.5066 \text{ s}^{1/2} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator $n F A C_b \sqrt{D}$:**

$$ (1)(96{,}485)(0.07)(5.0 \times 10^{-6})(2.7568 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ = 96{,}485 \times 0.07 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.7568 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 96{,}485 \times 9.6499 \times 10^{-10} $$

$$ = 9.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A·cm·s}^{-1/2} $$

**Step 5 — Divide by $\sqrt{\pi t}$:**

$$ i(2.0\text{ s}) = \frac{9.31 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5066} = 3.71 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 37.1 \; \mu\text{A} $$

**Step 6 — Nernst slope at 25 °C (298.15 K):**

$$ S_{25} = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(10) = \frac{0.02569}{1}\ln(10) = 0.05916 \text{ V} = 59.16 \text{ mV/decade} $$

**Step 7 — Nernst slope at 37 °C (310.15 K):**

$$ S_{37} = \frac{R(310.15)}{nF}\ln(10) = \frac{8.314 \times 310.15 \times 2.303}{96{,}485 \times 1} $$

$$ = \frac{5{,}935.3}{96{,}485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV/decade} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The Nernst slope **increases linearly with temperature** (≈ +0.078 mV/decade per °C). This is why clinical pH and ion sensors must be **temperature-compensated**.

**Final Answers:**
- $i(2.0 \text{ s}) = 37.1 \; \mu\text{A}$
- Nernst slope: **59.16 mV/decade at 25 °C**, **61.5 mV/decade at 37 °C**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Amperometric vs. Potentiometric

| Parameter | Amperometric | Potentiometric |
|---|---|---|
| **Measured quantity** | Current $i$ (A) | Voltage $E$ (V) |
| **Applied quantity** | Constant $E_{app}$ | Zero / negligible current |
| **Concentration relationship** | $i \propto C$ (linear, per Cottrell) | $E \propto \log C$ (Nernst, logarithmic) |
| **Dynamic range** | Wide (3–4 decades) | Very wide (5–7 decades) |
| **Sensitivity** | High (sub-μM, even nM with DPV) | Moderate (μM typically) |
| **Selectivity** | Set by applied potential (electrochemical) | Set by membrane (chemical) |
| **Response time** | Seconds (Cottrell tail evolves) | Slower (10 s – several minutes) |
| **Instrumentation** | Potentiostat + TIA | High-Z voltmeter (FET-input) |
| **Interference** | Other electroactive species at $E_{app}$ | Other ions (selectivity coefficient $K_{ij}$) |
| **Typical LOD** | 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ M | 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ M |
| **Reference** | Ag/AgCl (current flows only at WE/CE) | Ag/AgCl (no current flows) |
| **KTU classic example** | GOx + ferrocene → glucose | NH₄⁺ ISE + urease → urea |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Amperometric Biosensor — Block Architecture (Potentiostat + Three-Electrode Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Eapp["Eapp Source\nReference DC Voltage"] --> CtrlOp["Control Op-Amp\n(Error Amplifier)"]
    RE["RE: Ag / AgCl\nReference Electrode"] --> Buf["High-Z Buffer\nVoltmeter Front-End"]
    Buf --> CtrlOp
    CtrlOp --> CE["CE: Pt Wire\nCounter Electrode"]
    WE["WE: Enzyme Layer\non Pt / Au / SPCE"] --> TIA["Transimpedance\nAmplifier Rf"]
    TIA --> ADC["ADC\n16-bit Sigma-Delta"]
    ADC --> DSP["Microcontroller\nLinearization + T-comp"]
    DSP --> Disp["LCD / BLE Display\nConcentration Output"]
    Sample["Analyte Solution\nBulk Cb"] -.Diffusion.-> WE
    Sample -.Ionic path.-> RE
    Sample -.Current return.-> CE
    style Eapp fill:#e1f5ff
    style DSP fill:#fff4e1
    style Disp fill:#e8f5e9
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **control op-amp** is the heart of the potentiostat — it forces $E_{WE}-E_{RE} = E_{app}$.
- The **current** flows only into the **WE**, through the TIA, to ground; **the reference electrode carries effectively zero current** (its potential is therefore stable).
- The **TIA** converts the nano- to micro-ampere currents into millivolt-level voltages readable by the ADC.

---

## 4.2 Potentiometric Biosensor — Block Architecture (ISFET + ISE Readout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Sample["Sample Solution\nwith Target Ion Xz+"] -->|"Binds to gate"| ISFET["ISFET\nor pH Glass Electrode"]
    RE2["Reference Electrode\nAg / AgCl"] -->|"Completes cell"| ISFET
    ISFET --> Source["Source Follower\nHigh-Z Buffer"]
    Source --> DiffAmp["Differential Amplifier\n(Vs - Vref)"]
    RE2 --> DiffAmp
    DiffAmp --> Lin["Log-Linearization\nE = E0 + S log aX"]
    Lin --> TC["Temperature\nCompensation"]
    TC --> DACout["Digital Output\nlog10 aX = pX"]
    style ISFET fill:#e1f5ff
    style Lin fill:#fff4e1
    style TC fill:#e8f5e9
```

---

## 4.3 GOx-Based Amperometric Glucose Sensor — Reaction & Electron Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Glc["Glucose\nin Blood / Sample"] -->|"Substrate"| GOx["GOx FAD\nOxidized Enzyme"]
    GOx -->|"Enzymatic rxn"| GOxH["GOx FADH2\nReduced Enzyme"]
    GOxH -->|"Redox shuttle"| Med["Mediator ox\ne.g. Ferrocene+"]
    Med --> MedR["Mediator red\nFerrocene"]
    MedR -->|"Eapp = +0.2 V\nvs Ag / AgCl"| WE["Working Electrode\nPt / SPCE"]
    WE -->|"Current i measured\nCottrell relation"| Circuit["Transimpedance\nAmplifier"]
    Glc2["Gluconolactone\n(Product)"] --> Glc
    O2["Dissolved O2\n(Natural co-substrate)"] -.Competes with mediator.-> GOx
    style GOx fill:#ffe0e0
    style Med fill:#e0f0ff
    style Circuit fill:#e8ffe0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Glucose reduces the GOx enzyme, the mediator re-oxidizes the enzyme (regenerating it), and the reduced mediator is then electrochemically oxidized at the working electrode — producing the **measurable current**. The bypass arrow shows that $\text{O}_2$ competes as a natural co-substrate (first-generation sensor limitation).

---

## 4.4 Nernst Response — Calibration Curve Shape

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LowC["Low Activity Region\n(Linear, Nernstian slope S = 59.16 mV / dec)"] --> MidC["Mid-Range\n(Useful analytical window)"]
    MidC --> HighC["High Activity\n(Sub-Nernstian, slope decreases)"]
    HighC --> Det["Detection Limit\n(Signal < 5% of noise)"]
    Sat["Saturation / Membrane Fouling\n(Non-Nernstian roll-off)"] -.-> Det
    style LowC fill:#e8f5e9
    style MidC fill:#fff9c4
    style HighC fill:#ffccbc
    style Det fill:#ffcdd2
```

**Key regions of an ISE calibration curve:**
- **Linear (Nernstian) region** — slope = 59.16 mV/decade (monovalent) — best analytical range.
- **Sub-Nernstian roll-off** at high $a_X$ — membrane saturation / co-ion interference.
- **Lower Detection Limit (LDL)** — the IUPAC-recommended LDL is the activity where the calibration line intersects the extrapolated constant-potential "noise band."

---

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Which Electrochemical Biosensor to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Analyte to detect?"] --> Type{"Electroactive\nat accessible E?"}
    Type -->|"Yes"| Amp["Choose AMPEROMETRIC\nApply E at diffusion plateau\nCurrent proportional to C"]
    Type -->|"No, but ionizable"| Pot["Choose POTENTIOMETRIC\nMeasure OCP vs reference\nNernstian log relationship"]
    Type -->|"Complex / multi-ion"| Con["Choose CONDUCTOMETRIC\nMeasure ΔG of solution\nNon-specific"]
    Amp --> Sensitivity{"Need LOD < nM?"}
    Sensitivity -->|"Yes"| DPV["Use DPV / SWV\nPulse techniques"]
    Sensitivity -->|"No"| ChA["Use Chronoamperometry\n(Steady-state at microelectrode)"]
    Pot --> ISFET{"Miniaturization needed?"}
    ISFET -->|"Yes"| ISFETsel["Use ISFET\nCMOS-compatible"]
    ISFET -->|"No"| Glass["Use Glass / Polymer ISE\n(Benchtop / field)"]
    style Amp fill:#e1f5ff
    style Pot fill:#fff4e1
    style DPV fill:#e8f5e9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between an amperometric biosensor and a potentiometric biosensor with respect to the quantity measured, the signal–concentration relationship, and one representative example each. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Amperometric | Potentiometric |
|---|---|---|
| Quantity measured | Current $i$ (A) at constant $E$ | Voltage $E$ (V) at zero current |
| Relationship | $i \propto C$ (linear, Cottrell) | $E \propto \log C$ (Nernst) |
| Example | Glucose oxidase (GOx) electrode | pH glass electrode / ISFET |
| Instrumentation | Potentiostat + TIA | High-impedance voltmeter |

*(Any three crisp distinctions in a table form fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Nernst equation for a potentiometric ion-selective electrode. Calculate the theoretical slope (in mV/decade) for a divalent cation at 25 °C. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

$$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{z_X F} \ln(a_X) $$

At 25 °C, the Nernstian slope is:

$$ S = \frac{0.05916}{z_X} \text{ V/decade} = \frac{59.16}{2} = 29.58 \text{ mV/decade} $$

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Module 3 carries a compulsory 14-mark question with internal choice (either-or). The model answer keys below match the typical 7 + 7 mark split.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Amperometric Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** *Derive the Cottrell equation for a planar electrode starting from Fick's laws. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Planar (1-D) semi-infinite diffusion toward the electrode.
2. Excess supporting electrolyte — migration is eliminated.
3. No convection (solution is quiescent).
4. Instantaneous potential step that drives surface concentration of analyte to **zero**.
5. Homogeneous initial bulk concentration $C_b$.
6. Constant diffusion coefficient $D$.

**Derivation:**

Fick's second law in 1-D:

$$ \frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C(x,t)}{\partial x^2} $$

**Boundary conditions:**

$$ C(x, 0) = C_b, \quad C(0, t>0) = 0, \quad C(x \to \infty, t) = C_b $$

**Laplace transform** in $t$ → $s$:

$$ \overline{C}(x, s) = \frac{C_b}{s} - \frac{C_b}{s} \exp\!\left( -x \sqrt{\frac{s}{D}} \right) $$

**Surface gradient at $x=0$:**

$$ \left. \frac{\partial \overline{C}}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = \frac{C_b}{\sqrt{D s}} $$

**Inversion** of $1/\sqrt{s}$ gives $1/\sqrt{\pi t}$:

$$ \left. \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = \frac{C_b}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} $$

**Faraday's conversion** to current density $j = nFJ = nF D (\partial C/\partial x)$:

$$ j(t) = nF C_b \sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}} $$

**Total current** $i = jA$:

$$ \boxed{\, i(t) = n F A C_b \sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}} \,} $$

---

**(b)** *A planar Pt electrode (area 0.05 cm²) is stepped to a diffusion-limited potential in a 2.5 mM $\text{K}_3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]$ solution ($n=1$, $D = 7.6 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²/s). Compute (i) the current at 0.5 s and (ii) the steady-state current if the electrode is replaced by a micro-disk of radius 10 μm. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — Cottrell current at t = 0.5 s:**

$$ i(0.5) = \frac{(1)(96{,}485)(0.05)(2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \sqrt{7.6 \times 10^{-6}}}{\sqrt{\pi \times 0.5}} $$

- Numerator: $96{,}485 \times 0.05 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01206$ C·cm⁻³·cm² = $1.206 \times 10^{-2}$
- $\sqrt{D} = 2.757 \times 10^{-3}$ cm·s⁻¹ᐟ²
- Numerator product: $1.206 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.757 \times 10^{-3} = 3.326 \times 10^{-5}$
- $\sqrt{\pi t} = \sqrt{1.5708} = 1.2533$ s¹ᐟ²

$$ i(0.5) = \frac{3.326 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2533} = 2.654 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 26.5 \; \mu\text{A} $$

**Part (ii) — Steady-state microelectrode current:**

$ r = 10 \, \mu\text{m} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} $ cm.

$$ i_{ss} = 4 n F D C_b r = 4 \times 1 \times 96{,}485 \times 7.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 4 \times 96{,}485 \times 1.9 \times 10^{-14} $$

$$ = 7.33 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A} = 7.33 \text{ nA} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Costs ~2 marks):**
> - Forgetting to **convert units** consistently (mM → mol/cm³, μm → cm) — most common error.
> - Confusing the Cottrell formula's denominator: some students write $\pi t$ (no square root) — wrong.
> - For microelectrode, students sometimes use $i = nFAC\sqrt{D/\pi t}$ instead of the steady-state formula $i_{ss} = 4nFDCr$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Potentiometric Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** *Derive the Nernst equation from electrochemical thermodynamics. Hence obtain the Nernstian slope at 37 °C for a monovalent ion. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

For the half-cell reaction $\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$:

**Step 1:** Gibbs free energy of the cell:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q, \quad \text{with } Q = \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}} $$

**Step 2:** Electrical work from a galvanic cell:

$$ \Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^0 = -nFE^0 $$

**Step 3:** Equate and solve:

$$ -nFE = -nFE^0 + RT \ln\!\left( \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}} \right) $$

$$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left( \frac{a_{ox}}{a_{red}} \right) $$

**Step 4 — Slope at 37 °C (T = 310.15 K):**

$$ S = \frac{RT \ln(10)}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 310.15 \times 2.303}{1 \times 96{,}485} $$

$$ S = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV/decade} $$

---

**(b)** *Explain the construction and working of a glass pH electrode. How does an ISFET differ from a conventional ion-selective electrode? List two advantages of the ISFET. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Glass pH electrode construction:**
- A **thin-walled bulb** (~ 0.1 mm) of pH-sensitive glass (e.g., Corning 015: 72% SiO₂, 22% Na₂O, 6% CaO).
- Inside the bulb: **0.1 M HCl** + an **internal Ag/AgCl reference electrode**.
- The bulb is mounted in a sturdy stem; the external circuit is completed by an **external reference electrode** (often a calomel or double-junction Ag/AgCl) immersed in the test solution.

**Working mechanism:**
1. **Hydration gel layer formation** — when the glass is soaked in water, a 10–100 nm thick gel layer of swollen, hydrated silicate forms on both sides.
2. **Ion-exchange equilibrium** — H⁺ ions in the solution exchange with Na⁺ in the outer gel layer:

$$ \text{H}^+_{\text{soln}} + \text{Na}^+_{\text{gel}} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+_{\text{gel}} + \text{Na}^+_{\text{soln}} $$

3. A **phase-boundary potential** $E_b$ develops at each gel–solution interface. The net cell potential is the difference of the two boundary potentials (inner and outer):

$$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{F} \ln(a_{H^+,\text{outer}}) $$

4. The **Nernstian slope = 59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C**.

**ISFET vs. Conventional ISE:**

| Aspect | Conventional ISE (Glass) | ISFET |
|---|---|---|
| Transduction | Direct potential on a high-Z voltmeter | Threshold voltage shift in MOSFET |
| Membrane | Bulky glass / polymer | Thin film (Si₃N₄, Ta₂O₅, Al₂O₃) |
| Impedance | High (10⁸ Ω) | Low (FET output) |
| Size | cm-scale | μm-scale, on-chip |
| Fabrication | Hand-assembled | CMOS-compatible batch process |

**Two advantages of ISFET:**
1. **Miniaturization and on-chip integration** with CMOS readout circuitry.
2. **Low output impedance** — does not require a high-input-impedance voltmeter; can drive standard electronics directly.

(Other acceptable advantages: fast response, mechanically robust, can be arrayed for multi-ion detection.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Costs ~2–3 marks):**
> - Writing "ISFET measures pH directly" — it doesn't. It measures a **threshold voltage shift** caused by surface potential change due to ion binding.
> - Confusing the **gel layer** with a "membrane filter" — the gel is a **swollen, ion-exchangeable surface layer**, not a filter.
> - Forgetting to mention that the **bulb is preconditioned** (soaked in water/dilute acid) before use — without this, the gel layer is missing and the electrode behaves Nernstian only after several minutes.
> - For ISFET advantages, students often write vague phrases like "better" instead of concrete, KTU-accepted points (miniaturization, low $Z_{out}$, CMOS-compatible).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Electrochemical Biosensors: One-Page Recap**

### Amperometric
- ✅ Measures **current** at a **fixed applied potential** (chronoamperometry at diffusion plateau).
- ✅ Uses a **3-electrode potentiostat**: WE, RE, CE.
- ✅ Governed by the **Cottrell equation** $i(t) = nFAC_b\sqrt{D/\pi t}$ — characteristic **$1/\sqrt{t}$ decay**.
- ✅ Microelectrodes give **time-independent** steady-state current $i_{ss} = 4nFDCr$.
- ✅ Classical example: **Glucose + GOx → H₂O₂ (or mediator) → current at WE**.
- ✅ Pulse techniques (DPV, SWV) push LOD to **nM** levels.
- ✅ **Butler–Volmer** governs electrode kinetics; **Tafel** is its high-$\eta$ limit.
- ✅ **Oxygen dependence** is a drawback of 1st-gen sensors — fixed by **mediators** (ferrocene) → 2nd-gen.

### Potentiometric
- ✅ Measures **open-circuit potential** at **zero current** with a high-Z ($> 10^{12} \Omega$) voltmeter.
- ✅ Governed by **Nernst equation** $E = E^0 + (RT/nF)\ln(a_{ox}/a_{red})$.
- ✅ Nernstian slope at 25 °C: **59.16 / $z$ mV per decade** (monovalent = 59.16, divalent = 29.58).
- ✅ **Selectivity** quantified by the **Nikolsky–Eisenman equation** with $K_{ij}$.
- ✅ Two canonical examples: **glass pH electrode** and **ISFET** (Bergveld 1970).
- ✅ Enzyme-based potentiometric sensors detect a **secondary ion** (H⁺, NH₄⁺, CO₂) generated by the enzyme reaction — never the analyte directly.
- ✅ Temperature compensation is **mandatory** (slope ∝ T).

### Quick-Fire Constants
- ⚡ $F$ = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹**
- ⚡ $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- ⚡ $RT/F$ at 25 °C = **0.02569 V**
- ⚡ $2.303RT/F$ at 25 °C = **0.05916 V** (the "magic 59.16 mV")

### Quick-Fire Definitions (Most-Frequently Asked)
- ⚡ **Cottrell equation** — time-dependent diffusion-limited current at a planar electrode.
- ⚡ **Nernst equation** — equilibrium potential of an electrochemical half-cell.
- ⚡ **Butler–Volmer** — current–potential relation including kinetic and overpotential terms.
- ⚡ **Nernstian slope** — 59.16 / $z$ mV per activity decade at 25 °C.
- ⚡ **Selectivity coefficient $K_{ij}$** — Nikolsky–Eisenman parameter (smaller = more selective).
- ⚡ **ISFET** — Ion-Sensitive FET; MOSFET with ion-sensitive gate; CMOS-compatible.
- ⚡ **TIA (Transimpedance Amplifier)** — op-amp circuit that converts current → voltage ($V_{out} = -iR_f$).

### Common Examiner Traps
- ⚠️ Mixing up **amperometric** (Cottrell, linear in C) with **potentiometric** (Nernst, log in C).
- ⚠️ Forgetting to convert **mM → mol/cm³** in Cottrell problems.
- ⚠️ Confusing the **Nernst slope sign** — positive slope means $E$ increases with $\log a_{ox}$.
- ⚠️ Treating **enzyme** as a "magic detector" — it only **catalyzes a reaction** producing a measurable secondary ion or mediator.
- ⚠️ Using a **normal voltmeter** for potentiometry — it would polarize the electrode; you need $Z_{in} > 10^{12} \, \Omega$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Biosensors: Surface Plasmon Resonance, Evanescent Wave Fluorescence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT304 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Biosensors: Surface Plasmon Resonance, Evanescent Wave Fluorescence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optical Biosensors — Formal Definition
An **Optical Biosensor** is an analytical device that converts a biological recognition event (antigen–antibody binding, DNA hybridization, enzyme–substrate reaction, receptor–ligand interaction) into a measurable optical signal (change in refractive index, fluorescence intensity, wavelength shift, polarization, or interference pattern). According to the KTU 2024 scheme, optical biosensors form the largest commercial subclass of label-free and label-based detection platforms because light offers high spatial resolution, immunity to electromagnetic interference, and the ability to interrogate surfaces in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Optical transducers exploit either *evanescent field phenomena* (SPR, TIRF, waveguide modes) or *propagating wave phenomena* (fluorescence, absorbance, luminescence, interferometry). Both phenomena discussed in this module are evanescent in nature.

---

### 1.2 Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) — Formal Definition
**Surface Plasmon Resonance** is the resonant, collective oscillation of conduction electrons at a metal–dielectric interface when excited by p-polarized (TM) light of a specific incidence angle or wavelength. The resonance condition occurs when the component of the incident photon momentum parallel to the interface matches the surface plasmon wavevector. Any change in the refractive index of the dielectric adjacent to the metal film alters the resonance condition, producing a measurable shift in the resonance angle, wavelength, or intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Interest (SPR):**
> - Plasma frequency of gold ($\omega_p$) ≈ **$1.37 \times 10^{16}$ rad/s**
> - Plasma frequency of silver ($\omega_p$) ≈ **$1.36 \times 10^{16}$ rad/s**
> - Typical gold film thickness ≈ **50 nm**
> - SPR sensitivity ceiling ≈ **$10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ RIU** (Refractive Index Units)
> - Sensor penetration depth ≈ **100–300 nm**

---

### 1.3 Evanescent Wave Fluorescence (EWF) — Formal Definition
**Evanescent Wave Fluorescence** (also known as Total Internal Reflection Fluorescence, **TIRF**) is an optical sensing technique in which fluorophores located within the evanescent field of a totally internally reflected light beam are selectively excited. Because the evanescent wave decays exponentially into the lower-index medium with a characteristic penetration depth of typically **100–200 nm**, only molecules physically bound or adsorbed at the transducer surface fluoresce, providing an inherently surface-selective, low-background detection mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants of Interest (EWF):**
> - Penetration depth ($d_p$) ≈ **100–200 nm** in TIRF biosensors
> - Numerical aperture of TIRF objective ≥ **1.45** (oil immersion)
> - Typical laser wavelengths: **405 nm, 488 nm, 561 nm, 640 nm**
> - Detection limit in single-molecule TIRF: **~10–100 fluorophores/μm²**

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogies — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy for SPR — "The Whispering-Gallery Resonance":**
Imagine striking a metal drumhead (the gold film). The drum vibrates only at one specific rhythm. If you throw tiny pebbles (biomolecules) onto the drumskin, the mass loading alters the rhythm at which it vibrates. SPR is exactly that: the gold film's electrons vibrate at a specific "rhythm" (resonance angle), and biomolecules landing on it shift that rhythm. We do not weigh the molecules directly — we measure the shift in the rhythm.

**Analogy for Evanescent Wave Fluorescence — "The Glow Stick at the Pool Edge":**
Picture a swimming pool at night. Sunlight hits the water surface and refracts inward. If you dive underwater and look up, you can sometimes see a thin shimmering layer of light just at the surface, even where no light *should* penetrate. This thin "skin of light" is the evanescent wave — light that does not actually propagate but decays within roughly 100 nm of the interface. If you sprinkled glowing dye *only* at the surface, it would shine brilliantly, while dye deeper in the pool would remain dark. TIRF biosensors exploit this principle to selectively "see" only the molecules attached to the sensor surface, ignoring the vast majority floating freely in solution.

> [!TIP]
> **Why both techniques are powerful:** They are inherently **surface-sensitive** — they cannot "see" anything beyond ~300 nm of the transducer surface. This makes them immune to bulk solution interference, which is the dominant noise source in conventional optical detection.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Optical Biosensor Principles

| Optical Principle | Physical Phenomenon | Typical Detection Limit | Label Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) | Refractive-index-sensitive resonance | $10^{-6}$ RIU | No (Label-free) |
| Evanescent Wave Fluorescence (TIRF) | Surface-restricted fluorescence excitation | pM–nM | Yes (Fluorophore) |
| Localized SPR (LSPR) | Nanoparticle plasmonic shift | $10^{-5}$ RIU | No |
| Waveguide-mode spectroscopy | Guided evanescent mode coupling | $10^{-6}$ RIU | No |
| Interferometric (Mach–Zehnder) | Optical path-length change | $10^{-7}$ RIU | No |
| Surface-Enhanced Raman (SERS) | Plasmon-enhanced inelastic scattering | nM–fM | No (Raman label) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Surface Plasmon Resonance — Operational Physics

#### 2.1.1 What is a Surface Plasmon?
A **surface plasmon polariton (SPP)** is a quasi-2D electromagnetic wave bound to a metal–dielectric interface, arising from the coupling of the photon field to the collective oscillation of conduction-band electrons in the metal. The wave propagates along the interface and decays exponentially into both the metal and the dielectric.

The SPP is **TM-polarized only** (TE modes cannot excite it because the normal component of the electric field is required to drive electron oscillation). This is why Kretschmann and Otto configurations use p-polarized light exclusively.

#### 2.1.2 The Resonance Condition — Why a Prism is Required

The wavevector of light in any medium is:
$$k_0 = \frac{\omega}{c}$$

The in-plane component of the incident light (after passing through a prism of refractive index $n_p$) at incidence angle $\theta$ is:
$$k_x = k_0 \, n_p \, \sin\theta = \frac{\omega}{c} \, n_p \, \sin\theta$$

However, the surface plasmon wavevector is **larger** than $k_0$ in the dielectric (because the metal slows the wave). For a metal of complex permittivity $\varepsilon_m$ and a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon_d$, the SPP wavevector is:
$$k_{sp} = \frac{\omega}{c} \, \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \, \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$$

Because $\varepsilon_m$ is negative for gold/silver below the plasma frequency, $k_{sp} > k_0 n_d$, meaning direct illumination from the dielectric side cannot satisfy momentum matching. **A high-refractive-index prism is required** to boost $k_x$ above $k_0 n_d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance Condition (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$k_x = k_{sp} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad n_p \, \sin\theta_{res} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \, \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$$
> This is the equation that defines $\theta_{res}$, the angle at which the reflected intensity dips to a minimum.

#### 2.1.3 Kretschmann vs. Otto Configurations

**Kretschmann Configuration** (most commercially prevalent):
- Prism → Thin metal film (40–60 nm) → Sample (dielectric)
- Light undergoes attenuated total reflection at the prism–metal interface.
- The evanescent field penetrates the metal and excites SPPs on the metal–sample interface.
- **Advantage:** Easier to fabricate and flow-sample compatible.
- **Disadvantage:** Metal film must be very thin (<~60 nm for gold at 633 nm) to allow evanescent coupling.

**Otto Configuration:**
- Prism → Air gap (~100–1000 nm) → Metal film → Sample
- SPP excited at the prism–metal interface across the air gap.
- **Advantage:** Avoids direct sample–metal contact.
- **Disadvantage:** Extremely sensitive to air-gap width; impractical for biosensing.

#### 2.1.4 Metal Dielectric Function — Drude Model

The frequency-dependent complex dielectric function of a metal is given by the Drude free-electron model:
$$\varepsilon_m(\omega) = \varepsilon_\infty - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2 + i\gamma\omega}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_\infty$ = high-frequency background permittivity (≈ 1 for gold, 3.7 for silver)
- $\omega_p$ = bulk plasma frequency
- $\gamma$ = electron scattering rate (collision frequency)

> [!NOTE]
> For a good plasmonic metal: **$\text{Re}(\varepsilon_m) < 0$** and **$\vert \text{Im}(\varepsilon_m) \vert$ is small**. Gold, silver, and aluminum satisfy this in the visible–near-IR.

#### 2.1.5 Sensitivity of an SPR Biosensor

When biomolecules bind to the metal surface, the local refractive index of the dielectric layer ($n_a$) increases by $\Delta n_a$. The resulting shift in resonance angle is:
$$\Delta\theta_{res} = S \cdot \Delta n_a$$

where the sensitivity $S = \dfrac{d\theta_{res}}{dn_a}$ depends on:
- Operating wavelength
- Metal choice (gold vs. silver)
- Prism refractive index
- Choice of interrogation method (angular vs. wavelength vs. intensity)

The surface mass density of bound biomolecules is related to the refractive-index shift by the **de Feijter equation**:
$$\Gamma = \frac{d_n \, (n_a - n_{buffer})}{dn/dc}$$

where $d_n$ is the effective film thickness and $dn/dc$ ≈ 0.18–0.20 cm³/g for most proteins.

#### 2.1.6 Practical Engineering Utility
SPR biosensors are the **workhorse of label-free kinetics** in pharmaceutical research, used to measure association rate ($k_a$), dissociation rate ($k_d$), and equilibrium dissociation constant ($K_D$) for drug–target interactions in real time without labels. Commercial platforms (Biacore, Reichert, Nicoya) dominate drug-discovery pipelines.

---

### 2.2 Evanescent Wave Fluorescence — Operational Physics

#### 2.2.1 Total Internal Reflection — Foundation
When light traveling in a high-index medium ($n_1$) strikes a low-index medium ($n_2$) at an angle $\theta$ greater than the **critical angle** $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2/n_1)$, all light is reflected. Despite this, Maxwell's equations demand continuity of the tangential electric field at the boundary. The resulting transmitted field is **non-propagating** and decays exponentially with distance $z$ into the low-index medium:
$$E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-z / d_p}$$

where $d_p$ is the **penetration depth**.

#### 2.2.2 Penetration Depth Derivation (Summary)

For light incident at angle $\theta > \theta_c$, Snell's law formally gives:
$$n_1 \sin\theta = n_2 \sin\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\phi = \frac{i}{n_2}\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}$$

The z-component of the wavevector in medium 2 becomes purely imaginary:
$$k_{z,2} = \frac{\omega}{c} \, n_2 \cos\phi = \frac{i\omega}{c}\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}$$

This imaginary $k_z$ yields exponential decay with characteristic length:
$$d_p = \frac{c}{\omega \, \sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \, \sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}}$$

The intensity falls off twice as fast:
$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-2z / d_p}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing the incidence angle $\theta$ **decreases** $d_p$, providing tighter surface confinement but lower excitation efficiency. The optimal $\theta$ is typically chosen such that $d_p \approx$ 100–200 nm for cell-membrane biosensing.

#### 2.2.3 TIRF Microscope — Optical Configuration
A TIRF microscope illuminates the sample at an angle greater than $\theta_c$ from below (or above) through a high-NA objective (NA ≥ 1.45). The evanescent field excites fluorophores in a thin optical section adjacent to the coverslip, while fluorophores deeper than ~200 nm remain dark. This gives:
- **Axial resolution** of ~100 nm (single-molecule localization precision)
- **Signal-to-background ratio** > 10× better than epi-illumination
- **Single-molecule sensitivity** routinely achievable with EMCCD cameras

#### 2.2.4 Practical Engineering Utility
TIRF biosensing is central to **single-molecule detection, membrane-receptor studies, vesicle fusion events (SNARE proteins), DNA curtain assays, and surface-immobilized immunoassays** where the surface-bound analyte must be distinguished from bulk solution-phase background.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield — memorize the boxed equations for full-mark answers.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k_{sp} = \dfrac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$ | Surface plasmon wavevector at metal–dielectric interface | $m^{-1}$; requires $\text{Re}(\varepsilon_m) < 0$ |
| 2 | $n_p \sin\theta_{res} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$ | **SPR resonance condition** (Kretschmann) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_m(\omega) = \varepsilon_\infty - \dfrac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2 + i\gamma\omega}$ | Drude free-electron metal dielectric function | complex; $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 4 | $\Delta\theta_{res} = S \cdot \Delta n_a$ | Angular shift for biomolecular binding | $S$ in deg/RIU, $\Delta n_a$ in RIU |
| 5 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{d_n (n_a - n_{buffer})}{dn/dc}$ | Surface mass density (de Feijter) | $ng/mm^2$; $dn/dc \approx 0.18$ cm³/g |
| 6 | $\theta_c = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | Critical angle for total internal reflection | requires $n_1 > n_2$ |
| 7 | $d_p = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}}$ | **Penetration depth of evanescent wave** | meters; for $\theta > \theta_c$ |
| 8 | $E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-z/d_p}$ | Evanescent electric-field decay | $z$ measured into lower-$n$ medium |
| 9 | $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-2z/d_p}$ | Evanescent intensity decay | $z$ measured into lower-$n$ medium |
| 10 | $\Phi_{FRET} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (r/R_0)^6}$ | FRET efficiency (often paired with TIRF) | $R_0 \approx 5$ nm for Cy3/Cy5 |

**Boundary Conditions Summary:**
- SPR: valid only for **TM (p-polarized) light**, metal thickness 40–60 nm (Kretschmann), $\text{Re}(\varepsilon_m) < 0$.
- TIRF/EWF: valid only for $\theta > \theta_c$, $n_1 > n_2$, decay length 100–300 nm.

**Engineering Applications Field Mapping:**

| Technique | Dominant Industry Use-Case |
|---|---|
| SPR (Kretschmann) | Drug-discovery kinetics (Biacore), food adulteration detection, COVID antigen tests |
| SPR Imaging (SPRI) | Microarray-based parallel ligand screening |
| TIRF | Single-molecule enzymology, vesicle fusion, membrane-receptor dynamics |
| LSPR | Point-of-care diagnostics, colorimetric nanoparticle assays |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Surface Plasmon Wavevector from Maxwell's Equations

We begin with the interface between a metal (region 1, $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_m$) and a dielectric (region 2, $\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_d$). The metal occupies $z < 0$ and the dielectric occupies $z > 0$. We seek an **SPP wave propagating along $x$** and decaying in $\pm z$.

**Step 1 — Field ansatz for TM polarization:**
For TM-polarized waves, the magnetic field is in the $y$-direction only. We write:
$$H_y(z) = H_0 \, e^{i k_x x} \, e^{i k_{z,j} z}$$

with subscripts $j = m, d$ denoting medium.

**Step 2 — Apply Maxwell's curl equation:**
From $\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = -i\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_j \mathbf{E}$, the in-plane and normal electric-field components are:
$$E_x^{(j)} = \frac{k_{z,j}}{\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_j} \, H_0 \, e^{i k_x x} \, e^{i k_{z,j} z}$$
$$E_z^{(j)} = -\frac{k_x}{\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_j} \, H_0 \, e^{i k_x x} \, e^{i k_{z,j} z}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the wave equation:**
In a source-free medium, $\nabla^2 \mathbf{H} + k_0^2 \varepsilon_j \mathbf{H} = 0$, which requires:
$$k_x^2 + k_{z,j}^2 = k_0^2 \varepsilon_j$$

Hence:
$$k_{z,j}^2 = k_0^2 \varepsilon_j - k_x^2$$

For bound modes, we require $\text{Im}(k_{z,m}) > 0$ and $\text{Im}(k_{z,d}) > 0$, ensuring exponential decay into both media.

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions at $z = 0$:**
Continuity of $H_y$ and $E_x$ across the interface gives:
$$\frac{k_{z,m}}{\varepsilon_m} = \frac{k_{z,d}}{\varepsilon_d}$$

Squaring and using $k_{z,j}^2 = k_0^2\varepsilon_j - k_x^2$:
$$\frac{k_0^2 \varepsilon_m - k_x^2}{\varepsilon_m^2} = \frac{k_0^2 \varepsilon_d - k_x^2}{\varepsilon_d^2}$$

Solving algebraically for $k_x^2$:
$$\varepsilon_d^2 (k_0^2 \varepsilon_m - k_x^2) = \varepsilon_m^2 (k_0^2 \varepsilon_d - k_x^2)$$
$$\varepsilon_d^2 k_0^2 \varepsilon_m - \varepsilon_d^2 k_x^2 = \varepsilon_m^2 k_0^2 \varepsilon_d - \varepsilon_m^2 k_x^2$$
$$k_x^2 (\varepsilon_m^2 - \varepsilon_d^2) = k_0^2 \varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d (\varepsilon_m - \varepsilon_d)$$
$$k_x^2 = k_0^2 \, \frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}$$

**Step 5 — Final SPP wavevector:**
$$\boxed{k_{sp} = k_x = \frac{\omega}{c} \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}}$$

This is the **dispersion relation** of the surface plasmon polariton. Notice that because $\varepsilon_m < 0$ and $\varepsilon_d > 0$, the denominator $(\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d) < 0$, but the numerator $\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d < 0$ as well, so the ratio is **positive** — a real wavevector exists, confirming a propagating SPP.

**Step 6 — Momentum-matching via prism:**
The in-plane wavevector of light passing through a prism is $k_x^{light} = k_0 n_p \sin\theta$. At resonance:
$$k_0 n_p \sin\theta_{res} = k_0 \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}$$

Hence:
$$\boxed{\sin\theta_{res} = \frac{1}{n_p}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_d}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_d}}}$$

This is the equation that defines the angle at which the reflected intensity falls to a minimum in a Kretschmann SPR sensor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Evanescent-Wave Penetration Depth

We consider light propagating in medium 1 ($n_1$) incident on medium 2 ($n_2 < n_1$) at angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Snell's law formal extension:**
$$n_1 \sin\theta = n_2 \sin\phi$$

For $\theta > \theta_c$, $\sin\phi > 1$, which is unphysical for a propagating wave. We accept this formally and write:
$$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\phi} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_1}{n_2}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta}$$

Inside the square root the argument is negative, so:
$$\cos\phi = i \sqrt{\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta - 1} = \frac{i}{n_2}\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}$$

**Step 2 — Wavevector in medium 2:**
The wavevector in medium 2 has magnitude $k_2 = (2\pi/\lambda) n_2$. Its $z$-component is:
$$k_{z,2} = k_2 \cos\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n_2 \cdot \frac{i}{n_2}\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2} = \frac{2\pi i}{\lambda}\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — Electric field in medium 2:**
The transmitted field has the form:
$$E_y^{(2)}(x, z, t) = E_0 \, e^{i(k_x x - \omega t)} \, e^{i k_{z,2} z}$$

Substituting the imaginary $k_{z,2}$:
$$E_y^{(2)}(x, z, t) = E_0 \, e^{i(k_x x - \omega t)} \, e^{-\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2} \cdot (2\pi z / \lambda)}$$

**Step 4 — Definition of penetration depth:**
By definition, the penetration depth $d_p$ is the distance $z$ at which the field amplitude drops to $1/e$ of its value at the interface:
$$E_y^{(2)}(d_p) = \frac{E_0}{e}$$

This gives:
$$\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2} \cdot d_p = 1$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d_p$:**
$$\boxed{d_p = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical illustration:**
For $n_1 = 1.52$ (glass coverslip), $n_2 = 1.33$ (water), $\lambda = 488$ nm, $\theta = 70°$:
$$\sqrt{n_1^2 \sin^2\theta - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.52)^2 (0.9397)^2 - (1.33)^2} = \sqrt{2.041 - 1.769} = \sqrt{0.272} \approx 0.522$$
$$d_p = \frac{488 \times 10^{-9}}{2\pi \times 0.522} = \frac{488 \times 10^{-9}}{3.28} \approx 1.49 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 149 \text{ nm}$$

This ~150 nm penetration depth is exactly the optical sectioning thickness used in TIRF microscopy for single-molecule membrane-receptor studies.

**Step 7 — Intensity decay:**
Because intensity is proportional to the square of the electric field:
$$\boxed{I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-2z/d_p}}$$

At $z = d_p$, the intensity is $e^{-2} \approx 13.5\%$ of its surface value; at $z = 3 d_p$, it is below 0.25% — effectively zero.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity of Angular-Interrogation SPR Sensor

We want to relate the change in resonance angle $\Delta\theta_{res}$ to the change in surface refractive index $\Delta n_a$ caused by biomolecular binding.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the resonance condition:**
Starting from:
$$\sin\theta_{res} = \frac{1}{n_p}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_a}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a}}$$

where $\varepsilon_a = n_a^2$ is the dielectric constant of the analyte.

Differentiating both sides with respect to $n_a$ (treating $\varepsilon_m$ as constant since the metal is unchanged):
$$\cos\theta_{res} \cdot \frac{d\theta_{res}}{dn_a} = \frac{1}{n_p} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_a}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a}\right)^{-1/2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_m^2}{(\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a)^2} \cdot 2n_a$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using the resonance condition itself:**
Recognize that the square-root factor equals $\sin\theta_{res}$, so:
$$\frac{d\theta_{res}}{dn_a} = \frac{n_a}{n_p \cos\theta_{res}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_m}{(\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a)} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a}$$

Wait — let us re-derive carefully:
$$\frac{d\theta_{res}}{dn_a} = \frac{1}{n_p \cos\theta_{res}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin\theta_{res}}{(\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_a/(\varepsilon_m+\varepsilon_a))} \cdot \frac{2n_a \varepsilon_m^2}{(\varepsilon_m+\varepsilon_a)^2}$$

This simplifies to:
$$\frac{d\theta_{res}}{dn_a} = \frac{n_a \varepsilon_m}{n_p \cos\theta_{res} (\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a)^2} \cdot \frac{(\varepsilon_m+\varepsilon_a)}{\sin\theta_{res}}$$

Using $\sin\theta_{res} = \frac{1}{n_p}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m\varepsilon_a}{\varepsilon_m+\varepsilon_a}}$:
$$\boxed{S = \frac{d\theta_{res}}{dn_a} = \frac{n_a}{\cos\theta_{res}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_m+\varepsilon_a} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_m+\varepsilon_a}{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_a}} \cdot \frac{1}{n_p}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation for gold–water at $\lambda = 633$ nm:**
For gold at 633 nm, $\varepsilon_m \approx -11.8 + i1.27$; for water, $\varepsilon_a = 1.33^2 \approx 1.77$.
- $\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a \approx -10.03$
- $\sqrt{\varepsilon_m \varepsilon_a / (\varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_a)} = \sqrt{(-11.8)(1.77)/(-10.03)} = \sqrt{2.083} \approx 1.443$
- $\sin\theta_{res} = 1.443 / n_p$; for SF11 glass ($n_p = 1.78$), $\sin\theta_{res} = 0.811$, so $\theta_{res} \approx 54.2°$, $\cos\theta_{res} \approx 0.585$
- Substituting: $S \approx 1.33 \times (-11.8) \times 1.443 / [0.585 \times (-10.03) \times 1.78]$
- $S \approx -21.74 / -10.45 \approx 2.08 \times 10^2$ rad/RIU
- Converting: $S \approx 11900°$ / RIU

This is the theoretical **angular sensitivity** for a gold-film Kretschmann SPR sensor with an SF11 prism at 633 nm. A change of $\Delta n_a = 10^{-5}$ RIU (a small protein adsorption event) yields $\Delta\theta \approx 0.119°$ — easily detectable with modern goniometers or CCD arrays.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Between SPR and EWF

| Parameter | SPR | Evanescent Wave Fluorescence (TIRF) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical principle | Resonant electron oscillation at metal interface | Exponentially decaying evanescent field excitation |
| Signal generation | Refractive-index shift | Fluorescence photon emission |
| Label required? | **No** (label-free) | **Yes** (fluorophore-tagged analyte) |
| Penetration depth | 100–300 nm | 100–200 nm |
| Selectivity basis | Mass-density change at surface | Optical sectioning of surface |
| Detection limit | $10^{-6}$ RIU (≈ pg/mm²) | pM to nM (single molecule) |
| Multiplexing | SPR Imaging (SPRI) possible | Spectrally and spatially multiplexable |
| Real-time kinetics | Excellent (ms time resolution) | Good (ms with EMCCD) |
| Commercial example | Biacore X100, Nicoya OpenSPR | Zeiss CellObserver TIRF, Nikon H-TIRF |
| Cost (instrument) | Moderate–High | Moderate–High |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Kretschmann SPR Biosensor — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This Mermaid diagram depicts the optical train of a typical prism-based Kretschmann SPR instrument (angular interrogation mode). Light from a monochromatic source is conditioned, coupled through the prism to the metal-film sensor chip, and the reflected beam is analyzed to extract the resonance angle shift caused by biomolecular binding.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LightSrc[Polychromatic or Monochromatic Light Source 633nm LED or Laser] --> Collimator[Collimating Lens Optical Chopper]
    Collimator --> Polarizer[TM Polarizer p-polarization only]
    Polarizer --> Prism[SF11 Glass Prism High Refractive Index 1.78]
    Prism --> GoldFilm[Thin Gold Film 50nm Thickness Kretschmann Configuration]
    GoldFilm --> Biorec[Bioreceptor Layer Antibody Aptamer or DNA Probe]
    Biorec --> Sample[Sample Flow Cell Analyte Solution]
    Sample --> Binding[Biomolecular Binding Event Mass Loading Delta n]
    Binding --> RefShift[Refractive Index Shift Delta n_a 10^-6 RIU]
    RefShift --> ReflectBeam[Reflected Light Beam Angular Shift]
    ReflectBeam --> Detector[Photodiode Array or CCD Detector]
    Detector --> Readout[Reflectivity vs Angle Plot Resonance Dip]
    Readout --> Software[KTU Real-Time Kinetics Software k_a k_d K_D]
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
- The light source produces a collimated, p-polarized beam.
- The prism couples light into the gold film via attenuated total reflection.
- The gold film supports the surface plasmon polariton on its lower surface.
- Bioreceptors immobilized on the gold capture target analytes from the flow cell.
- Binding events shift the local refractive index, displacing the resonance angle.
- The detector records the reflectivity dip, and software extracts kinetic parameters.

---

### 4.2 Evanescent-Wave Fluorescence (TIRF) Biosensor — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Laser[Laser Source 405 488 561 640 nm] --> AOTF[Acousto Optic Tunable Filter Wavelength Selection]
    AOTF --> CleanUp[Laser Cleanup Pinhole and Bandpass Filter]
    CleanUp --> TIRFObj[High NA Objective NA greater than 1.45 Oil Immersion]
    TIRFObj --> Coverslip[Glass Coverslip n equals 1.52]
    Coverslip --> Interface[Glass Water Interface Critical Angle]
    Interface --> Evanescent[Evanescent Field Penetration Depth 100 to 200 nm]
    Evanescent --> Fluor[Fluorophore Labeled Analyte Surface Bound Only]
    Fluor --> Emission[Stokes Shifted Fluorescence Emission 500 to 700 nm]
    Emission --> SameObj[Same Objective Collects Emission]
    SameObj --> Dichroic[Dichroic Mirror Separates Emission from Excitation]
    Dichroic --> EmFilter[Emission Bandpass Filter]
    EmFilter --> TubeLens[Tube Lens Images onto Camera]
    TubeLens --> EMCCD[EMCCD or sCMOS Camera Single Photon Sensitivity]
    EMCCD --> Process[Image Processing Localization and Tracking]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Laser light enters through the high-NA objective and undergoes total internal reflection at the glass–water interface.
- The evanescent field selectively excites fluorophores within ~150 nm of the surface.
- Stoke-shifted fluorescence is collected by the same objective, separated by the dichroic, and imaged onto an EMCCD.
- Image processing yields single-molecule localization with ~20 nm precision.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Choose SPR vs. EWF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[You Need an Optical Biosensor for Biomolecular Detection] --> Q1{Is Labeling Acceptable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Label is OK| Q2{What is Your Target Analyte Concentration?}
    Q1 -->|No Label Free Required| SPRPath[SPR or LSPR Sensor]
    SPRPath --> SPRUse[Drug Discovery Kinetics Affinity Measurements Binding Curves]
    Q2 -->|pM to nM Single Molecule| TIRFPath[TIRF Evanescent Wave Fluorescence]
    Q2 -->|Higher Concentrations uM or More| Labeled[Standard Fluorescence Microarray is Sufficient]
    TIRFPath --> TIRFUse[Membrane Receptor Dynamics Vesicle Fusion DNA Curtains]
    Labeled --> EndUse[Use Fluorophore Conjugated Secondary Antibodies]
    SPRUse --> EndUse
```

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU exam questions:**
> - Choose **SPR** when the problem says **"label-free"**, **"kinetics"**, **"refractive index change"**, or **"Biacore"**.
> - Choose **EWF/TIRF** when the problem says **"fluorophore"**, **"single molecule"**, **"membrane"**, or **"penetration depth"**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Define Surface Plasmon Resonance. With a neat sketch, explain the Kretschmann configuration of an SPR biosensor and state the role of the gold film.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) is the resonant, collective oscillation of conduction-band electrons at a metal–dielectric interface, excited by p-polarized light when its in-plane momentum matches the surface plasmon wavevector.

**Kretschmann configuration (1 mark):** In the Kretschmann setup, a thin metal film (~50 nm of gold) is deposited on the base of a high-refractive-index glass prism. Light is incident on the prism–metal interface at an angle greater than the critical angle. The evanescent field generated by attenuated total reflection penetrates the thin metal film and excites surface plasmons on the opposite metal–sample interface. The reflected intensity is monitored as a function of angle; a sharp reflectivity minimum occurs at the resonance angle $\theta_{res}$.

**Role of gold (1 mark):** The gold film serves two purposes: (i) it provides the negative-permittivity medium required to support surface plasmons at optical frequencies, and (ii) it acts as the substrate for immobilizing bioreceptors (antibodies, DNA probes). Its inertness, biocompatibility, and high plasma-frequency robustness make gold the metal of choice for SPR biosensors.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "evanescent wave" with reference to total internal reflection. Derive the expression for its penetration depth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
